Not ideal? The place hasn't had windows for a decade. Year 9s go there to do ket and summon demons. My brother lost his virginity there and got chlamydia AND scabies. "Felix what the fuck." "Will you come?" The thing is, I'm obviously going to go because this is insane and I need to see it. Also Keya's getting her nails done with Jordan's mum (weird relationship development but whatever) so I've got nothing else to do. The Uber driver nearly refuses to take us when Felix gives the address. "That's not a house, bruv." "It is," Felix insists. "It's condemned." "It's character property." The driver looks at me like 'is your boyfriend okay?' and I want to die but also thirty quid is thirty quid so I just shrug. The murder house is worse than I remembered. It's not even trying to be a house anymore. It's just... giving up. There's vegetation growing inside, actual trees coming through the windows. The front door's been replaced with a sheet of plywood that Felix moves aside like it weighs nothing. "You live here?" I ask. "Temporarily." "Felix, there's no roof." "There's some roof." "SOME roof?" Inside is mental. Proper mental. There's camping equipment in what might have been a living room - expensive stuff, like those posh tents from Glastonbury that cost more than my dad's car. But also there's paintings everywhere. Massive oil paintings in gold frames, just propped against the moldy walls. "Family portraits," Felix says when he sees me looking. I get closer to one. It's some Victorian woman in a massive dress looking miserable in front of a horse. But the face... "That looks exactly like you." "Family resemblance is strong." "No, Felix, that looks EXACTLY like you." "Don't be ridiculous." But I'm already taking photos because this is definitely going on the group chat. Except when I check my phone there's no signal. Of course there's no signal in the murder house. "How do you charge your phone?" I ask. "There's a generator." "Where?" He waves vaguely at nothing. I'm looking at more paintings now and they're all him. Different clothes, different time periods, but it's his face. His exact face. There's one of a soldier from like World War One that has his same weird perfect nose. "These are all you." "They're relatives." "Felix." "Strong genes." "FELIX." He's not looking at me, instead unpacking a Tesco bag full of black pudding onto a camping table. His weekend supply, I guess. The table's got other stuff on it - papers in languages I don't recognize, what looks like a proper old map of the Midlands where our town doesn't even exist yet, and a jewelry box full of teeth. "Why've you got teeth?" "They're not mine." "That's not better." "They're antique." "ANTIQUE TEETH?" He's getting stressed now, doing that thing where he looks like he might cry. "This was a mistake. I shouldn't have brought you here." "You think?" "I just wanted to hang out normally." "This isn't normal! Nothing about this is normal! You live in a condemned building with paintings of yourself from the Victorian era and a box of teeth!" "They're not all Victorian. Some are Edwardian." I can't. I actually can't. "I'm leaving." "Wait." He grabs my arm with his freezing hands. "Please. I know it's weird. I know I'm weird. But I just... I don't have anyone else to talk to." And the thing is, he looks so pathetic standing there in this rotting house with his black pudding and his paintings and his antique teeth that I actually feel bad for him. "Are you in a cult?" I ask. "What?" "Is this a cult thing? The paintings and the teeth and the murder house?" "It's not a murder house." "That's not a no to the cult question." He thinks about it. Actually properly considers it. "I don't think so." "You don't THINK so?" "Eugenie says it's more of a family tradition." "What tradition?" "Existing." Before I can ask what the fuck that means, there's a noise from upstairs. Footsteps. In a house with no proper stairs. "That'll be her," Felix says, and he looks terrified. "Who?" "Eugenie. She said she might drop by." "Drop by? Through the ceiling?" The footsteps get closer. On the ceiling now. Actually on the ceiling. "We should go," Felix says. "You THINK?" We proper leg it out of there. I don't look back but I swear I see something moving in the upstairs window that doesn't exist. The Uber back costs forty quid and Felix pays without blinking. I get home and immediately reverse image search the paintings. One's in the Leicester Museum. "Portrait of Unknown Young Woman, 1847." Another's in a book about local history. "The Montague Family, 1892." All of them have Felix's face. All of them. What the fuck have I gotten myself into?
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the established voice and plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nish's voice is remarkably consistent and well-realized. The chapter captures her specific brand of teenage narration - the rhetorical questions she asks herself, the way she repeats things for emphasis ("You live in the murder house?"), the casual cruelty about her brother losing his virginity and getting "chlamydia AND scabies," the way she notices irrelevant details (Felix's perfect nails, the tie inside out). The voice never slips into adult authorial intrusion. The aside about Lewis beating up his stepdad being "not relevant" while clearly being relevant to Nish's worldview is exactly right. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely funny and character-specific. The exchange about "some roof" works well. Felix's "They're not all Victorian. Some are Edwardian" is a perfect character beat - pedantic, deflecting, slightly absurd. The rhythm of the back-and-forth feels authentic to how teenagers actually talk. The Uber driver's brief appearance adds texture without overstaying its welcome. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows the plan closely - the condemned building, the paintings, the camping equipment, the teeth (a nice addition not in the plan), Eugenie's presence implied through footsteps, the reverse image search payoff. The teeth are a particularly good invention - "antique teeth" is funnier and weirder than anything in the plan. TONAL CONSISTENCY: The darkly comic tone is maintained throughout. The horror elements (footsteps on the ceiling, something in a window that doesn't exist) are genuinely unsettling but filtered through Nish's pragmatic, slightly annoyed perspective, which is exactly the right approach. The building "giving up" and "not even trying to be a house anymore" is a nice bit of personification that fits the voice rather than feeling like imposed literary flourish. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too competent - it hits every beat cleanly without much surprise beyond the plan. The "antique teeth" is the main invention beyond the plan. The emotional beat where Felix says he doesn't have anyone else to talk to and Nish feels bad is handled well but is the most conventional moment in the chapter. The ending with the reverse image search is effective but telegraphed from the moment Nish starts photographing the paintings. The house description ("giving up," "vegetation growing inside") is vivid without being purple. There's no forced poetry - the metaphors are grounded in Nish's voice. The piece avoids tell-don't-show largely by dramatizing everything through dialogue and action rather than summary. CONSISTENCY WITH PROFILES: Felix's voice samples ("They're not mine" / "That's not better" / "They're antique") are perfectly consistent with his profile - the apologetic pedantry, the catastrophic loneliness, the Victorian politeness. Nish's protective instinct toward him emerging despite herself is consistent with her character. FOLLOWED PLAN: Very closely. The plan mentioned "Portrait of Unknown Young Woman, 1847" and the local history book - both appear. The Uber driver's skepticism, the camping equipment, the paintings, Eugenie's implied presence - all executed. The teeth are a good addition. Overall this is a confident, well-executed chapter that delivers on its promises without being flashy about it. The voice is the real achievement here. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Madison's Party Was Carnage Everyone knows about Madison's party where the police came but nobody tells it right so let me set the record straight. First off, Madison's mum was already hammered when we arrived. Proper wine-mum wasted, wearing a dress from ASOS that was clearly meant for Madison, trying to add everyone on Instagram. She kept calling Felix "Edward" and touching his hair which is definitely noncey considering we're literally sixteen and she's like forty-something pretending to be thirty. "You're so pale," she kept saying. "Do you not go outside?" Felix just stood there frozen like when you see a teacher in Tesco and don't know how to act. The house was already rammed. Year 11s everywhere, couple of Year 10s who definitely weren't invited, and someone's older brother who left school three years ago but still comes to these things which is tragic. Everyone's drinking whatever they could nick from their parents - so basically Glenn's vodka and wine that tastes like vinegar. Keya's already fucked and it's only nine o'clock. She's crying in the bathroom about Jordan but also eating a share bag of Doritos so she's fine. I check on her and she goes "I think I'm pregnant" and I'm like "yeah you told me Tuesday" and she's like "did I?" This is why you shouldn't drink when you're pregnant but I'm not her mum so. Felix hasn't drunk anything because he "doesn't drink" which is the most suspicious thing he's said yet. What seventeen-year-old doesn't drink? Even the Muslim kids drink, they just don't tell their parents. "You're making everyone nervous," I tell him. "Why?" "Because you're sober and staring at people." "I'm not staring." "You're literally staring at Tom's neck right now." "He has an interesting jugular." "That's the weirdest thing you've ever said and you once asked me if blood comes in different flavors." He stops staring at Tom's neck and stares at mine instead which is worse. Madison appears with her usual drama, announcing that someone's definitely pregnant because she found a test in the bins. Everyone looks at Yasmin who goes bright red but I know it's Keya's because she did three tests in Madison's bathroom and didn't even wrap them in tissue, just lobbed them straight in the bin because she has no sense of self-preservation. "It's not mine," Yasmin says, and everyone believes her because she's never even held hands with anyone. "Whose is it then?" Madison's loving this. She lives for drama. Last year she started a rumor about herself just to see what would happen. Nobody admits anything so Madison gets bored and puts on music so loud the neighbors definitely hate us. By ten, it's already messy. Someone's smashed a vase and hidden the pieces under the sofa. Lewis's cousin is selling ket in the garden even though Lewis isn't here because of the stepdad situation. A Year 10 is crying because another Year 10got with her ex. Standard party stuff. Felix and I end up in the garden because he says he needs air even though we're literally outside. Jordan Fletcher's already tactical chundering in the koi pond which is disgusting but also impressive forward planning. "I can't do this," Felix says. "Can't do what?" "This." He gestures at everything. "All these people. The smells. The sounds. The necks." "Stop saying necks." "Sorry." "Do you want to leave?" "I can't. I promised Madison I'd stay til eleven." "Why?" "She said I was mysterious and leaving early would be 'on brand.'" I'm about to tell him that Madison's full of shit when someone screams. Proper horror movie screams. Everyone rushes to look and there's a whole-ass woman in the tree. Not a girl, a proper adult woman, just sitting in Madison's mum's oak tree wearing what looks like a ballgown from the Victorian era but also might be from Zara's new collection. She's just sitting there, watching the party. "What the fuck?" someone says. "Is that your mum?" someone asks Tom. "Why would my mum be in a tree?" "Performance art?" suggests Madison, because she did drama GCSE and thinks everything's performance art. Felix goes white. Whiter. Translucent. "That's Eugenie," he says, but only I hear him. The woman in the tree - Eugenie - waves. Not a normal wave. A little finger wiggle like she's at the opera or something. "I'm calling the police," Madison's mum slurs from the patio door. "Don't!" Felix actually shouts, which makes everyone look at him. "She's... she's my cousin." "Your cousin's in a tree?" Madison asks. "She's eccentric." "Eccentric?" "She went to Oxford." Everyone nods like this explains everything because posh people are mental and we all know it. But Eugenie's already gone. Not climbed down gone. Just gone. The tree's empty and everyone's arguing about whether we imagined it and Jordan's going "there was a fit woman in the tree" even though he's still being sick. The police arrive about five minutes later because of the noise complaint, not the tree woman. Madison's mum tries to flirt with them which goes about as well as you'd expect. They make everyone go home and take Jordan's brother's details because he's twenty and shouldn't be at a Year 11 party. Felix proper legs it when he sees the police cars. Actually runs away, leaving me to walk home alone which is shit friend behavior even if we're not actually friends. I get about halfway home when I see her. Eugenie. Standing outside Tesco Express like she's waiting for an Uber, except she's definitely not because she's holding what looks like a rabbit but might be a cat. It's hard to tell because it's not moving. She sees me and smiles. She's got Felix's face but older, sharper, like someone took his features and made them mean. "You're the one helping Felix with his situation," she says. Not a question. "What situation?" "His dietary requirements." Why does everyone keep saying that? "I sell him black pudding." "Yes." She says it like it means something else. "Make sure he feeds properly." "On black pudding?" "On whatever keeps him stable." She hands me twenty quid. Just straight up gives me money like I'm a dealer or something. "I don't-" "For your discretion." Then she's gone. And I mean gone. I look down at the twenty quid for literally a second and when I look up she's vanished. The Tesco security cameras probably caught it but who's gonna check? I text Felix: "ur cousin is mental" He texts back immediately: "I'm so sorry" "she gave me £20" "Keep it" "why" "Compensation" "for what" He doesn't reply. Sunday I text him twelve times but he blanks me. Keya texts saying Jordan found out about the baby and he's actually happy which is mental but also kind of sweet I guess. Monday morning Felix isn't at the bins. His phone's off. Tuesday I hear from Madison that he's off sick. Wednesday his name's not on the register anymore. What the fuck.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the established voice and plan. The model has successfully captured Nish's unreliable, self-interrupting narration with genuine skill. Let me assess it across the various dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nish's voice is remarkably consistent and convincing. The parenthetical asides ("This is why you shouldn't drink when you're pregnant but I'm not her mum so"), the casual cruelty ("Even the Muslim kids drink, they just don't tell their parents"), and the deflating self-awareness all ring true. She's judgmental, funny in a mean way, and genuinely observant. Felix's dialogue is pitch-perfect - "He has an interesting jugular" is exactly the kind of line that's both funny and genuinely unsettling. Eugenie's brief appearance delivers on her character profile: the finger wiggle "like she's at the opera," the clipped menace, the inexplicable twenty quid. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. The exchange about staring at necks is economical and funny. "She went to Oxford" / "Everyone nods like this explains everything" is a sharp social observation. The Felix/Nish texting exchange at the end is perfectly calibrated - his one-word responses building dread without melodrama. PLOT EXECUTION: The chapter follows its plan closely - Madison's mum being drunk and noncey, Felix not drinking, the koi pond vomiting, Eugenie in the tree, the police, Eugenie outside Tesco, the twenty quid, Felix disappearing. The plan is executed faithfully and with good pacing. The chapter ends on a genuinely unsettling note (Felix vanishing from the register) that earns its darkness without overselling it. SHOW VS TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. Madison's character is established through action ("Last year she started a rumor about herself just to see what would happen"). The party chaos is rendered through specific, grounded details rather than summary. However, there are moments where Nish editorializes a bit heavily - "Standard party stuff" is a slight cheat, though it's in-voice. CREATIVITY: The details are specific and fresh - the pregnancy test not wrapped in tissue, Jordan's "tactical chundering" as "impressive forward planning," the Zara/Victorian ballgown ambiguity. These feel genuinely observed rather than generic. The "Oxford" explanation for tree-sitting is a lovely comic beat. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly too competent - it executes the plan so faithfully that it occasionally feels like a checklist being ticked. The Keya pregnancy subplot is handled a bit perfunctorily. The ending (Felix disappearing from the register) is effective but the transition from party to aftermath is slightly rushed. The "Eugenie's already gone. Not climbed down gone. Just gone" construction is used twice in close proximity (once for the tree, once for outside Tesco), which is a minor repetition issue. PURPLE PROSE/FORCED METAPHOR: Essentially none. The prose is deliberately flat and colloquial, which is appropriate and well-executed. No unearned poeticism. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: Very few. The writing is confident and controlled. The unreliable narrator device is handled with genuine sophistication - Nish's contradictions and digressions feel organic rather than mechanical. OVERALL: This is a genuinely accomplished piece of YA darkly comic fiction. It's funny, unsettling, and character-consistent throughout. The vampire ambiguity is maintained perfectly. The chapter earns its moments of genuine weirdness by grounding them in specific, believable social detail. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: Shit Gets Weirder Felix texts me sixty times Sunday. I'm not exaggerating. Sixty. I counted. All variations of "I'm sorry" and "please let me explain" and "Eugenie isn't usually like that" and "are you okay" and one that just says "trees are not her normal approach." I ignore all of them because I've got Biology revision and my dad's doing my head in about working in the shop Monday morning before school. "You never help anymore," he goes, like I don't work every Sunday and two evenings a week for literally no money except what I nick from the till. "I've got GCSEs," I tell him. "Your brother managed GCSEs and working." "Yeah and he got all 4s didn't he?" My mum clips me round the head for that but gentle-like because she knows I'm right. Dip's thick as shit and working at Halfords now so not exactly winning at life. Monday morning I'm trying to restock the crisps (we've started doing those posh Tyrrell's ones because gentrification's coming even to our shit area) when Felix appears at the shop door looking like he's been sleeping in a bin. "We're not open," I say through the glass. "Please." "Opens at seven." "Nish, please." My dad appears behind me. "Is this your boyfriend?" "No." "He looks unwell." "He always looks like that." "Does he want black pudding?" I'm going to kill my dad. Actually kill him. Felix's face does something complicated. "We're out," I lie. "No we're not," Dad says, because he's a traitor. "We've got loads. Too much. You want some, son?" Felix nods like a drowning man being offered a life jacket. Dad lets him in because my dad has no survival instincts and would definitely die first in a horror film. Felix buys eight packages of black pudding and demolishes one right there at the counter like it's a Kit Kat. "You weren't at school," I say. "I was dealing with things." "What things?" "Family things." Dad's watching this like it's EastEnders. "You two have a falling out?" "No," I say. "Yes," Felix says. "Which is it?" "His cousin's mental," I tell Dad. "Whose isn't?" Dad says, which fair point. Our cousin Raj thinks he's going to be on Love Island even though he looks like a potato with anxiety. Felix follows me to school which I can't stop him doing because it's a free country innit. He properly corners me behind the languages block where Year 9s go to vape and there's that weird smell that might be cat piss or might be Leon's lynx Africa. "Did Eugenie talk to you?" he asks, urgent. "Yeah, she's mental. Runs in the family clearly." "What did she say exactly?" "Told me to make sure you eat." His whole body relaxes. "That's all?" "Yeah. No wait, she said make sure you feed properly." "Did she say what specifically?" "No, but I assumed black pudding because you're obsessed." He looks so relieved I think he might cry. "She didn't mention the original arrangement?" "What arrangement?" "Nothing. It doesn't matter." "Felix, what arrangement?" But then the fire alarm goes off because of course it does. It's always the fire alarm. If there was an actual fire we'd all die because nobody takes it seriously anymore. We trudge out to the tennis courts for registration and Mr. Henderson's doing a head count but everyone knows it's just Year 10s vaping in the disabled toilets again. They never learn. "What arrangement?" I ask again but Felix pretends he can't hear me over everyone complaining about the cold. He avoids me the rest of the day. Literally turns around when he sees me coming. Sits on the other side of the classroom. At lunch he's nowhere, probably eating black pudding in a cupboard somewhere like the freak he is. Keya finds me after school. "You and Felix had a domestic?" "We're not together." "Madison says you are." "Madison thinks she saw Harry Styles in Primark last week." "Maybe she did." "In our Primark? In the Midlands? Harry Styles?" "Could happen." This is why Keya's pregnant. No critical thinking skills. That night I'm in bed scrolling TikTok when I get a text. Three blood drops. 🩸🩸🩸 Same as before but it's a different number. Then: "He's getting hungry." I text back: "who is this" "You know who this is." "eugenie?" "Feed him." "he feeds himself like a big boy" "Not properly." "not my problem" "It will be." Then she sends a photo. It's me at the shop this morning, taken from outside. You can see Felix at the counter and me looking pissed off and my dad in his embarrassing Leicester City pajama bottoms he wears under his trousers when it's cold. "are you stalking me???" "Feed him properly or I will." "that a threat?" "It's a promise." I block the number immediately. Fuck this. Fuck Felix and his mental cousin and their weird black pudding cult or whatever this is. But I screenshot everything first because I'm not stupid. Tuesday Felix is back at the bins before school. "I need more," he says. "Your cousin's threatening me." "What?" I show him the texts. His face does that thing where he looks like he might throw up. "Oh no." "Oh no what?" "She's escalating." "Escalating what?" "The situation." "WHAT SITUATION?" "I can't tell you." "Why?" "Because you'll run." "I'm already running. This is me running. Your cousin's in trees and taking creepy photos and sending me threatening texts about feeding you." "I know." "So tell me what's happening." "I can't." "Felix." "I literally can't. She'll know. She always knows." The thing is, he looks properly scared. Not worried or anxious, but scared like when my brother saw our dad cry that one time. "Is she dangerous?" I ask. "Yes." "To me?" "To everyone." "Felix, what the fuck?" "Just keep bringing me black pudding. Please. I'll pay double. Triple. Whatever. Just until I figure this out." "Figure what out?" But he's already walking away, clutching his Tesco bag of black pudding like it's keeping him alive. Wednesday I get another text: "He's getting hungrier." Thursday: "Soon he won't be able to control it." Friday morning: "Last chance." I don't tell Felix about these texts. I don't know why. Maybe because he already looks like he's dying and I don't want to be responsible for pushing him over the edge. Or maybe because I'm starting to think Eugenie's right. Whatever's happening with Felix, it's getting worse. And I'm right in the middle of it.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the established voice and plan, though it deviates somewhat from the chapter plan (which was labeled Chapter 7 in the plan but written as Chapter 6). The plan called for "Felix texts me sixty times Sunday" as Chapter 7 content, and that's exactly what this chapter opens with, suggesting a numbering discrepancy. The chapter also incorporates elements from the Chapter 6 plan (Eugenie texting, the threatening messages) and Chapter 7 (Felix cornering Nish, the fire alarm). This is a minor structural issue but worth noting. Voice: The Nish voice is consistently excellent throughout. "My dad has no survival instincts and would definitely die first in a horror film" is exactly the kind of observation the character profile calls for - casually mean, self-aware, funny in a way that feels earned rather than performed. The Keya exchange about Harry Styles in Primark is genuinely sharp and the "This is why Keya's pregnant. No critical thinking skills" payoff is darkly comic in exactly the right register. Dialogue: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange at the shop with Dad ("Does he want black pudding?" / "We're out" / "No we're not") is naturalistic and funny without being labored. The text exchange with Eugenie has good rhythm - the escalating formality against Nish's lowercase dismissiveness creates real tension. Felix's "trees are not her normal approach" as a text message is a genuinely funny detail that also does character work. Character consistency: All characters behave exactly as profiled. Felix's evasiveness, his relief when Eugenie "only" mentioned feeding, his terror - all consistent. Nish's dad being a horror-movie victim type, Keya's lack of critical thinking, Eugenie's threatening politeness - all land. The detail about Dip getting 4s and working at Halfords is the kind of specific, mean, real detail that elevates the writing. Plot: The chapter successfully advances the mystery without resolving anything, which is the stated intention. The escalating texts from Eugenie create genuine tension. The "original arrangement" tease is effective. The fire alarm interruption is a bit convenient but fits the chaotic, interrupted-conversation style of the piece. Show vs. Tell: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells, though there are moments where Nish's narration explains things that could be dramatized. "He looks properly scared. Not worried or anxious, but scared like when my brother saw our dad cry that one time" - this is actually good showing through comparison, a nice technique. The ending ("Whatever's happening with Felix, it's getting worse. And I'm right in the middle of it.") is the weakest moment - it's a bit of a generic thriller ending that doesn't fit the chaotic, self-undermining voice established elsewhere. It's too neat, too "chapter ending," and breaks the conversational register. Darkness/Lightness: The balance is well-maintained. The comedy (Dad in Leicester City pajamas, Raj looking like a potato with anxiety) sits naturally alongside the genuine menace of Eugenie's surveillance photo. The chapter earns its darker moments because the comedy is grounded. Weaknesses: The ending is the main flaw - it's too tidy and thriller-ish for a narrator who's supposed to be unreliable and meandering. The chapter plan promised more chaos and less resolution, and the final three paragraphs feel like they belong to a different, more conventional YA thriller. Also, the chapter is slightly longer than it needs to be - the Thursday/Friday text montage at the end feels rushed compared to the richly dramatized earlier scenes. Overall, this is confident, funny, well-characterized writing that successfully executes a difficult voice. The flaws are real but minor. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: Shit Gets Weirder So that was three weeks ago and honestly I don't know what happened. Felix disappeared. Like properly disappeared, not just off school for a sick day disappeared. His name's not on any register, his phone's dead, even his shit's gone from the murder house (I checked, don't judge me). Madison's parents are getting divorced because of the party but really because her mum's been shagging the builder since January. The party was just the excuse her dad needed. Madison's pretending she's traumatized but she's already planning her two birthdays two Christmases angle. Keya's pregnant. Not Yasmin, which everyone assumed because Yasmin gained weight. Turns out Yasmin just discovered Uber Eats. Keya told everyone at lunch Tuesday, casual as anything, eating chips like "yeah so I'm keeping it." Jordan actually cried. Not cute crying either, proper ugly crying into his Subway sandwich. They're looking at flats now which is mental because neither of them can even drive. But that's all normal shit compared to what's happening to me. I'm still getting the texts. Different number every time so blocking's pointless. Started simple - just blood drops. Then words: "He's getting hungrier" "Feed him" "You promised" "He needs you" "Time's running out" I never promised shit. I sold black pudding to a weird posh boy for profit. That's not a blood oath or whatever this is. The paintings disappeared. I mean they're gone from the internet. I had screenshots on my phone - that Victorian woman with Felix's face, the soldier, all of them. Went to show Keya and they're corrupted files. The museum website's got nothing. Even that local history book I found on Google Books - the pages are missing. Just gone. "File not found." How does that even happen? Thursday the murder house burned down. Nobody reported it. I only know because I was on the bus to Tesco and saw the smoke. By the time I got there (yes I got off the bus to look, shut up) it was just black bones of building and no fire brigade, no police, nothing. Like it burned itself quietly without telling anyone. There's FOR SALE signs up already. "Development opportunity." My brother says they're building flats but my brother also thinks he's going to win the lottery so. I see Eugenie sometimes. Always in Tesco Express, always at the reduced section. Last week she bought twelve packages of black pudding, all the ones with yellow stickers. Winked at me as she left. The security guard didn't even look at her. Nobody ever looks at her except me. "That your mate's cousin?" the guy on the till asked. "How'd you know?" "Looks just like him innit. But fit." Which is gross because she's old (like thirty) but also true. She does look exactly like Felix if Felix wasn't malnourished and actually knew how to do his hair. My dad thinks I'm on drugs now because black pudding keeps going missing and I can't explain it. I'm not even taking it anymore - it's just disappearing. We had twenty packages Monday morning, by Wednesday there's three. "Are you selling them?" Dad asks. "To who? The black pudding dealers?" "Don't be smart." "Don't be stupid." Mum clips me for that but she's worried too. Started checking my arms for track marks like I'm in a Channel 4 documentary about youth gone wrong. The thing that nobody knows - that I'm not telling anyone - is I got my GCSE results early because of some admin fuck up. Straight 9s. Even in French and I don't speak French. The email said "exceptional circumstances" but wouldn't say what circumstances. Birmingham offered me a place for Medicine starting September. Full ride. Scholarship and everything. They never give scholarships to people like me. People from here. But there it is in writing - "exceptional candidate" and "unique potential" and "delighted to offer." I should be buzzing. I should be telling everyone. This is my ticket out, everything I wanted. But I'm not going. Because last night I found something under my pillow. A tooth. Old, like propery old. Wrapped in paper that had one word: "Soon." I know it's from Felix. Don't ask me how, I just know. The tooth is exactly like the ones in his jewelry box, and it smells like him - that weird rust and expensive cologne smell. He's coming back. Or he never left. Or something. Keya thinks I'm having a breakdown. "You've been proper weird since that posh boy left." "I'm fine." "You're not eating." "I'm eating." "You're not. And you keep looking at people's necks." Fuck. I am. I'm doing what Felix did, staring at jugulars like they're interesting. Tom's got a particularly good one. Very visible. Very accessible. "It's stress," I tell her. "What stress? You already got into Birmingham." "How do you know that?" "Your mum told my mum told everyone." Of course she did. The texts are getting more frequent now. Sometimes just coordinates. I looked them up once - it's the bypass, right where the murder house was. Sometimes it's times and dates. Tomorrow, midnight. Sunday, 3am. I never go but they keep coming. Today's was different. "He's so hungry he's forgotten how to be human." Then a photo. It's dark and blurry but there's something in it. A shape in a tree. Could be anything. Could be a big cat. Could be Felix. Could be nothing. I'm going mad. That's what this is. Stress-induced psychosis from GCSEs and my parents and Keya's pregnancy drama. There's no vampires, no Eugenie, no Felix in trees. Just a weird boy who moved away and my brain filling in gaps with horror film shit. But I keep the tooth under my pillow. Just in case.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the established voice and concept. Let me work through it carefully. **Voice and Character Consistency** Nish's voice is remarkably consistent throughout. The chapter opens with "So that was three weeks ago" which immediately signals the confessional, direct-address mode established in the plan. Her characteristic deflections are present: she catalogues other people's drama (Madison's parents, Keya's pregnancy) before getting to the genuinely strange stuff happening to her. The aside about Yasmin and Uber Eats is exactly the kind of casually mean, specific detail that makes Nish feel real rather than constructed. Her self-correction ("I checked, don't judge me") and her dismissal of her brother's lottery ambitions are consistent with the profile's description of someone who notices everything and is casually judgmental. The moment where she catches herself staring at Tom's jugular is genuinely effective - it's the chapter's best beat, where the horror bleeds into Nish's psychology without being announced. "Very visible. Very accessible." is chilling precisely because Nish doesn't seem to register how chilling it is. **Plot and Structure** The chapter largely follows the plan but makes some significant structural changes. The plan had this as Chapter 7 ("Shit Gets Weirder") covering Felix texting sixty times, the fire alarm interruption, and a second blood text from a different number. The actual chapter is more of a synthesis of Chapters 7 and 8 from the plan - it covers Felix's disappearance, the ongoing texts, the burned house, Eugenie in Tesco, and the Birmingham offer. This is a notable deviation from the plan, though the material is handled well. The Birmingham scholarship/Medicine offer is a smart addition not in the plan - it gives Nish a concrete reason to stay that she's choosing to ignore, which deepens her character. The tooth under the pillow is a good horror beat. **What Works** The mundane-horror juxtaposition is consistently effective. Eugenie buying reduced black pudding at Tesco Express while being an ancient vampire is exactly the right register. The corrupted files and the house burning down "quietly without telling anyone" are genuinely unsettling details delivered in Nish's matter-of-fact voice. The texts escalating from emoji to coordinates to "He's so hungry he's forgotten how to be human" is well-paced. The Keya pregnancy reveal is handled with exactly the right tone - "casual as anything, eating chips like 'yeah so I'm keeping it'" - and Jordan crying into his Subway sandwich is a specific, funny, sad detail. **What Doesn't Work** The chapter is somewhat tell-heavy in places. The explanation of what's happening to Nish (the texts, the paintings disappearing, the house burning) is delivered as a list rather than dramatized. This is partly intentional given the confessional format, but it means the chapter lacks scenes - it's mostly summary. The Birmingham scholarship feels slightly convenient as a plot device and the "exceptional circumstances" explanation is vague in a way that feels like a placeholder rather than deliberate mystery. The dialogue is minimal and functional rather than distinctive. The exchange with her dad ("Are you selling them?" / "To who? The black pudding dealers?") is good, but there's not much of it. The ending with the tooth under the pillow is effective but the "just in case" callback is slightly too neat for a story that's supposed to resist resolution. **Amateurish Elements** The chapter is not amateurish in execution. The voice is controlled, the details are specific, and the tonal balance between comedy and dread is maintained. There are no purple prose moments - the writing is deliberately flat and colloquial, which serves the piece. No forced metaphors; the imagery that appears (the house as "black bones of building") is brief and earns its place. The main weakness is structural: this reads more like a penultimate chapter than a middle chapter, and it's doing the work of two planned chapters in one, which means some of the planned beats (Felix cornering her, the fire alarm, the conversation about "the original arrangement") are lost. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 9 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: I Still Don't Know So that was three weeks ago and I honestly don't know what happened. I'm at the shop right now, it's Thursday afternoon, meant to be revising for Biology retakes even though I got a 9 but Mum doesn't believe me. Dad's in the back arguing with the Walkers delivery guy about cheese and onion proportions. Standard Thursday really. Except I've got twelve packages of black pudding in my school bag. Not selling them. Not eating them. Just... having them. Like how some people carry lucky pennies or whatever. Mine just happen to be processed pig blood that cost me thirty quid I don't have. Keya comes in around four, massive now, waddling like those women on One Born Every Minute. Jordan's with her carrying her bags like a proper boyfriend which nobody saw coming. "You alright?" she asks. "Yeah why?" "You're staring at my neck." "No I'm not." "You literally are." She's right. I am. It's got this vein that's properly visible and I can see her pulse and it's weirdly calming? That's mental. I'm mental. "Sorry. Tired." "You're always tired now." "GCSEs innit." "They're over." "Retakes." "You got all 9s." "How does everyone know that?" "Your mum put it on Facebook." Kill me. Jordan's buying energy drinks even though Keya's not meant to have caffeine but I don't serve him because I see Eugenie outside. She's just standing there on the pavement, looking at our shop window like there's something interesting about our display of out-of-date Pot Noodles. "Back in a sec," I tell Keya. Outside, Eugenie smells expensive. Proper rich person smell, like shops you're scared to go in. "He's coming back," she says. "Who?" "Don't be obtuse." "Felix?" "He's very hungry." "Not my problem." "You still have feelings for him." "I never had feelings for him. He was just some weird posh boy who ate black pudding." She smiles. It's not a nice smile. "You carry black pudding in your bag." How does she know that? "Coincidence." "Twelve packages is not coincidence. It's preparation." "For what?" "For when he comes back wrong." "Wrong how?" But she's already walking away. Not vanishing this time, just walking like a normal person except nobody looks at her. Jordan comes out and walks straight through where she was standing, doesn't even notice. That night I can't sleep. Keep checking my phone for texts but nothing comes. The tooth under my pillow feels warm, which is impossible but there it is. 3am I hear something outside. Not a cat. Bigger. I don't look. Friday's when it happens. I'm in Biology (retaking even though I don't need to but school admin's fucked) when the new kid walks in. It's Felix but it's not Felix. Same face, same stupid posh boy hair, same walk like he's underwater. But his eyes are different. Hungrier. And he's too pale even for February Felix standards. "Class, this is Francis," Mrs. Patel says. "He's just transferred." Francis. Not Felix. Francis. He sits right next to me even though there's loads of empty seats. "Hi," he says, and his voice is Felix's voice but colder. "Hi." "You smell like black pudding." What the fuck do you say to that? "I work in a shop." "No," he says. "You smell like you're carrying it. Twelve packages. In your bag." I look at him properly. Same face. Exactly the same face. But Felix had this softness, this anxiety, this trying-too-hard-to-be-human thing. Francis doesn't have that. Francis looks like he knows exactly what he is. "Want some?" I ask, because what else do I do? "Not yet." "When?" "When you offer it properly." Mrs. Patel's droning about cell division but all I can focus on is not-Felix beside me, smelling like rust and old money and something else. Something wrong. At lunch he follows me. Not like Felix did, all awkward and apologetic. This is deliberate. Predatory. "You knew him," he says. Not a question. "Who?" "My cousin. Felix." "Cousin?" "Distant. Very distant." "Right." "He talked about you." "Did he?" "Said you saved him." "I sold him black pudding." "Same thing, in his case." We're by the bins now, the same bins where this all started. Francis is standing exactly where Felix stood that first morning, but he's not shaking. He's perfectly still. Too still. Nothing about him moves except his mouth. "Is he coming back?" I ask. "Do you want him to?" "I don't care." "Liar." "I'm not—" "You kept the tooth." "How do you—" "I can smell it on you. Old calcium and desperation." That's properly mental but also accurate? The tooth does smell desperate, if desperation has a smell. "What are you?" I ask. "Hungry." "That's not an answer." "It's the only answer that matters." He leans closer and his breath is cold, like opening a freezer. "He was weak. Couldn't do what was necessary. But I'm not weak." "What's necessary?" "You'll see." Then he's gone. Not walked away, not even vampire-vanished. Just gone between one blink and the next. I've still got the twelve black puddings in my bag. They're getting heavy. That night I get one text: "Sunday. Midnight. Where it started." The bins. That's where it started. I'm not going. Obviously I'm not going. I'm not stupid. You don't meet maybe-vampires at midnight by bins. That's how you end up on true crime podcasts. But I put the tooth in my pocket. And I charge my phone. And I set an alarm for 11:45. Just in case. Because here's the thing nobody tells you about maybe-vampires: even when they're gone, even when you know they're dangerous, even when every logical part of your brain is screaming run— You still want to know what happens next. And I've got twelve packages of black pudding and nothing to lose except my life, which in this shit town isn't worth much anyway. Keya texts: "you coming out tomorrow?" I text back: "maybe. depends." "on what?" Good question. Depends on whether Felix is Felix or Francis or something else. Depends on whether Eugenie's threats are real. Depends on whether I'm brave or stupid or both. Depends on whether vampires are real or I'm just having a very specific mental breakdown involving black pudding and posh boys with eating disorders. My dad knocks on my door. "You alright? You've been quiet." "I'm fine." "Your mum's worried." "Mum's always worried." "This is different worried." "I'm fine." "Alright. Don't stay up too late." It's 11:30 now. Fifteen minutes to decide. The tooth is warm in my pocket. The black pudding's in my bag. My phone's charged. I know what I'm going to do. Have known all along, probably. Since that first text, those three blood drops, I've been heading towards this moment. Whatever Felix is, whatever Francis is, whatever's waiting at the bins— Actually wait, someone's at the door. At midnight? Dad's going mental, shouting about the time. But I can hear the voice and it's
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the concluding chapter of this darkly comic British YA vampire story, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: maintain the voice, deepen the mystery without resolving it, and end mid-sentence in a way that feels earned rather than lazy. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Nish's voice is remarkably consistent throughout. The self-interruptions, the casual cruelty, the deflection through humor—"Kill me" after her mum posts her grades on Facebook—all feel authentic. The detail about staring at Keya's neck and finding it "weirdly calming" is a genuinely unsettling character beat that shows Nish being changed by her proximity to whatever Felix/Francis is, without the story ever confirming anything supernatural. The line "That's mental. I'm mental." is perfectly calibrated to her voice. The introduction of Francis as a darker, more predatory version of Felix is clever—it raises the stakes while maintaining ambiguity. Is Francis a different vampire? Felix having "come back wrong"? A delusion? The story wisely refuses to answer. Francis's dialogue is appropriately chilling: "Not yet" and "When you offer it properly" have genuine menace without tipping into camp. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows the plan closely—three weeks have passed, Felix has disappeared, Nish is still carrying black pudding, Eugenie appears again, and a new/different Felix shows up. The plan mentioned "Francis" as a development and the chapter delivers this effectively. The midnight cliffhanger ending mid-sentence is exactly what was planned and works well as a structural choice. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The Keya exchange about staring at her neck is economical and funny. Francis's lines are genuinely unsettling. The dad knocking on the door is touching in its mundanity. The weakness is that some of Francis's lines veer slightly toward generic vampire menace ("I'm not weak," "You'll see") but this is minor. SHOW VS. TELL: The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The twelve black puddings as a character detail is excellent showing. However, the final paragraphs before the cliffhanger become more tell-heavy: "I know what I'm going to do. Have known all along, probably." This is the chapter's weakest section—it over-explains Nish's emotional state in a way that undercuts the voice. CREATIVITY: The "tooth under my pillow" detail is intriguing and unexplained—where did it come from? This is either a brilliant unexplained detail or a continuity error from the plan (the plan doesn't mention a tooth). It reads as intentionally mysterious but could confuse readers who haven't seen the full story. The "Francis not Felix" twist is genuinely creative and unsettling. WEAKNESSES: The final pre-cliffhanger paragraphs are the chapter's low point. "Whatever Felix is, whatever Francis is, whatever's waiting at the bins—" followed by "Actually wait, someone's at the door" is a bit too cute. The philosophical musing about wanting to know what happens next ("You still want to know what happens next") is the most generic moment in the chapter—it's the kind of thing any YA novel might say, and it doesn't sound like Nish. The line "which in this shit town isn't worth much anyway" is similarly a cliché. The tooth detail appears without prior establishment in this chapter, suggesting it was meant to be seeded earlier. As a standalone chapter, it's slightly confusing. TONAL CONSISTENCY: The balance of comedy and dread is well-maintained. Jordan carrying Keya's bags "like a proper boyfriend which nobody saw coming" is a perfect throwaway line. The mundane (dad arguing about cheese and onion proportions) against the supernatural (Eugenie appearing outside) is handled with skill. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter follows the plan closely—three weeks later, Nish still has black pudding, Eugenie appears, Felix disappears, and the story ends mid-sentence. The plan mentioned "I think Felix might come back" and the chapter delivers this with Francis. The plan's ending "tell him I've got black pudding in my bag. Just in case he's actually" is echoed in the chapter's structure if not its exact words. Overall, this is a strong concluding chapter that maintains voice, delivers on the story's promises of non-resolution, and ends effectively. Its weaknesses are in the over-explained emotional beats near the end and one or two generic lines that don't sound like Nish. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a darkly comic British YA piece told in first-person by Nish, a working-class girl from the Midlands who gets drawn into a mystery involving Felix, a posh new boy with apparent vampiric tendencies. The prompt asked for darkly comic British YA fiction, and the piece largely delivers on that brief with considerable skill. VOICE AND CHARACTER: The narrator's voice is the piece's greatest strength. Nish is genuinely funny, sharp, and specific - her observations about school social dynamics, her dad's Leicester City pajamas, Keya's pregnancy, Jordan's tactical chundering in the koi pond - these feel authentically rendered. The voice is consistent throughout all eight chapters, which is a real achievement. Felix is also well-drawn as a character: his awkwardness, his posh-boy fish-out-of-water quality, his genuine desperation all feel earned. The dynamic between them has real chemistry even without being romantic. DIALOGUE: The texting exchanges are particularly strong - the rhythm of Felix's formal language against Nish's casual texting style creates genuine comedy. The in-person dialogue is also mostly effective, though it occasionally becomes a vehicle for exposition ("What arrangement?" "Nothing." "Felix." "I can't tell you." etc.) which is a weakness. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The mystery unfolds at a reasonable pace through the first five chapters, but then the structure becomes somewhat repetitive. Chapters 6 and 7 both begin with "So that was three weeks ago" which is either a deliberate structural joke or an error - it reads as an error. The plot stalls in the middle chapters with repeated variations of "Felix is hungry / Eugenie is threatening / I don't know what's happening." The introduction of Francis in Chapter 8 is interesting but feels rushed and underdeveloped. The ending is deliberately unresolved (mid-sentence cliffhanger), which is a legitimate choice for YA but feels slightly cheap here. WORLD BUILDING: The world is built through accumulation of specific detail rather than exposition - the murder house, the paintings, the antique teeth, the black pudding obsession. This is skillfully done. The vampire mythology is deliberately kept vague, which works for the genre and the narrator's perspective. COMEDY: The dark comedy lands consistently. The observations about school life, class dynamics, and teenage behavior are sharp and specific. The running gag about black pudding as vampire sustenance is genuinely clever - it's both funny and thematically coherent (iron content, blood sausage, etc.). The Keya pregnancy subplot adds texture without overwhelming the main story. WEAKNESSES: The piece has some structural problems. The repetition in later chapters (multiple "three weeks ago" time jumps, repeated "what arrangement?" exchanges) suggests the author ran out of plot momentum. The Francis introduction in Chapter 8 is intriguing but feels like a new story beginning rather than a satisfying development of the existing one. The piece never quite commits to revealing what Felix is, which is fine, but the hints become repetitive rather than building tension. Some of the "weird things happening" accumulate without escalating - the burning house, the disappearing paintings, the coordinates - these feel like they should add up to something but don't quite cohere. The ending mid-sentence is a bit of a cop-out. TONE: The balance between comedy and genuine unease is well-maintained for most of the piece. The darkness never overwhelms the comedy, and the comedy never deflates the genuine mystery. This is hard to do and the piece mostly succeeds. AMATEURISH ELEMENTS: The chapter 7 beginning repeating chapter 6's opening is a notable error. Some of the later chapters feel like they're spinning wheels. The "I'm not going. Obviously I'm not going... But I set an alarm for 11:45" is a well-worn YA trope deployed without subversion. Overall this is a strong, confident piece of genre writing with a genuinely distinctive voice, some real wit, and effective genre mechanics, held back by structural repetition and an unresolved ending that feels incomplete rather than deliberately open. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 14 Pacing: 12 World Building: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Coherent: 12 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Unearned Transformations: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 72.9/100
+ Space Western — Cargo Unknown (Chapter Avg: 76.3 | Final: 66.4)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Space Western Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: Captain, something&#x27;s... moving in that crate we picked up. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Character Ideas - **Captain Variants**: Grizzled veteran smuggler; Young idealistic freight hauler in over their head; Former military officer turned merchant; Bounty hunter with a code; Space prospector down on their luck - **Crew Types**: Ragtag family of misfits; Professional mercenaries; Skeleton crew barely keeping ship running; Mix of humans and aliens; Former convicts seeking redemption - **The Crate's Origin**: Abandoned space station; Derelict colony ship; Black market deal gone wrong; Legal salvage that's more than it seems; Payment for a mysterious job ## What's In The Crate? - **Living Creature Options**: Alien predator/parasite; Escaped bio-weapon; Rare endangered species worth millions; Shapeshifter; Sentient AI in biological form; Time-displaced human; Colony of microscopic aliens that form a collective intelligence - **Plot Twists**: It's a child/innocent being; It's connected to one crew member's past; It's hunting something else on the ship; Multiple entities; It's from Earth's distant past/future ## Tone Possibilities - **Horror-Western**: Alien meets Tombstone - isolated crew faces deadly threat - **Adventure-Western**: Indiana Jones in space - valuable cargo leads to chase across frontier - **Noir-Western**: Morally gray choices, everyone has secrets, no clear heroes - **Comedy-Western**: Misunderstandings and chaos as crew deals with unexpected passenger - **Drama-Western**: Focus on human relationships tested by crisis ## Story Arc Ideas 1. **The Hunt**: Creature escapes, becomes predator, crew must survive and trap it 2. **The Moral Dilemma**: Creature is valuable but dangerous, crew divided on what to do 3. **The Connection**: Creature bonds with crew member, others see it as threat 4. **The Conspiracy**: Crate was planted deliberately, someone knows what they're carrying 5. **The Transformation**: Creature begins changing the ship/crew in unexpected ways ## Setting Elements - **Ship Types**: Rusty freighter; Modified mining vessel; Old military transport; Cobbled-together salvage ship - **Locations**: Outer rim territories; Asteroid mining belts; Lawless space between systems; Dying colonies; Space truck stops and trading posts ## Conflict Drivers - **External Threats**: Pirates wanting the cargo; Government/corporate agents; Other alien species; Environmental hazards; Mechanical failures - **Internal Conflicts**: Trust issues among crew; Moral disagreements; Past grudges surfacing; Survival vs profit; Authority challenges ## Pacing Options - **Slow Burn** (Chapters 1-3 setup, 4-5 escalation, 6-7 climax, 8 resolution) - **Fast Start** (Immediate crisis chapter 1, complications throughout) - **Multiple Crescendos** (Several crisis points building to finale) ## Western Elements to Include - Frontier justice, law far away - Saloon/bar equivalent (mess hall scenes) - Showdowns and standoffs - Honor codes and broken promises - Isolation and self-reliance - Gold rush/prospecting parallels - Indigenous rights parallels with aliens - Train robbery parallel (ship heists) # Reflection Looking at these ideas, I need to find the sweet spot between compelling drama and authentic space western feel. **What Works:** - The moral dilemma angle is strong for westerns - it's not just about survival but about choices and codes - A creature that's both valuable and dangerous creates natural conflict between crew members - The isolation of space mirrors the frontier isolation perfectly - Having the creature be more than just a monster - perhaps sentient or juvenile - adds depth **What Doesn't Work as Well:** - Pure horror might overshadow the western elements - Comedy could undermine tension needed for 8 chapters - Too many external threats might dilute the core crate mystery - Overly complex conspiracy might not fit the straightforward western style **Most Compelling Direction:** I'm drawn to combining several elements: - A morally complex captain (not purely good or bad) - A divided crew with different motivations - A creature that's valuable, dangerous, but also sympathetic - External pressure from those who want it - Western themes of survival, loyalty, and difficult choices on the frontier The story should feel like a pressure cooker - contained mostly to the ship with mounting tension as different parties want different things. The creature should evolve from threat to something more complex, forcing characters to make hard choices. The space western tone works best with: - Practical, lived-in technology (not sleek sci-fi) - Focus on personal stakes over galaxy-saving - Moral ambiguity where survival often trumps ethics - Strong character voices with frontier dialect - Action balanced with character moments 8 chapters gives room for: - Proper setup and worldbuilding - Character development and reveals - Multiple complications and reversals - Satisfying climax and resolution The sweet spot seems to be: A cargo run gone wrong, where the crew must decide between profit, survival, and doing what's right, all while dealing with a creature that's more than it initially appears and external forces closing in.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** A struggling freight captain and their small crew discover their salvaged cargo contains a young alien creature worth a fortune to the right buyer—but also being hunted by those who created it. As the creature begins to trust them, the crew must choose between profit, survival, and protecting an innocent life. **Tone & Style:** Gritty space western with moral complexity. The narrative voice will be close third-person, alternating between the Captain and 1-2 crew members to show different perspectives. Dialogue will blend frontier vernacular with spacer slang—practical and rough around the edges. Technology is analog, mechanical, unreliable—switches, levers, and duct tape rather than holograms and AI. **What I'm Aiming For:** - Character-driven drama within action framework - Moral ambiguity where "right" choices have real costs - The creature as catalyst for revealing character truths - Authentic working-class space frontier feel - Building sympathy for the creature gradually - Tension through confined spaces and limited resources - Western showdown dynamics adapted to spacecraft - Found family themes emerging under pressure **What I'm Avoiding:** - Galaxy-spanning stakes or chosen one narratives - Clean-cut heroes and villains - Over-explaining technology or worldbuilding - The creature as pure monster or pure victim - Excessive external threats that dilute core conflict - Resolution that's too neat or morally simple **Character Dynamics:** - Captain: Former military, now barely keeping ship flying, haunted by past compromises - First Mate: Younger, idealistic, sees opportunity for fresh start - Engineer: Older, pragmatic, has survived by never getting attached - The creature: Juvenile of engineered species, dangerous when threatened but capable of bonding # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "Salvage Rights" (1000 words)** Opens with the *Dusty Jake* claiming salvage from a derelict research vessel in the Outer Reaches. Captain Sara Chen and her crew (first mate Tommy, engineer Brick) are desperate for a score. They find the crate among corporate research equipment, sealed with military-grade locks. Against Brick's warnings, Sara decides to take it. First hints that someone else was on that derelict recently. Chapter ends with them jumping to transit space, and Tommy hearing something shift inside the crate. **Chapter 2: "Precious Cargo" (1000 words)** The crew debates opening the crate. Sara recognizes the corporate markings—Weyland-Yamaha, known for bioweapons. Tommy discovers scratches inside the crate. When they carefully crack it open, they find a sedated creature—vaguely reptilian, clearly engineered, juvenile. Brick wants to space it immediately. Tommy sees dollar signs. Sara notices its collar has a tracker (damaged). The creature begins to wake. They lock it in the cargo hold just as proximity alarms announce an incoming ship. **Chapter 3: "The Wolves Circle" (1000 words)** A sleek corporate vessel hails them—Weyland-Yamaha security chief Marcus Rains wants to "inspect" their salvage, offers to buy any "biological materials" for good money. Sara stalls, claims engine problems. The creature is now awake, terrified, making sounds that might be language. Tommy realizes it's trying to communicate. Brick discovers the tracker is partially active—they're being hunted. Sara must decide: turn over the creature for enough money to fix all their problems, or run. She chooses to run, making an enemy of Rains. **Chapter 4: "What We Carry" (1000 words)** During the chase, the ship takes damage. They're forced to hide in an asteroid field. Tommy spends time with the creature, names it "Scout," realizes it's learning their language frighteningly fast. Scout draws pictures—shows them what Weyland-Yamaha was doing: breeding these creatures as weapons, but Scout's generation developed sentience. Brick reveals he worked corporate security once, knows what happens to people who cross these companies. Sara confesses she left military because she was ordered to destroy a colony ship of refugees. Everyone's carrying guilt. Scout escapes the cargo hold. **Chapter 5: "Trust and Teeth" (1000 words)** Scout is loose on the ship during repairs. Initially seems like a hunt, but they realize Scout is helping—fixing things, learning their systems. It's incredibly intelligent. However, when Brick corners it accidentally, Scout's defensive capabilities emerge—acid spit, camouflage, incredible speed. Brick is injured but not killed—Scout held back. Tommy treats both Brick and Scout's wounds (it was injured in its escape from Weyland-Yamaha). Sara makes contact with an old military friend who might know a safe place for Scout. But it means going through corporate-controlled space. **Chapter 6: "The Rattler's Den" (1000 words)** They dock at Rattler's Station for supplies and intel. Sara meets her contact while Tommy watches Scout and Brick repairs the ship. Rains has put a bounty on them. Bar confrontation with bounty hunters—classic western standoff in a space saloon. Sara's contact betrays her—he's working for Rains now. They barely escape, but Rains' ship is waiting. He offers one last deal: the creature for their lives and enough money to disappear. Scout, understanding every word, looks at Tommy with trust. The crew must decide together. **Chapter 7: "Stand and Deliver" (1000 words)** The crew votes to protect Scout. They use their knowledge of the asteroid field to set a trap. Space battle with western shoot-out dynamics—using debris as cover, engines instead of horses, missiles instead of bullets. Brick rigs the empty crate as a decoy bomb. Scout helps by interfacing with their systems, enhancing their old ship's capabilities. But Rains boards them for a final confrontation. Sara faces him while Tommy gets Scout to an escape pod. Brick must choose between saving his own skin and helping his crew family. **Chapter 8: "New Trails" (1000 words)** The showdown: Rains reveals Sara's military past—she killed innocents under orders. But she's done following bad orders. Brick arrives to even the odds. Scout returns from the pod to protect its new family, but Sara stops it from killing Rains—they're better than that. They maroon Rains on the derelict where they found Scout, with just enough supplies to survive until rescue. Scout has become part of the crew, helps them navigate to a frontier world where different species coexist. They're still broke, still flying a rustbucket, but they're free and they've kept their souls. Final image: the crew sharing a meal together, Scout included, as they head toward a binary sunset.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How Humans vs LLMs Approach Writing ## How a Human Writer Might Approach This Prompt A human writer seeing "Captain, something's... moving in that crate we picked up" would likely: 1. **Start with visceral instinct** - What scares THEM about something unknown moving in a confined space? What personal experience of dread can they tap into? 2. **Get messy with it** - Maybe write three different opening scenes, pick the weirdest one. Maybe the story becomes about something completely different than the crate by chapter 4 because that's where the characters led them. 3. **Pull from specific influences** - Not just "space western" but "I want this to feel like that one scene in Firefly where..." or "like Blood Meridian but in space" 4. **Include personal obsessions** - Maybe they're really into mushroom cultivation and somehow that becomes part of how the creature works. Or they have opinions about labor rights that bleed through. 5. **Write toward uncertainty** - Not knowing how it ends when they start, discovering the story through writing it 6. **Include inconvenient details** - Someone has IBS. The ship's toilet backs up at the worst moment. People say the wrong thing and can't take it back. ## Telltale LLM Writing Approaches ### General LLM Failings: **1. Hyper-competent plot efficiency** - Every detail serves the narrative perfectly - Every character arc completes satisfyingly - Chekhov's gun always fires exactly when needed - No loose threads or red herrings **2. Emotional safety** - Violence is purposeful and clean - Sex/attraction is either absent or meaningful - Trauma is processed healthily - Bad guys get comeuppance, good guys learn lessons **3. Thematic obviousness** - Metaphors are clear and consistent - Character growth maps perfectly to theme - Dialogue that states what stories are "about" - Every scene reinforces the central message **4. Linguistic tells** - "Palpable tension" and other workshop phrases - Perfectly balanced sentence structures - Descriptions that hit all five senses in order - Ending chapters on studied cliffhangers - Everyone speaks in complete sentences unless specifically characterized not to **5. Conflict resolution addiction** - Every argument gets resolved - Misunderstandings are cleared up - People apologize and mean it - Teams come together despite differences ## Deep Dive Critique of My Plan ### Where I've Fallen Into LLM Tropes: **1. The creature is too perfectly sympathetic** - It's juvenile (innocent) - It's been experimented on (victim) - It helps the crew (useful) - It bonds with Tommy (loveable) - This is SAFE. Where's the genuine alienness? The uncomfortable reality of a true predator? **2. The moral resolution is too clean** - They vote to protect Scout (democratic!) - They don't kill Rains (moral high ground!) - Scout becomes family (aww!) - Real humans might: Kill Rains and feel nothing. Sell Scout and rationalize it. Have someone betray the group for money and NOT redeem themselves. **3. Character arcs are too neat** - Sara: military guilt → redemption through saving innocent - Brick: cynical survivor → finds family - Tommy: idealist → proven right - Where are the people who don't change? Who get worse? Who were right all along in ugly ways? **4. The pacing is too television-perfect** - Each chapter advances plot steadily - Revelations come at optimal moments - Action-reflection-action pattern - Real stories stall out, go sideways, spend too long on weird details **5. The world-building is too utilitarian** - Weyland-Yamaha (obvious Alien reference) - "Outer Reaches" (generic frontier) - Everything exists to serve THIS story - Where's the weird shit that's just there? The subplot about someone's divorce? The running gag that doesn't pay off? **6. Relationships are too functional** - Everyone has a role and stays in it - Conflicts arise from external pressure - No one genuinely dislikes each other for petty reasons - Where's the crew member who's just annoying? The sexual tension that makes everything worse? ## Brainstorming More Human Approaches ### Make it messier: **Character additions/changes:** - Someone who's actively high during crucial moments - A crew member who NEVER accepts Scout and that's never resolved - Someone filming everything to sell as content, making things worse - A character who speaks mostly in quotes from old Earth movies - Someone who's brilliant but also genuinely stupid about specific things **Plot complications that go nowhere:** - They think Scout is one thing for 3 chapters but are completely wrong - Someone's ex shows up at the worst moment for unrelated reasons - The ship AI has opinions but they're usually wrong - They spend a whole chapter dealing with a clogged fuel line - Someone gets space flu and is useless for half the story **Uncomfortable elements:** - Scout smells terrible, like genuinely awful - Someone suggests eating Scout when food runs low - The corporate guy is actually reasonable and they're arguably in the wrong - Scout kills something they love and doesn't understand why they're upset - Someone has a sexual reaction to danger that freaks everyone out **Language/style shifts:** - One chapter that's entirely maintenance logs - Dialogue where people talk over each other and never finish sentences - A character who won't stop explaining their conspiracy theory about Earth - Technical descriptions that are clearly wrong but no one notices - In-jokes that are never explained to the reader **Unresolved elements:** - Someone steals money and it's never addressed - A romantic subplot that just peters out - They never find out what Scout actually is - Someone's backstory is three different contradictory versions - The ending leaves them worse off than they started **Specific over generic:** - Not "Outer Reaches" but "The Fuckoff Nebula" (named by the first surveyor) - Not protein bars but specifically everyone's sick of lime-flavored yeast - Not "the Captain's past" but Sara's specific memory of the smell of burning meat - Not "corporate security" but Rains' particular habit of humming while he works - Not "Scout learns language" but Scout specifically can't pronounce "R" sounds and it's never cute **Genre disruption:** - Chapter 5 becomes a bottle episode about someone's birthday - The climax is interrupted by mandatory bureaucracy - The western showdown is via competitive maintenance - Someone solves a problem by actually reading the manual - The scariest moment is about taxes **Real human concerns:** - Someone's genuinely worried about their credit score - Long argument about whose turn it is to clean - Someone's medication runs out and they become unbearable - Toilet paper rationing becomes a serious plot point - Someone's just been dumped via long-range message and won't shut up about it
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** A broke freight crew discovers their salvaged crate contains something alive—a genuine alien predator that starts hunting them through their ship. But the real problem isn't the creature; it's that everyone on board has their own desperate reason to reach port, and they can't agree whether to turn back, call for help, or try to kill it. The creature becomes almost secondary to their inability to work together when it matters. **Tone & Voice:** Claustrophobic space western told in close third-person, jumping between perspectives without warning. Dialogue overlaps, interrupts, goes nowhere. Technical language is sometimes wrong (the characters don't know everything they think they do). The prose is workmanlike, occasionally vulgar, with moments of unexpected poetry when someone's guard drops. **What I'm Aiming For:** - The creature remains genuinely alien—we never fully understand it - People make bad decisions for human reasons (horny, tired, stubborn, drunk) - The western elements are there but broken: the showdown happens in a bathroom, the honor code gets people killed - Specific, lived-in details: everyone knows the coffee maker sounds like Sara's ex - Multiple problems happening at once with no clear priority - Characters who don't learn lessons, just survive (or don't) - An ending that solves the immediate problem but nothing else **What I'm Avoiding:** - The creature becoming sympathetic or part of the family - Clear moral choices with clear outcomes - Everyone getting along when it counts - Explaining what the creature is or where it came from - Character arcs that complete - Technical accuracy over emotional truth - Resolution of interpersonal conflicts **Key Characters:** - Sara Chen: Captain. Functioning alcoholic.
