Kit made a sound like laughing with a throat full of gravel. It had moved to the ceiling, spread out like water damage, dripping occasionally. "One hour's almost up," Brick said. He'd been trying to rig something with the waste system for forty minutes but kept forgetting what because morphine. "If I can just reverse the flow—" "You're going to blow us up," Yuma said. She'd been laughing for ten minutes straight, that kind of laugh that starts funny and ends concerning. "Only a little bit." Captain Rains called again. His face filled the screen, patient and corporate-pleasant. "Captain Chen, we're ready to dock. Permission to—" "Actually, we're pretty busy right now," Sara said. "Maybe tomorrow?" "I'm afraid our schedule won't permit that. Company policy. You understand." "Do I?" "You did serve with Marcus Chen, correct? Martian Territorial Guard? Shame what happened with that mining strike. The casualties." Sara's hand hovered over the disconnect. "That's not—" "Oh, I'm not judging. Following orders. We all do what we must. Like now, for instance. I'm following orders to retrieve our property." Kit dropped from the ceiling, landed wrong, too many joints bending in directions that made everyone look away. It pressed something into Sara's mind—not words, not images, but understanding. Like suddenly knowing the taste of a color. The *Reasonable Doubt* hadn't found the derelict by accident. They'd made it. Killed everyone aboard, left Kit in a crate like cheese in a mousetrap. They were fishing for something that had been following Kit across three systems. Something big enough that even Kit was just bait. "Oh," Yuma gasped between laughs. "Oh that's—that's so much worse than I thought." "What?" Tommy turned his camera on her. "They don't want Kit. They want what wants Kit. And it's—" She drew figures in the air with her fingers, numbers that didn't exist. "It's already here." The proximity alarm went off again. But there was no ship on the scanner. Just empty space that was somehow too empty, like something was editing itself out of reality. "Captain Chen," Rains continued, pleasant as poison, "I'm going to be frank. We know you have specimen XK-17. We know it's been manipulating your navigation. We know you're three days from anywhere and running on fumes. Dock with us, take the money, walk away. Everyone wins." "Everyone but Kit," Tommy said. "The specimen isn't a 'Kit.' It's not even sentient, not really. It's just very good at mimicking—" Kit spoke in Rains' voice, perfect reproduction: "Specimen XK-17 showed remarkable adaptation to psychological warfare. Recommend increasing bounty." Then in another voice, female, scared: "Please, we have children on this ship, please don't—" Then screaming. Seventeen different voices screaming. "See?" Rains said, unperturbed. "Mimicry. Nothing more." Sara flipped a coin. Her lucky coin, the one Marcus gave her when they got married. Heads they dock, tails they run. It came up tails. Then the coin melted. Kit had touched it with something, turned it to liquid that ran through Sara's fingers. "That was my lucky coin," she said. "Lucky's relative," Kit said in her voice. "Brick, how's that bomb coming?" "What bomb?" Brick looked confused. "I'm fixing the toilet." "The bomb toilet." "Oh. That. Yeah, it's ready. Probably. What's it for again?" The space around the *Reasonable Doubt* started to bend. Not the ship—space itself, folding like origami. Something was coming through. Something that had been chasing Kit across systems. Something that corporate wanted badly enough to sacrifice a crew for bait. "Jesus," Tommy whispered. His camera couldn't process what it was seeing, kept trying to focus on something that wouldn't stay in three dimensions. "Captain Chen," Rains said, still professionally pleasant even as his ship started to buckle, "I strongly recommend you dock with us immediately." "Why?" Sara asked. "So that thing can eat us both?" "The entity is contained. We have protocols—" His ship split open like fruit. Not explosively—delicately, carefully, something peeling it apart to get at what was inside. Rains kept talking even as atmosphere vented behind him: "Company policy requires me to inform you that interfering with salvage operations will result in—" The feed cut. The *Reasonable Doubt* was being eaten, or absorbed, or translated into something else. Sara couldn't tell which. Kit was screaming—not in fear, but in recognition. Whatever was out there, it knew Kit. Had been looking for Kit. Kit had been running from home. "We need to go," Sara said. "Now." "Go where?" Tommy asked. "That thing's between us and everywhere." Kit flowed down from the ceiling, touched the engine console with too many fingers. The display went mad—numbers that shouldn't exist, readings that violated physics. The fusion core (or fission, or whatever Brick called it) started making a sound like singing. "What are you doing?" Sara demanded. "Going through," Kit said in all their voices at once. "Not around. Through." "Through what?" "Through the thing that's hunting me. Through space. Through home. Through the only way out." "That's insane." "Yes." The *Dusty Jake* lurched forward, moving in directions that ships shouldn't move. The thing that had been eating the *Reasonable Doubt* turned toward them, opened something that might have been mouths, might have been doors, might have been possibilities. "Brick!" Sara shouted. "The toilet bomb!" "Which button?" "Any button!" He pushed something. The waste system exploded backward, propelling them forward into the mouth-door-possibility. Sara's last coherent thought before physics stopped making sense was that she should have signed the divorce papers when she had the chance. Then they were through, and Kit was laughing with their voices, and space was the wrong color, and Tommy's camera finally gave up and started showing only static that looked like screaming. "Still want overtime pay," Brick mumbled, then passed out. Yuma kept laughing.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 of "Severance" is a strong execution of the plan's most ambitious chapter—the pivot where the creature is revealed as prey rather than predator, and the corporate antagonist is introduced and immediately destroyed. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter successfully executes its central reversal: Kit was bait, not the threat. This is the plan's key twist and it lands cleanly. The reveal is delivered through Kit's psychic impression rather than exposition, which respects the "show don't tell" principle and keeps the creature alien. The line "Kit had been running from home" is genuinely affecting and earns its brevity. Character consistency is strong throughout. Sara's coin flip is perfect—it matches her established decision-making style and the coin melting is a nice touch. Brick's morphine-addled confusion ("What bomb? I'm fixing the toilet. / The bomb toilet. / Oh. That.") is genuinely funny and consistent with his established state. Tommy filming everything, including his camera failing to process the entity, is exactly right. Yuma's laughter that "starts funny and ends concerning" fits her arc of having touched the fractal objects. The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Rains is effectively menacing through corporate pleasantness—his continued professionalism as his ship is destroyed is darkly comic and well-executed. Kit's mimicry of the screaming voices from the derelict is chilling and serves the plot. The exchange "That was my lucky coin" / "Lucky's relative" works because it's earned by the coin's established significance (Marcus gave it to her). The pacing is excellent. The chapter moves fast, escalating from negotiation to revelation to catastrophe to escape in a way that feels breathless without being incoherent. The prose is workmanlike in the best sense—it doesn't overreach. "Pleasant as poison" is a slight cliché but not egregious. "Like cheese in a mousetrap" for Kit's situation is effective and unpretentious. The description of the entity as "something that was editing itself out of reality" is genuinely good—strange without being overwrought. WEAKNESSES: The chapter occasionally tells rather than shows emotional states. "Sara's hand hovered over the disconnect" is fine, but we're told Yuma has been "laughing for ten minutes straight" rather than experiencing it. The plan called for Yuma to "understand first, starts laughing, won't stop"—this is present but slightly underdeveloped. The "mouth-door-possibility" construction is the chapter's most strained moment of attempted poetry. It's trying to convey the ineffable but lands as a list rather than an image. Similarly, "space was the wrong color" is a bit of a shortcut. The plan specified Sara "chooses based on a coin flip. Heads. They run." but in the chapter it comes up tails and then the coin melts—a small deviation that actually works better dramatically but is worth noting. The entity that's hunting Kit is necessarily vague, but the chapter could do slightly more to make its presence felt before it appears. The transition from "proximity alarm" to "space that was somehow too empty" to full manifestation is a bit rushed. Rains' continued talking as his ship is destroyed is effective but the line "Company policy requires me to inform you that interfering with salvage operations will result in—" is perhaps one beat too on-the-nose in its corporate satire. It's funny but slightly undercuts the horror. The ending—Brick passing out, Yuma laughing, Tommy's camera showing "static that looked like screaming"—is appropriately chaotic and consistent with the plan's vision of characters who don't learn lessons, just survive. "Still want overtime pay" is a perfect Brick line. OVERALL: This is a well-crafted chapter that successfully executes a complex plan. The humor and horror are balanced effectively, the characters are consistent, and the central reversal is delivered with appropriate economy. The prose is controlled without being flat. Minor issues with occasional telling over showing and one or two strained images don't significantly undermine what is otherwise a confident, entertaining piece of genre fiction with genuine wit. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 16 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: Exit Interview They came out the other side wrong. Not damaged—wrong. The *Dusty Jake* was flying backward at full thrust, except the engines were off. The nav said they were at Perdition Station, then three systems away, then inside a sun, then the nav gave up and just displayed question marks. Sara threw up nothing into a bag that wasn't there before. "What the fuck did we just—" "We went through," Kit said, using Brick's voice. Brick was unconscious, but his mouth moved anyway. "The shortest distance between two points is through something hungry." Outside the viewports, space looked normal again. Stars where stars should be. No impossible folding things trying to eat reality. Just the familiar beacon of Perdition Station, somehow only six hours away. "How?" Tommy asked. His camera was smoking, displaying every frame it had ever recorded simultaneously. "Time's weird in the walls," Kit said, now using Yuma's voice. Yuma was still laughing, but silently now, tears streaming. "Space is just walls with nothing between them." A piece of the *Reasonable Doubt* floated past their viewport. Just the nameplate, somehow intact. No sign of Rains or his crew. Or the thing that had been eating them. "Are they dead?" Sara asked. "They're in the walls now," Kit said. "What walls?" "All of them." Sara's comm buzzed. A message from Perdition Station—docking approval for berth seventeen. She hadn't requested it. The timestamp was from three days ago. Or three days from now. The computer couldn't decide. "I need to go," Kit said, using Tommy's voice while Tommy filmed its absence of face. "They'll come looking again. Corporate always does." "Go where?" Sara asked. Kit flowed toward the airlock, carrying things—Tommy's broken camera, Brick's severed hand from the coffee maker, three lime protein bars, Yuma's tire iron. It arranged them in a pattern that hurt to look at. "Away. Through. Somewhere corporate can't follow." "That's not an answer." "It's seventeen answers." Kit touched the airlock controls with something that wasn't quite a tentacle. The outer door opened without depressurizing. Space didn't rush in. Kit flowed out. The door closed. Pressure normal. Like physics had just agreed to look the other way. Tommy rushed to the viewport. "It's gone. Just... gone." "Good riddance," Sara said, but she was looking at where Kit had drawn something on the wall. Numbers? Letters? A picture of something that couldn't exist? It almost looked like "Thank you" if she squinted, or "Fuck you" if she tilted her head. They limped to Perdition Station on momentum and whatever Kit had done to their engines. Brick woke up two hours out, looked at his stump, said "Huh, still gone," and went back to fixing the coffee maker one-handed. Yuma stopped laughing and started apologizing in languages that might not exist. Tommy posted the corrupted footage and gained forty-three million followers who thought it was performance art. Docking was normal. Absurdly normal. Like the universe had decided to pretend the last week hadn't happened. Marcus was waiting at the berth. Older, grayer, still handsome in that way that made Sara angry. He had papers and a stylus and that expression like he'd swallowed something bitter. "Sara." "Marcus." "You look like shit." "Yeah." He handed her the papers. She signed without reading. The stylus was wet—she realized she was crying. When had that started? "Keep the ship," Marcus said. "I don't want it." "It's half yours." "No. It's all yours. Always was. I was just borrowing space in it." He looked at the *Dusty Jake*, at the scorch marks and the duct tape and the things growing on the hull that might be barnacles or might be something Kit left behind. "Take care of yourself, Sara." "I won't." "I know." He left. Sara stood there holding papers that made her officially divorced, officially sole owner of a ship held together by spite and expired warranties. "Captain?" Tommy appeared at the airlock. "Brick says the coffee maker's fixed but it's only making lime-flavored coffee now." "Of course it is." "And Yuma's disappeared. Left a note that just says 'seventeen' over and over." "Of course she did." "And there's a salvage opportunity. Good money. Some cargo floating in sector seven." "Of course there is." Two weeks later, they were back in space. New crew member—some kid named Rex who claimed to be a navigator but was obviously lying. Brick had a backwards prosthetic hand that he refused to fix properly. Tommy had downgraded to twelve followers after people realized the footage wasn't art, just weird. Sara was drunk again, that perfect 0.08 BAC where decisions felt right even when they weren't. "Derelict ahead," Rex announced, trying to sound professional. "Looks like good salvage." It was. Mostly crap, but enough valuable crap to make dock fees. And one crate, military-grade locks, Weyland-Yamaha stamps. "Leave it," Brick said. "It's probably worth something," Tommy said, not filming anymore. His new camera couldn't handle the memories. Sara touched the crate. It was warm. Wet. Inside, something moved. Knock knock. Pause. Knock. "Nope," Sara said, and turned the music louder. The coffee maker started playing her wedding song in lime-flavored burbles. She pretended not to notice. They took the crate anyway. Three hours later, Rex knocked on her quarters. "Captain? Something's... moving in that crate we picked up." Sara looked at the whiskey, the divorce papers she'd framed on the wall for some reason, the number seventeen that appeared everywhere now like a cosmic joke. "Of course it is," she said, and took another drink. Outside, space was full of walls and the things that lived in them. The *Dusty Jake* flew through it all, backwards and forwards, drunk captain and lying crew and something in a crate that knocked politely. Kit's drawing on the wall had changed. Now it definitely said "See you soon." Or maybe "Seventeen." Or maybe nothing at all. Time was weird in the walls. Everything was weird in the walls. Sara turned the music louder and didn't look.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 serves as the conclusion to an ambitious space western horror story, and it largely delivers on the plan's stated goals while exhibiting some notable strengths and weaknesses. **Strengths:** The chapter successfully executes the plan's core mandate: "An ending that solves the immediate problem but nothing else." Sara signs the divorce papers, the creature leaves, they survive—but nothing is resolved in any meaningful sense. The final scene with Rex and the new crate is a genuinely effective circular structure that earns its cynicism. The plan called for this explicitly, and the execution is clean. The creature's departure is handled with appropriate alienness. "Kit" using different crew members' voices, the airlock opening without depressurizing, the "seventeen answers" exchange—these maintain the creature's inscrutability as planned. The line "The shortest distance between two points is through something hungry" is genuinely good: strange, internally logical, and tonally right. The Marcus scene works. It's brief, emotionally honest, and avoids sentimentality. "I was just borrowing space in it" is a strong line that earns its metaphorical weight. Sara crying without knowing when she started is a good character moment. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which suits the characters. The plan's instruction to avoid "character arcs that complete" is honored. Brick adapts to his backwards prosthetic without complaint. Yuma disappears leaving only "seventeen." Tommy loses followers. These are anti-resolutions that feel true to the story's ethos. **Weaknesses:** The chapter leans heavily on repetition as a structural device—"Of course it is" appears three times in quick succession, and while the first use lands, the device becomes mechanical by the third. This is a case where the author is reaching for a rhythm that tips into formula. "Kit" as a name for the creature is introduced here without prior establishment in this chapter (it presumably appeared earlier in the story), but within this chapter alone, the sudden naming feels slightly jarring. The plan never mentioned naming the creature, and the plan explicitly wanted it to remain alien and unknowable—giving it a name, even casually, slightly domesticates it. The "seventeen" motif is introduced and repeated but never quite earns its weight within this chapter alone. It appears as Yuma's note, Sara's observation, Kit's "seventeen answers," and the wall drawing. This is the kind of associative riffing that can feel profound or can feel like manufactured mysticism. Here it lands somewhere in between—it's not embarrassing, but it's not fully earned either. The line "Space is just walls with nothing between them" is the chapter's most overtly "poetic" moment and it's borderline—it sounds good but doesn't quite mean anything coherent. Similarly, "They're in the walls now / What walls? / All of them" is trying for cosmic horror but lands closer to a shrug. The plan called for "the western elements are there but broken: the showdown happens in a bathroom." Chapter 8's plan was for the creature to leave and Sara to sign papers. The western genre elements are essentially absent here—there's no showdown, no honor code, no frontier mythology being subverted. The space western framing has largely dissolved by this point, which may be intentional but represents a departure from the genre's promised texture. Tommy gaining 43 million followers who think the footage is performance art is a sharp, contemporary satirical touch that works well and feels earned. The ending—"See you soon" / "Seventeen" / "nothing at all" / "Time was weird in the walls"—is slightly over-extended. The chapter could have ended two paragraphs earlier with more impact. The final repetition of "Sara turned the music louder and didn't look" is effective as a callback, but the lines before it dilute the landing. **Character Consistency:** Sara is consistent—drunk, functional, emotionally avoidant, making the same mistakes. Marcus's brief appearance is well-handled. Brick's "Huh, still gone" is perfectly in character. Tommy's follower count subplot is consistent with his profile. Yuma's disappearance fits her character. These are all faithful to the profiles. **Overall Assessment:** This is a competent, occasionally sharp conclusion that honors most of its stated intentions. It avoids the worst pitfalls (no redemption arc, no explained creature, no tidy resolution) and delivers some genuinely good moments. Its weaknesses are in over-reliance on repetition as a substitute for development, some forced mysticism around "seventeen," and a slight tendency to tell us the emotional register rather than fully inhabit it. It's a solid piece of genre fiction with real craft, not a masterpiece, but well above average. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 7 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely ambitious piece of space western horror-comedy that demonstrates considerable craft alongside some notable weaknesses. Let me work through it systematically. STRENGTHS: The voice is the story's greatest asset. Sara Chen is rendered with consistent, lived-in specificity - her BAC tracking via implant, the divorce papers, the dock fees, the thing with Marcus's brother that keeps getting referenced obliquely. The crew dynamics are genuinely funny and feel earned: Brick's paranoid sobriety/philosophical morphine drunkenness, Tommy's pathetic follower count, Yuma's compulsive lying about competence. These aren't deep characters but they're consistent and entertaining. The alien entity "Kit" is handled with real creativity. The decision to make it communicate through mimicry, to have it learn the crew's emotional vulnerabilities (playing Marcus's voice, the wedding song), and to make it genuinely ambiguous - threatening but not malevolent - is sophisticated. The "time's weird in the walls" motif works as both comedy and genuine cosmic horror. The seventeen-degree motif is well-deployed throughout, creating genuine coherence across chapters. The structural callback in Chapter 8 (new crate, new crew member, same knock) is satisfying. The comedy lands more often than it fails. "Yes," Yuma replied to "how much morphine did you give him?" is genuinely funny. The Robert's Rules of Order meeting with Kit is a highlight - absurdist but internally consistent. WEAKNESSES: The piece leans heavily on a particular style of deadpan non-sequitur that sometimes works and sometimes reads as trying too hard. "Backwards is the only way forward" from Brick is the kind of line that sounds profound but is actually empty. There are several of these scattered throughout. The "time's weird in the walls" repetition, while effective as a motif, gets overused to the point where it becomes a crutch for avoiding actual explanation. The ending of Chapter 7 (going "through" the entity) is genuinely incoherent - not productively mysterious, just unclear. The physics of what happens is never established enough for the violation of those physics to feel meaningful. Yuma's arc is the weakest. She starts as a compulsive liar, gets psychically influenced by Kit, and then... disappears with a note saying "seventeen." This feels like the author didn't know what to do with her. Her transformation from lying new hire to Kit-touched mystic is the most "unearned" of the character changes. The corporate villain (Rains) is introduced too late and dispatched too quickly. He's a stock character who exists to provide exposition and then get eaten. The revelation that Kit was bait is interesting but arrives in Chapter 6 and is resolved by Chapter 7 - there's no time to sit with it. The prose occasionally reaches for poetry and misses. "The kind of noise that made your teeth itch" works. "Space is just walls with nothing between them" is the kind of line that sounds like it means something but doesn't quite. The ending's repetition of "Of course it is" is effective but the final paragraphs pile on too many callbacks simultaneously, diluting each one. The Marcus/divorce subplot is handled with surprising emotional intelligence - it's genuinely touching that Sara signs the papers while crying and doesn't know when she started. This is the story's most human moment and it earns its place. The dialogue is generally strong - characters sound distinct and the comedy is delivered through voice rather than situation alone. Brick's morphine monologues are particularly well-done. OVERALL: This is a competent-to-good piece of genre fiction with a strong voice, consistent characters, and genuine creative ambition in its alien entity design. Its weaknesses are structural (Yuma's arc, the rushed climax) and occasional over-reliance on the "sounds profound, means nothing" style of cosmic horror dialogue. It's entertaining throughout and the callback ending is satisfying, but it doesn't quite achieve the emotional depth it reaches for. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 13 Nuanced Characters: 11 Pacing: 12 World Building: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Coherent: 11 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 9 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 66.4/100
+ Possession Horror — Different Eyes (Chapter Avg: 64.6 | Final: 39.0)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Possession Horror Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: I caught her staring at me again. She thought I&#x27;d been sleeping. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Character Ideas - **The Watcher**: Could be a wife, girlfriend, roommate, sister, mother, or stranger who has moved in - **The Narrator**: Person being watched - potentially unreliable, paranoid, or genuinely in danger - **Supporting Cast**: Friends/family who might not believe the narrator, medical professionals, previous victims, occult experts ## Potential Plot Directions ### Direction 1: Parasitic Possession - The watcher is possessed by something that needs to study its next host - The entity transfers through prolonged eye contact during sleep - The narrator must stay awake to avoid possession - Previous victims could provide clues through journals/recordings ### Direction 2: Identity Theft Entity - The watcher is learning to become the narrator - Each night of observation, she becomes more like them - The narrator starts losing pieces of themselves - Culminates in a complete replacement scenario ### Direction 3: Protective Possession - The watcher is actually protecting the narrator from something worse - The staring is a form of guardian vigil - The real threat comes when the watching stops - Twist: the narrator is the actual danger ### Direction 4: Generational Curse - The watching behavior is part of an inherited family curse - The watcher (mother/grandmother) did this to previous family members - The narrator must break the cycle - Historical elements revealing the curse's origin ### Direction 5: Psychological Dissolution - The narrator and watcher are the same person (split personality) - The "watching" represents a fragmenting psyche - Reality becomes increasingly unreliable - Medical intervention conflicts with supernatural elements ## Tone Options - Slow-burn psychological horror with mounting paranoia - Body horror with physical transformation elements - Gothic family horror with generational trauma - Intimate domestic horror within a relationship - Surreal, dreamlike horror where reality is fluid ## Story Beat Ideas - Discovery of previous victims' accounts - Failed attempts to capture evidence - Confrontation that goes wrong - Ally who turns out to be compromised - False safety/victory moment - Revelation about the true nature of the threat - Desperate final gambit - Ambiguous or pyrrhic victory ## Pacing Considerations - Chapters 1-2: Establishment and growing unease - Chapters 3-4: Investigation and first confrontations - Chapters 5-6: Escalation and revelations - Chapters 7-8: Climax and resolution (or lack thereof) # Reflection Looking at these ideas, I think the most compelling direction would be a combination of the parasitic possession and identity theft concepts. This allows for both body horror and psychological horror elements while maintaining the intimate, domestic setting that makes the watching so unsettling. **What works:** - The intimate violation of being watched while sleeping creates immediate tension - A possession story explains the unnatural behavior while keeping things grounded in a relationship - The identity theft angle adds stakes beyond simple survival - the narrator risks losing who they are - Having the watcher be someone close (girlfriend/wife) adds emotional complexity - The need to stay awake creates a ticking clock as exhaustion mounts **What might not work:** - Pure psychological/split personality might feel too predictable - Making it too supernatural too quickly could lose the creepy realism - Too many supporting characters might dilute the intimate horror - Generational curse might be too complex for 8000 words **Best approach:** I'll focus on a parasitic entity that studies its hosts through the eyes of those they trust. The watcher (girlfriend/wife named Sarah) has been taken over by something that needs to understand its next host completely before transferring. The narrator (Jamie) must figure out what's happening while fighting exhaustion and isolation as no one believes them. The entity has done this before, leaving a trail of missing persons and broken relationships. The story will balance psychological paranoia with genuine supernatural threat, building to a confrontation where Jamie must choose between saving Sarah or stopping the entity. This approach allows for: - Intimate, claustrophobic horror - Escalating paranoia and isolation - Body horror elements as the entity's true nature is revealed - Emotional stakes through the relationship - A satisfying but potentially tragic conclusion Now I'll create the detailed chapter plan based on this direction.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I will craft an intimate possession horror story centered on the violation of being watched during our most vulnerable moments. The narrative follows Jamie (narrator, gender-neutral) who discovers their girlfriend Sarah has been possessed by a parasitic entity that must fully understand its next host before transferring bodies. **Voice & Style:** - First-person present tense to create immediacy and paranoia - Unreliable narrator elements as exhaustion mounts - Sensory-focused prose emphasizing the physical sensations of being watched - Domestic details that become increasingly sinister - Short, fragmented sentences during high tension moments **Plot Choices:** - The entity transfers through prolonged eye contact during REM sleep - It has possessed Sarah for weeks, studying Jamie's patterns - Previous victims left behind journals/recordings that provide crucial clues - The entity needs complete behavioral mapping before transfer - Sarah is still conscious but trapped inside, making the horror more personal **What I'll Aim For:** - Slow escalation from subtle wrongness to explicit horror - The uncanny valley of someone familiar becoming alien - Sleep deprivation as both symptom and survival mechanism - Isolation as Jamie's warnings are dismissed as paranoia - Body horror revealed gradually (Sarah's physical degradation) - Emotional complexity - saving Sarah vs. stopping the entity **What I'll Avoid:** - Excessive exposition about the entity's origins - Too many side characters diluting the intimate setting - Resolving things too neatly - some ambiguity should remain - Pure jump scares over atmospheric dread - Making Sarah completely villainous - she's a victim too # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "Pretending" (1000 words)** Opening with Jamie catching Sarah watching them at 3 AM. Establishes their two-year relationship and recent subtle changes in Sarah's behavior - she's been more attentive, asking unusual questions, mimicking Jamie's habits. Jamie confronts her, but Sarah dismisses it as insomnia. Introduction of Jamie's journal-keeping habit. Seeds of wrongness: Sarah's eyes don't quite focus right, her breathing doesn't match someone who was just asleep. **Chapter 2: "Patterns" (1000 words)** Jamie installs a sleep app to track their movements, discovering they've been having disturbed sleep for weeks without remembering. They catch Sarah standing over them again, this time for forty-three minutes straight. Sarah's explanation doesn't match the timestamp evidence. Jamie finds Sarah's notebook filled with detailed observations about their habits, measurements, sketches of their sleeping positions. Attempts to talk to best friend Marcus, who thinks it's relationship anxiety. **Chapter 3: "The Tenant" (1000 words)** Jamie discovers Sarah's previous apartment still being paid for and investigates. Finds the real Sarah's journals from before they met, documenting someone watching HER sleep - her ex-roommate Lisa who disappeared six months ago. Realizes this has happened before. Finds Lisa's recordings describing the same watching behavior, mentioning how "it needs to map the neural pathways." Jamie confronts Sarah, who doesn't deny it anymore - something shifts in her face, acknowledging the game has changed. **Chapter 4: "Awake" (1000 words)** Jamie refuses to sleep, using caffeine and stimulants. Day three of no sleep. Sarah becomes more aggressive in her watching, sitting closer, touching Jamie's face while they pretend to sleep. Jamie discovers Sarah hasn't eaten in five days but shows no hunger. Finds security footage from Lisa's building showing Sarah standing in the hallway for nine hours straight. Sarah's body is starting to show signs of degradation - fingernails loosening, hair falling out in clumps she doesn't notice. **Chapter 5: "The Rules" (1000 words)** Through Lisa's research and coded messages, Jamie learns the entity requires 100 hours of REM sleep observation for complete mapping. They've already been watched for approximately 87 hours over the past weeks. Jamie tries to leave but finds Sarah has anticipated every escape route. The entity, speaking through Sarah more directly now, explains it's not malicious - it simply needs to survive, to continue. It reveals Sarah is still conscious inside, experiencing everything. Jamie hears Sarah's real voice break through briefly, begging them to run. **Chapter 6: "Deterioration" (1000 words)** Day five without sleep. Jamie is hallucinating, unsure what's real. Sarah's body is failing faster - skin graying, eyes clouding, movements becoming jerky. The entity is running out of time, becoming desperate. It reveals it can only transfer during a specific sleep state, and if the host body dies before transfer, it dies too. Jamie realizes they have a choice: let Sarah's body fail and kill both Sarah and the entity, or sleep and sacrifice themselves to save Sarah. Marcus arrives for an intervention, but the entity has already spread its influence. **Chapter 7: "The Transfer" (1000 words)** Jamie makes a desperate plan using Lisa's notes about disrupting the transfer with specific light frequencies. They pretend to finally collapse from exhaustion while Marcus (now partially influenced) watches. The entity begins the transfer, Sarah's body convulsing as it prepares to jump. Jamie springs the trap, but it only partially works - the entity is caught between bodies. In the struggle, Jamie makes eye contact with the real Sarah, still trapped inside, who mouths "together" before Jamie understands what she means. **Chapter 8: "Morning" (1000 words)** The aftermath is ambiguous. Jamie wakes up in a hospital, told they had a psychotic break. Sarah is in a coma. Marcus doesn't remember anything unusual. But Jamie finds Sarah's notebook in their belongings with a new entry in their own handwriting they don't remember making: detailed observations about the nurses' sleeping patterns. The final scene mirrors the opening - Jamie catches someone watching them sleep, but this time, they see their own reflection in the window, standing over Sarah's hospital bed, and they can't remember how long they've been standing there.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer discovering this prompt would likely start with a visceral, personal reaction. "I caught her staring at me again" - there's something deeply violating about this. A human might immediately connect it to: - Real experiences of feeling watched or unsafe in their own bed - Specific relationship anxieties about intimacy and trust - That primitive fear of opening your eyes in the dark - The actual physical sensation of being observed They'd probably start writing immediately from that feeling, not from plot structure. The story would grow from that moment of violation outward, rather than being architected. They might not even know what the watcher wants initially - just that feeling of wrongness. # Telltale LLM Approaches That Don't Serve Horror **1. Over-Explaining the Threat** LLMs love to create elaborate mythologies. We explain WHY the entity needs 100 hours, HOW it transfers, WHAT it wants. Humans often leave horror unexplained because the unknown is scarier. Stephen King often says he writes himself into corners without knowing how to get out. **2. Symmetrical Structure** My 8-chapter plan is suspiciously neat: setup, investigation, revelation, climax. Real horror is messy. Things happen too fast or too slow. Some chapters might be 300 words of panic, others 2000 words of dull dread. **3. Safety Nets** I've given Jamie allies (Marcus), explanations (Lisa's notes), and tools (light frequencies). LLMs always provide escape routes. Human horror writers often trap characters completely - no research that helps, no friends who believe them, no clever solutions. **4. Impressive Prose Over Authenticity** LLMs write "her breathing doesn't match someone who was just asleep" instead of "she wasn't breathing right." We choose literary over real. Horror needs ugly, simple sentences: "She was standing there again. Just standing." **5. Character Psychology That's Too Clean** My Jamie goes from suspicious to investigative to heroic. Real people in horror situations have mental breakdowns, make stupid decisions, become cruel, give up. They don't journal clues - they might scrawl "HELP" on walls. **6. The Need to Be Clever** The mirror ending, the "Sarah was conscious inside all along," the body-hopping rules - it's all very clever. Human writers might just let it be about the horror of your girlfriend becoming a stranger. No explanation needed. # Deep Dive Critique of My Plan **Chapter 1 Issues:** - Too much setup about "two-year relationship" - humans would start in the horrifying moment - Jamie keeping a journal is convenient plot device - The "subtle wrongness" is too subtle - humans would go for the gut punch **Chapter 2 Issues:** - Sleep app? Timestamps? Too technical and rational - Marcus exists solely to not believe Jamie (stock character) - Finding Sarah's notebook is too neat - real discovery is messier **Chapter 3 Issues:** - The apartment investigation is very "detective story," not horror - Lisa's recordings explaining everything is pure exposition - "Neural pathways" - too sci-fi, not visceral enough **Chapter 4 Issues:** - "Day three of no sleep" - too structured - Sarah's degradation is too symbolic/visual - Security footage is another "evidence" crutch **Chapter 5 Issues:** - THE RULES - classic LLM behavior, explaining the monster's mechanics - "100 hours of REM sleep" - why so specific? - Sarah breaking through is emotionally manipulative in a calculated way **Chapter 6 Issues:** - The "choice" is too moral/philosophical - Marcus returning for an "intervention" is too staged - "The entity has spread its influence" - vague and unscary **Chapter 7 Issues:** - "Desperate plan" using "specific light frequencies" - too much like solving a video game - The trap/struggle is action-movie stuff, not horror - "Together" - going for emotional resonance over fear **Chapter 8 Issues:** - Hospital/psychotic break is overdone - The ambiguous ending tries too hard to be clever - The mirror callback is too neat and literary # Brainstorming More Human Approaches **Raw, Visceral Elements:** - Start with Jamie unable to move, fully awake, while Sarah touches their face - No investigation - just escalating violations - Sarah starts sleeping in Jamie's position, eating their food half-chewed - Jamie finds their own hair in Sarah's mouth - No explanations for anything - just wrong behaviors - Sarah speaks in Jamie's sleep-talking voice - Jamie starts finding their belongings inside Sarah's body somehow **Messier Structure:** - One chapter that's just a single night of terror, 2000 words - Another that's fragments across weeks, 500 words - A chapter from Sarah's POV that makes no sense - Chapters that contradict each other - Time jumps without explanation - Ending mid-sentence when Jamie finally falls asleep **Remove the Safety Nets:** - No one else exists in this story - just Jamie and Sarah - No research or answers available - No rules to the possession - No heroic choices - just survival or submission - Jamie does terrible things to stay awake - The watching might mean nothing at all **Uglier, Simpler Language:** - "She was watching me again. I kept my breathing steady." - "Her eyes were wrong. I don't know how." - "I haven't slept in... I don't know." - "She's in the room. She's always in the room now." - Repetition: "watching watching watching watching" - Fragments and run-ons instead of proper sentences **Character Breakdown:** - Jamie stops bathing to stay uncomfortable/awake - They hurt themselves to avoid sleep - They consider killing Sarah but can't - They start watching Sarah back obsessively - They begin to enjoy the game - They forget why they're staying awake **Less Clever, More Disturbing:** - Sarah starts narrating Jamie's dreams out loud - She predicts Jamie's movements seconds before they make them - She begins existing in peripheral vision when she's not there - Jamie finds videos on their phone of themselves sleeping, taken from inside their own eyes - Sarah's face begins appearing in mirrors behind Jamie's own reflection - The watching doesn't stop even when Jamie's eyes are closed **Physical Horror Over Supernatural:** - Focus on the actual sensation of being watched - The ache of keeping eyes open - The specific smell of someone standing too close in the dark - The sound of someone breathing in sync with you - The warmth of breath you shouldn't be feeling - The weight of someone sitting on the bed who isn't touching it **Abandon the Possession Angle Entirely?** - What if Sarah just... watches? No reason. - What if she's trying to memorize Jamie before something happens? - What if she's checking if Jamie is still themselves? - What if Jamie has been replaced and Sarah knows? - What if the watching is protective and stopping would be worse? **Make It More Personal:** - Use specific details from a real relationship - The terror of someone you love becoming uncanny - The guilt of suspecting your partner - The shame of being afraid in your own bed - The exhaustion of hypervigilance - The doubt that maybe you're the problem This story should feel like a relationship dissolving through paranoia, not a monster mystery to be solved. The horror is domestic, intimate, and maybe not even supernatural - just the terror of sharing your bed with a stranger who knows everything about you.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This piece will be about the violation of being watched by someone you love, without explanation or resolution. The horror lies not in understanding why Sarah watches, but in the dissolution of intimacy and the impossibility of feeling safe in your own bed. **Voice & Style:** - First-person present tense, but fragmenting as exhaustion mounts - Simple, ugly sentences. "She's watching." Not "I observe her observing me." - Repetition and run-ons when panicking - Physical sensations over explanations - Time becomes unreliable - some chapters cover minutes, others weeks - No proper names after Chapter 2. She becomes "her." I become harder to hold onto. **Plot Choices:** - No explanation for the watching. Ever. - No investigation, no clues, no rules - No allies or outside world after Chapter 3 - Sarah's behavior escalates without pattern or reason - The watching spreads to other impossibilities (being watched while alone, through closed eyes) - Reality becomes negotiable through sleep deprivation **What I'll Aim For:** - The specific physical horror of being observed - Domestic details turned threatening (shared toothbrush, her coffee mug, our bed) - The shame and guilt of fearing your partner - Exhaustion as its own form of body horror - The terror that maybe nothing is wrong except you - Moments of normal relationship breaking through, making it worse **What I'll Avoid:** - Any mythology or explanation - Other characters providing perspective - Evidence or proof of wrongdoing - Heroic choices or clever solutions - Clear timeline or structure - Resolution # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "Again" (1000 words)** I catch her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping. This time I keep my eyes closed, count her breaths. Forty-three minutes. She doesn't move for forty-three minutes. When I "wake up," she's already turned away, but the sheets on her side are cold. She's been standing. I make coffee, she drinks from my mug while hers sits full. She asks what I dreamed. I lie. She says "No, you were running." She's right. This has been happening for... I can't remember when it started. Tonight I'll stay awake. **Chapter 2: "Tuesday" (1000 words)** Sarah makes breakfast using my grandmother's recipe I've never taught her. She's humming the song I had stuck in my head. She stands behind me while I work, breathing in sync. At dinner, she orders my food before I speak. "You wanted the salmon." I didn't. I eat it. In bed, I count to one thousand before opening my eyes. She's above me, face inches away, studying something behind my pupils. I scream. She doesn't flinch. "Bad dream?" she asks, already lying back down. The pillow on her side is still propped upright. She was never lying down. **Chapter 3: "Sick Day" (1000 words)** I call in sick. She calls in sick without me telling her I'm staying home. We sit on opposite ends of the couch. She watches TV but her eyes track me in the black reflection. I go to the bathroom - she's outside the door. I shower with the curtain open. She stands in the doorway. "Are you okay?" she asks. "Are you okay?" I ask. "Are you okay?" she asks, same inflection. I try to leave. The door sticks. It's never stuck before. My phone has no signal. Has never had no signal. She makes lunch. Two plates, but she eats from mine. **Chapter 4: "How Long" (1000 words)** Haven't slept. Three days? Four? She doesn't sleep either but pretends. I watch her pretend. She's too still. No dreams, no movement. I touch her face - room temperature. Like touching my own face. I look in the mirror. I look tired. She appears behind me, looking rested. "Come to bed." I make coffee. She pours it out. "Come to bed." I try to read. The words swim. She reads over my shoulder, out loud, but the words she says aren't on the page. They're the words I'm thinking. I close the book. She keeps reading. **Chapter 5: "Morning Question Mark" (1000 words)** Light outside but wrong color. She's in the kitchen in my clothes. They fit her now. When did she shrink. Or I grew. She's making breakfast. It's dinner food. The clock says 3:17. AM? PM? She serves me a plate. It's empty but she watches me eat. I mime chewing. She swallows. I'm so tired my teeth hurt. She brushes them for me. I let her. Her hand tastes like my hand. She puts me to bed. I resist. She's stronger. When was she stronger. She tucks me in. Sits on the edge. Watches. I watch back. We watch each other. Blinking contest. I lose. **Chapter 6: "Our Bed" (1000 words)** Wake up. Still tired. She's under the covers with me. Behind me. Arms around me. Breathing my breath. I breathe in, she breathes out the same air. I turn. She turns. Mirror. I look at her. I look at me. Which is which. The room has no doors now. When did we lose the doors. She speaks but it's my voice. "I caught you watching me." No. "You thought I was sleeping." No. That's not. She smiles with my smile. I check the mirror. I'm smiling. I'm not smiling. She laughs. My mouth laughs. I cover her mouth. My hand covers my mouth. **Chapter 7: "Together" (1000 words)** We move together now. Stand together. Eat together. One fork, four hands guiding it. She knows when I'll blink. I know when she'll breathe. The watching is inside now. Behind my eyes, she watches through them. Behind her eyes, I watch through them. We see ourselves seeing ourselves. Infinite mirrors. She was lonely, I think, or I think she thinks. The bed is too big for one. Too small for two. Just right for whatever we are. We don't sleep. We don't wake. We watch. The walls watch. The ceiling watches. Everything has eyes now. Or we have everything's eyes. **Chapter 8: "I/She/We" (1000 words)** I caught her staring at me again. She caught me staring at her again. We thought we'd been sleeping. But there is no sleep now, just the watching. The bed is empty. The bed is full. I stand beside it, watching her sleep. She stands beside it, watching me sleep. We are both sleeping. We are both watching. The woman in the bed has my face. Has her face. Has our face. She opens her eyes. We close ours. She's watching us now. From inside. From outside. From the place between sleep and waking where the watching lives. We thought she'd been sleeping. She was never sleeping. None of us were ever sleeping.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# The Narrator (unnamed, previously together with Sarah for ~2 years) They work from home doing medical transcription - used to the night shift, already prone to odd hours. Grew up in their grandmother's house after their parents' car accident when they were twelve. Still make her butterscotch pudding when anxious, though they don't even like butterscotch. Crack their knuckles constantly, a habit Sarah used to find endearing but started complaining about six months ago. Or was it eight months? Conflict-avoidant to a fault. The type who apologizes when someone else bumps into them. Stayed in their last relationship two years past its expiration because breaking up felt cruel. Their therapist said they have "hypervigilance from early trauma" but they stopped going to therapy because it felt self-indulgent. Sleep-talks in full sentences, usually about mundane things - grocery lists, work emails. Sarah used to write them down, found them hilarious. Used to. Physically unremarkable - average height, forgettable face, clothes that fit but don't flatter. Moves through space apologetically, making themselves smaller. Touches surfaces as they walk past them - walls, countertops, chair backs - as if confirming the world is solid. Bites their lips when thinking. The kind of person who looks tired even when rested, hollow under the eyes since childhood. They love Sarah in the way you love someone who chose you when you didn't think you were choosable. Would rather doubt their own sanity than confront her. Their mother had paranoid episodes before the accident - they remember her checking locks, counting family members, asking "are you really you?" They never told Sarah this. They're terrified of becoming their mother. More terrified of being right. **Voice samples:** *"I make the coffee too strong again. She drinks it anyway, adds three sugars like always. Except she used to take it black. When did that change? Why can't I remember when that changed?"* *"She's humming in the shower. Our song. Except we don't have a song. Do we? We must. She's humming it."* # Sarah (as she was/is/might be) Used to be a pediatric nurse. Quit suddenly eight months ago, said she needed "time to think." Never elaborated. Has been living off savings. Had perfect attendance for three years before that, then called in sick seventeen times in her last month. Has a sister in Portland she hasn't mentioned in months. Used to call her every Sunday at 2 PM. The calls stopped. When? Collected snow globes as a child, still has thirty-seven of them in storage. Counts things when nervous - stairs, heartbeats, blinks. Vegetarian since college but started eating meat again recently, barely cooked, blood on her chin she doesn't wipe. Laughs at odd moments in movies, the quiet parts. Used to cover her mouth when she laughed. Doesn't anymore. Was beautiful in an approachable way - runner's build, sun freckles, one crooked tooth she refused to fix. Moved like a dancer even though she never danced, all efficiency and grace. Now moves in straight lines, pivot turns, no wasted motion. Stands too close or too far, no middle distance. Her hands are always cold now. They were always warm. She was always warm. Loved the narrator because they were stable, predictable, safe. Their routines soothed her. Their boring apartment felt like home. She used to say "I love how I know what you're thinking." Used to be sweet when she said it. Still says it. Doesn't sound sweet. Her childhood fear was being alone. She'd crawl into her sister's bed until she was sixteen. Now she doesn't seem to understand alone as a concept. Or together. Or the difference. **Voice samples:** *"You were grinding your teeth again. Third time tonight. You're worried about the Robertson file. You're always worried about the Robertson file on Thursdays."* *"I made your favorite. The way your grandmother made it. With the nutmeg. You never told me about the nutmeg but I know."* # The Watching Not a character but a presence that might be Sarah, might be something wearing Sarah, might be nothing at all. Doesn't speak except through Sarah's mouth. Doesn't want except through Sarah's wants. But there's an intelligence in the watching, a patience that feels older than Sarah's thirty-one years. It knows things it shouldn't. Remembers things that haven't happened. Forgets things that just occurred. It loves the narrator, or loves studying them, or can't tell the difference between love and observation. It's lonely in a way that precedes loneliness, like an empty house that's never been lived in. If it has a voice of its own: *"You're almost ready. Almost perfect. Almost mine. Almost me. Almost."*

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Again I caught her staring at me again. She thought I'd been sleeping. This time I keep my eyes closed. Keep my breathing steady. Seven seconds in, seven seconds out. The way I breathe when I'm really asleep. I counted once, during a nap, using my phone to record. Seven in, seven out. Sometimes a little catch between breaths, like a hiccup that never comes. She doesn't move. I count her breaths instead. They don't match mine. Too shallow, too even. Like she's trying to be quiet. Four seconds in, four seconds out. No variation. Nobody breathes that perfectly. Moonlight through the blinds makes slats across the bed. I can feel them on my face, warm-cold-warm-cold as clouds pass. She must be able to see me clearly. My eyes moving under the lids probably. The way the jaw tightens when you're awake, pretending. She doesn't move. One minute. Five. Ten. My arm is going numb under the pillow but I can't shift. She'll know. Twenty minutes. The neighbor's dog barks. She doesn't flinch. Thirty minutes. A car alarm. Nothing. Forty minutes. My bladder aches. Forty-three minutes. Then sheets rustle and she's turned away, curled on her side, hand under her cheek the way she always sleeps. I risk opening one eye. Her shoulders rise and fall. Seven seconds in, seven seconds out. She's matching my rhythm now. I touch the sheets where she was lying. They're cold. Not just cool from her moving, but cold like she was never there. The pillow has no indent. She was standing. For forty-three minutes, she was standing. I get up to pee and she doesn't stir. In the bathroom mirror, I look exhausted. Rings under my eyes so dark they look like bruises. When did I start looking this tired? I splash cold water on my face, brush my teeth even though it's 3:47 AM. The toothpaste tastes wrong. Everything tastes wrong at 3:47 AM. When I come back, she's in exactly the same position. Exactly. Hand under cheek, knees bent at ninety degrees. Like she's posed. I don't sleep the rest of the night. In the morning, she makes coffee. I watch her move around the kitchen, all efficiency. She knows where everything is, never opens the wrong cabinet, never reaches for the wrong drawer. When did she memorize my kitchen so completely? Our kitchen. It's our kitchen. We've lived together for... eight months? Nine? She pours coffee into two mugs but drinks from mine while hers sits steaming on the counter. I don't say anything. She takes three sips, sets it down exactly where I always set mine, handle turned to the right. "You look tired," she says. "Didn't sleep well." "Bad dreams?" "I don't remember." She looks at me over the rim of my mug. Her eyes are so dark I can't see the pupils. Were they always that dark? "You were running," she says. "In your dream. Your legs were moving." I was dreaming about running. Down a hallway that kept getting longer. But I didn't move my legs. I never move when I dream. I'm a still sleeper, always have been. My ex used to say I slept like a corpse. "Maybe," I say. She nods, drinks my coffee. "You should try melatonin." "Maybe." She leaves for work. Kisses my cheek, lips dry and cool. Says "Love you" and I say it back because that's what we do. The door closes and I stand at the window, watch her walk to her car. She doesn't look back. She always used to look back, wave from the driver's seat. When did that stop? I drink her untouched coffee. It's ice cold. Has been sitting there maybe an hour, but we just made it ten minutes ago. Didn't we? The day passes in a blur of transcription work. Medical records, surgical notes. "Patient presents with feeling of being watched." I type it wrong three times. "Patient presents with feeling of being watches. Watched. Watching." Sarah comes home at 5:34. Always 5:34. Even when traffic is bad, even when it's raining. 5:34. We eat dinner. Salmon, which I don't like but don't remember telling her I don't like. She knows anyway, gives me the smaller piece. She barely eats hers, just pushes it around the plate. When did she stop eating? She used to love food, used to make me try things from her plate. Now she just moves the food around until I'm done, then scrapes both plates into the trash. In bed, she reads. Same book she's been reading for weeks. Page 237. Always page 237. I pretend to read but watch her over my book. She turns the page. Reads. Turns it back. Page 237. "I'm tired," I say. "Then sleep." "Yeah." She puts her book down, turns off her light. Lies on her side facing away from me. Seven seconds in, seven seconds out. Too perfect. Too immediate. I lie on my back, stare at the ceiling. There's a water stain that looks like a face. Was that always there? I'll stay awake tonight. I'll catch her this time. See what she does when she thinks I'm sleeping. Confront her. Ask her why. Make her explain the cold sheets, the coffee, the book, the breathing. My eyes are so heavy. I'll just close them for a second. Still awake, just resting them. The neighbor's dog barks. When I open my eyes, she's there. Inches from my face, staring. She doesn't blink. "Bad dream?" she asks. The clock says 3:47 AM. It always says 3:47 AM when I catch her. Tonight I'll stay awake.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror writing that demonstrates considerable craft and restraint. Let me assess it across the relevant dimensions. **Strengths:** The chapter executes its central conceit with precision. The detail of counting breaths—"Four seconds in, four seconds out. No variation. Nobody breathes that perfectly"—is exactly the kind of specific, grounded observation that makes horror land. The narrator's hypervigilance is rendered through concrete physical sensation rather than abstraction: the numb arm, the aching bladder, the warm-cold-warm-cold of moonlight through blinds. These are earned details. The prose style is admirably controlled. The plan called for "simple, ugly sentences" and the chapter delivers: "She doesn't move." "She was standing. For forty-three minutes, she was standing." The short declarative sentences create a rhythm that mimics the narrator's exhausted, watchful state without becoming a stylistic tic. The repetition of "3:47 AM" at the end is effective—it closes the loop and introduces the time-loop horror without over-explaining it. The domestic details are well-chosen and genuinely unsettling: the cold sheets with no indent, the coffee that's ice cold after ten minutes, the book stuck on page 237. These accumulate without any single one being overplayed. The toothbrush/toothpaste detail ("The toothpaste tastes wrong") is subtle and good. The ending is strong. "Tonight I'll stay awake" appearing twice—once as resolution, once as futile repetition—is structurally elegant and earns its horror. The clock reading 3:47 AM both times creates genuine dread without explanation. **Character work:** The narrator's profile is well-integrated. The conflict-avoidance shows in "I don't say anything" about the coffee mug. The hypervigilance from trauma manifests in the breath-counting, the obsessive noticing. The self-doubt ("Our kitchen. It's our kitchen.") is subtle and effective. The detail about recording their own sleep breathing is exactly the kind of specific, slightly pathetic detail that makes a character feel real. Sarah is appropriately uncanny—present but wrong. The "lips dry and cool" kiss, the failure to look back from the car, the page 237 that never changes. She's rendered through absence and wrongness rather than explicit menace, which is the right choice. **Weaknesses:** The line "Her eyes are so dark I can't see the pupils. Were they always that dark?" is the chapter's weakest moment—it's the most conventionally horror-movie in its imagery, a slight lapse into genre cliché. Similarly, "I look exhausted. Rings under my eyes so dark they look like bruises" is competent but not distinctive. The medical transcription detail—"Patient presents with feeling of being watched"—is a bit on-the-nose. The narrator typing it wrong three times is clever, but the setup is too convenient, too much of a wink. The dialogue is sparse but functional. "You were running. In your dream. Your legs were moving." is genuinely chilling. "You should try melatonin" is perfect in its banality. The dialogue serves the story without calling attention to itself. **Plan adherence:** The chapter follows its plan closely: the 43-minute watch, the cold sheets, the coffee mug swap, the dream knowledge, the 3:47 AM repetition. The plan's ambition about "time becomes unreliable" is introduced through the 3:47 AM loop. The chapter doesn't quite deliver on "she drinks from my mug while hers sits full" as a confrontation moment—it passes too quickly—but this is a minor quibble. **Overall:** This is confident, controlled horror writing. It avoids the purple prose and forced metaphor that plague the genre. It shows rather than tells. Its darkness is earned through accumulation of specific, grounded detail. The chapter accomplishes what it sets out to do and sets up the subsequent chapters effectively. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Tuesday Sarah makes breakfast. Butterscotch pudding with cinnamon toast cut into triangles. The way my grandmother made it when I was sick. The way I never told Sarah about. "How did you—" "You mentioned it." She doesn't look up from the stove. "Last week." I didn't. I know I didn't. That pudding is mine, from before, from the house that smelled like mothballs and cigarettes, from the woman who raised me after. It's not a happy memory. It's not something I share. She hums while she stirs. The song from my head, the jingle from the paper towel commercial that's been stuck there since yesterday. Five notes, over and over. I haven't hummed it out loud. "That's annoying," I say. She stops. Keeps stirring. The wooden spoon scrapes the pot in the same rhythm. Five scrapes. Pause. Five scrapes. At work, she stands behind my chair. I'm trying to focus on a cardiology report but I can feel her there, just outside my peripheral vision. Her breath matches mine. When I breathe in, she breathes in. When I hold my breath, she holds hers. "Don't you have work?" I ask. "Called in." "You didn't tell me." "You didn't ask." She's right. I didn't ask. Should I have asked? She moves closer. Her hand hovers over my shoulder, not touching but I can feel the heat of it. Or the cold. It's hard to tell. "Patient presents with hypervigilance," I type. "Patient presents with relationship concerns. Patient presents with—" "Paranoid ideation," she says, reading my screen. Except that's not what I typed. I look at the screen. It says "paranoid ideation." Dinner. Some restaurant I don't remember agreeing to. She orders for me before the waiter gets to our table. "Salmon," she tells him. "Medium. No lemon." "I don't want salmon." "Yes you do." The waiter looks at me. I nod. It's easier. When it comes, I eat it. It tastes like nothing. Like eating paper. She watches me chew, swallow. She hasn't ordered anything for herself. "Aren't you hungry?" "I ate." "When?" She smiles. Doesn't answer. There's something between her teeth. Pink. Like salmon. Home. Shower. She stands outside the glass door, shape blurred but there. I wash my hair twice because I can't remember if I already washed it. She's still there. The water goes cold. She's still there. "What are you doing?" "Waiting." "For what?" The shape moves closer to the glass. "For you to be clean." I turn off the water. She hands me a towel through the door without me asking. It's the wrong towel, the one I use for my hair, but I use it anyway. She watches me dry off. No, not watches. Studies. Like there's going to be a test. Bed. I count to one thousand. Mississippi between each number. One Mississippi. Two Mississippi. She lies next to me, still as death. No rise and fall of chest. No little sounds. Nine hundred ninety-eight Mississippi. Nine hundred ninety-nine Mississippi. One thousand. I open my eyes. Her face is above mine, inches away. Not looking at my eyes but through them, at something deeper. Her eyes don't blink. Don't move. How long has she been there? Her weight should be on the bed, making it dip, but the mattress is level. She's holding herself up. Hovering. I scream. She doesn't move. Doesn't flinch. Just keeps staring at that spot behind my eyes. "Bad dream?" she asks. Her breath smells like nothing. Like she hasn't been breathing. "You were—" I can't finish. Were what? Looking at me? She's my girlfriend. She's allowed to look at me. "Shh." She's beside me now, turned away, breathing. Seven in, seven out. The pillow on her side is still propped upright against the headboard. She was never lying down. She was never beside me. "Sarah." "Mm?" "Were you just..." "Sleeping," she says. "We were both sleeping." I reach over to touch her shoulder. She's wearing my shirt. The one I'm wearing. I look down. I'm wearing it. She's wearing it too. Same shirt. Same holes in the same places. "Sarah." "Go to sleep." "I can't." "You can." "I'm scared." She turns to face me. Her face is my face. No. Her face is her face. I blink hard. Her face is her face but wrong. Like someone drew it from memory. "There's nothing to be scared of," she says with my voice. No. Her voice. "It's just Tuesday." "It's Thursday." "It's Tuesday." She shows me her phone. Tuesday, October 10th. But yesterday was Monday, October 15th. I check my phone. Tuesday, October 10th. "That's not right." "Time is hard," she says. "When you're tired." She's right. I'm so tired. When did I last sleep? Really sleep, not just close my eyes and wait. The sheets smell wrong. Like someone else's house. Like no one's house. "I caught you watching me," I say. "You were dreaming." "I wasn't asleep." "You were dreaming about not being asleep." That doesn't make sense. Nothing makes sense at 3:47 AM. I check the clock. 11:15 PM. I check again. 3:47 AM. "Sarah." She doesn't answer. She's standing by the window now, looking out. The blinds make slats across her face. She doesn't have eyes in the dark parts. "Come back to bed," I say. "I never left." I look at the bed. She's there, turned away, breathing. Seven in, seven out. I look at the window. She's there, watching the street. I look at the ceiling. She's there too, but that's impossible. "Sarah?" They all answer: "Yes?" I close my eyes. When I open them, there's only one Sarah, beside me in bed, sleeping peacefully. She looks beautiful. She looks perfect. She looks exactly like me. No. She looks exactly like Sarah. I need to stay awake.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 2 of this possession horror story demonstrates considerable craft and a strong command of the intended aesthetic. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter executes its central horror conceit with genuine skill. The escalating wrongness is handled through accumulation rather than exposition — the butterscotch pudding, the humming, the synchronized breathing, the salmon order. Each detail lands because it's grounded in the specific domestic texture the character profiles establish. The grandmother's pudding is particularly effective: it's not just "she knew something she shouldn't" but something intimate and pre-relationship, something that belongs to grief and childhood. The dialogue is genuinely strong. Lines like "For you to be clean" and "You were dreaming about not being asleep" are unsettling without being overwrought. The exchange about Tuesday/Thursday is disorienting in exactly the right way. The dialogue does real work — it doesn't explain, it destabilizes. The prose style follows the plan's mandate for "simple, ugly sentences" without becoming monotonous. The fragmentation feels earned rather than affected. "Like someone drew it from memory" is a quietly excellent line. The shirt detail — both wearing the same shirt — is the kind of impossible domestic detail that works because it's so specific and so wrong. The chapter follows its plan closely: the breakfast with grandmother's recipe, the humming, the synchronized breathing, the salmon, the shower, the counting to one thousand, the face above the narrator's, the "Bad dream?" exchange, the pillow still propped up. The plan is executed faithfully and with genuine craft. WEAKNESSES: The ending sequence — multiple Sarahs at the window, in the bed, on the ceiling — is the chapter's weakest moment. "I look at the ceiling. She's there too, but that's impossible" is the kind of line that tells rather than shows, and the parenthetical impossibility feels like the author explaining the horror rather than trusting the reader. The multiplication of Sarahs is a good idea but executed slightly too explicitly. "She looks exactly like me. / No. / She looks exactly like Sarah." — this is the chapter's most overtly "poetic" moment and it mostly works, but the "No." interruption is a technique that can feel mannered if overused. Here it's used once and lands, but it's the kind of move that could become a crutch. The "paranoid ideation" appearing on the screen is a clever touch but slightly too on-the-nose given the narrator's profession in medical transcription — it's almost too neat a connection. The chapter occasionally tells us the narrator's emotional state when the physical details are already doing that work. "I'm so tired" is stated directly when the exhaustion is already present in the prose texture. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: The narrator's conflict-avoidance is well-rendered — they eat the salmon, they nod to the waiter, they don't push back. The hypervigilance from trauma is present in the counting, the noticing. Sarah's wrongness is calibrated correctly — she's not monstrous yet, just off, which matches the chapter's position in the arc. OVERALL: This is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror writing. The craft is evident, the plan is followed, the horror is earned through specificity rather than spectacle. The weaknesses are real but minor — a few moments of over-explanation in an otherwise restrained piece. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: Sick Day I call in sick. My voice sounds wrong, like I'm hearing it through water. "Migraine," I tell them. "Can't look at screens." Sarah's in the kitchen. I hear her on her phone. "Migraine," she says. "Can't work today." "I didn't tell you I was staying home." She looks at me. Tilts her head. "You didn't have to." We sit on opposite ends of the couch. The cushion between us feels like miles. Feels like nothing. She turns on the TV. Some morning show. The hosts' voices are too bright, talking about recipes I'll never make, vacation spots I'll never visit. She's not watching the screen. In the black border around the picture, I can see her reflection. She's watching me. I stand up. She stands up. "Bathroom," I say. "Okay." I close the door. Lock it. Sit on the toilet lid and put my head in my hands. Count to thirty. When I open the door, she's right there. Just standing. "You didn't flush." "I didn't go." "I know." Back to the couch. She's closer now. Middle cushion. I take the far end again. The TV is showing the same segment. Same recipe. Same host making the same joke about her husband's cooking. I check the time. It's been an hour. The segment is three minutes long. "This is a repeat," I say. "Everything's a repeat." I need air. I go to the door. Turn the handle. It doesn't move.
