Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Would Approach This Piece A human writer approaching "They fucked with the wrong babysitter" would likely start from a place of **gleeful trashy fun**, not competent professionalism. They'd probably begin with an image that made them laugh - maybe the babysitter covered in blood while helping with math homework - and work backwards. They wouldn't worry about making Sarah "likeable" or giving her a noble reason for her skills. Maybe she's just a psycho who happens to be good with kids. Humans would write this **drunk on the premise**. They'd indulge in the violence, make it wet and gross and specific. They wouldn't sanitize it with "efficient tactical movements" but would write "she shoved his nose bone into his brain" or "his eyeball popped like a grape." They'd find comedy in horror, not through witty quips but through absurd juxtapositions. A human wouldn't feel obligated to explain shit. How does Sarah know how to kill? Who cares. Why is she babysitting? Money, probably. They'd trust the reader to fill gaps or not care about them. # Telltale LLM Approaches That Don't Serve This Piece ## The "Competence Porn" Trap LLMs love making characters professionally excellent. Sarah being "Former Marine Force Recon" is such an LLM move - it's the "correct" answer that explains everything neatly. A human might make her background messier: learned to fight in juvenile detention, daddy was a hitman, or never explain it at all. ## Emotional Scaffolding LLMs always build proper emotional journeys. "Kids are terrified but Sarah keeps them focused" - so measured, so appropriate. Humans would let kids piss themselves, say "fuck" for the first time, maybe one kid turns out to be a budding psychopath who enjoys it. ## The Explanation Addiction "Sarah learns about the hard drive through brief, brutal interrogation" - LLMs cannot resist explaining how information transfers. A human might just jump cut: guy's dying, next scene Sarah knows about the drive. Let readers connect dots. ## Moral Safety Rails Even in my "dark" story, Sarah protects the kids' father, destroys evidence of his crimes. She's still fundamentally good. An LLM can't help making her heroic. A human might have her blackmail the father, or steal the crypto herself, or not give a shit about anyone. ## The Clean Violence Problem "She kills first intruder with kitchen knife and hand-to-hand combat" - so clinical. LLMs write violence like technical manuals. Humans write "she opened him up like a fish and he tried to hold his guts in but they kept slipping through his fingers like raw sausage." ## Orchestral Pacing My 8-chapter structure is so balanced: 2 setup, 2 escalation, 2 climax, 2 resolution. Real stories are lopsided. Maybe 5 chapters of normal babysitting with creeping dread, then 3 chapters of absolute mayhem. Or 1 normal chapter then 7 of pure chaos. # Deep Dive Critique of My Plan ## The Setup Is Too Clean Starting at 8 PM and ending at 4 AM? Time stamps? Chapter titles? This screams LLM organization. A human would start in medias res with Sarah stepping over a body to check if Jackson brushed his teeth, or start with mundane babysitting and never mention what time it is. ## Sarah Is Too Perfect "Former Marine Force Recon operator now working as a babysitter while attending nursing school" - I'm explaining too much and making her too noble. She's skilled but going to nursing school! She's helpful! A human would make her a fuck-up. Maybe she's babysitting because she can't hold a real job. Maybe she learned to fight in prison. Maybe she's naturally violent and babysitting is the only thing that calms her. ## The Kids Are Too Standard Mia: artistic teenage cynicism. Jackson: video game obsession. These are placeholder kids. Real kids are weird. Maybe Jackson collects roadkill. Maybe Mia deals Adderall. Maybe they're both deeply strange and that's why they need a babysitter at 13 and 11. ## The Villains Are Too Logical Professional criminals seeking cryptocurrency? Corrupt cops? This is so organized. Humans would make it messier - maybe they're looking for something stupid, like embarrassing photos. Maybe they're at the wrong house. Maybe Vincent is the dad's secret boyfriend. ## The Action Is Too Strategic Each chapter has clear action beats building to resolution. Real violence is chaotic. Maybe Sarah wins through pure luck. Maybe she's terrible at fighting but keeps getting up. Maybe the kids do most of the killing while she cowers. ## The Ending Is Too Neat Sarah gives a statement, protects the father, disappears into morning commuters, considers one more job. Every thread tied. A human would end mid-sentence during the violence, or with Sarah arrested, or just her eating cereal while bodies cool around her. # Brainstorming More Human Approaches ## Voice/Tone Ideas - Write it like Sarah's telling the story to her cellmate years later - Stream of consciousness during the violence - fragmented, repetitive - Total deadpan - describe horrific violence like recipe instructions - Child's POV instead - misunderstanding the violence - Found footage feel - security cameras, 911 transcripts, text messages - Write it horny - Sarah's turned on by violence, inappropriate tension - Comedy of errors - everything goes wrong for everyone ## Character Mess - Sarah's actually a terrible babysitter - kids hate her, she's mean - She's drunk/high the whole time - The kids are more violent than her - She's babysitting her dealer's kids for drug debt - She has undiagnosed brain damage that makes her incredibly violent - The parents hired her BECAUSE she's violent - She's sleeping with the dad and this is revenge from the mom ## Plot Chaos - No explanation for anything - pure experience - The violence is completely disproportionate from page one - It's not even her - she's house-sitting, these aren't her kids - Multiple unrelated groups attack the same night - The kids orchestrated everything - Sarah's hallucinating everything while having a breakdown - The "attackers" are actually cops/EMTs trying to help ## Structural Rebellion - One 8000-word run-on sentence - Each chapter is shorter - 8 chapters but only 2000 words total - Chapters get progressively more incoherent - Write chapter 8 first, work backwards - Same events from 8 different perspectives - Just dialogue, no description - Just description, no dialogue ## Ending Alternatives - Sarah dies on page 3, kids handle the rest - Everyone dies, story continues anyway - Stops mid-fight, never resolves - Flash forward 20 years - Jackson's in therapy - The cops arrest Sarah, she goes to prison happy - Turns out this happens every Friday - The babysitter agency knew this would happen ## Language Choices - Never use Sarah's name - just "the babysitter" - Never describe the violence directly - just sounds and aftermath - Write it like a children's book with horrible content - Use recipes/instruction manual format - Corporate memo style - Yelp reviews of the babysitting service - Court transcript The human approach would commit to a wild choice and ride it into the ground, not try to balance everything perfectly. It would trust mess over meaning, sensation over sense.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention Fuck the backstory. Sarah's just a babysitter who's good at killing people. No explanation. She's 26, babysits for drug money, and has something deeply wrong with her that makes violence feel like scratching an itch. Not evil, not good - just off. **Voice:** First person present, but disconnected. Sarah narrates like she's describing someone else's life. Short sentences. No internal monologue about feelings. She describes opening a man's throat the same way she describes making mac and cheese. Violence happens mid-sentence without announcement. No quips, no sass - just flat observation and terrible things happening. **Structure:** Radically lopsided. Five chapters of almost normal babysitting with something rotting underneath. Then three chapters of wet, specific violence that doesn't stop. No buildup, no warning - just sudden catastrophic brutality that keeps going. **What I'm NOT doing:** Explaining Sarah's skills. Making her likeable. Protecting anyone's feelings. Writing around the violence. Creating sympathy. Building to revelations. Tying up loose ends. Making the kids brave or helpful. **What I AM doing:** Making readers uncomfortable. Writing violence that feels wrong. Letting banality and horror exist in the same sentence. Making Sarah someone you wouldn't leave your kids with even before the killing starts. Ending mid-action because real violence doesn't have closure. **The kids:** Theo (12) collects teeth - not just his. Ruby (9) hasn't spoken in two years by choice. They're the Marsh kids, and their parents pay triple because no one else will watch them. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: "Friday" Sarah shows up at the Marsh house at 6 PM eating gas station sushi. Mrs. Marsh is wine-drunk, Mr. Marsh reeks of secretary perfume. They're going to marriage counseling (they're lying - separate hotels). Ruby's drawing pictures of car accidents in remarkable detail. Theo shows Sarah his teeth collection, including one that's "still warm" from a playground fight. Sarah doesn't react to any of this. She makes fish sticks, puts on Cartoon Network. Notices the house has too many locks for a normal family. Finds a man's wallet in the kitchen drawer that doesn't belong to Mr. Marsh. The photo ID is torn out. She pockets $300 from it, puts it back. Normal Friday. ## Chapter 2: "Bedtime" Sarah reads Ruby a story about rabbits while the girl draws dismemberments. Theo wants to show Sarah something in the basement - it's a freeze-dried cat he's been working on. Sarah helps him adjust the chemicals. They hear footsteps upstairs. Sarah doesn't mention it. Tucks Theo in, he asks if she's ever killed anyone. She says yes. He asks what it felt like. She says "wet." He seems satisfied. Ruby's already in bed, pretending to sleep, drawing under her covers. Sarah goes downstairs. Makes popcorn. Three men are in the living room going through drawers. She watches them from the kitchen, eating popcorn. They haven't noticed her yet. ## Chapter 3: "Introductions" Sarah walks into living room with the popcorn bowl. Sits on couch. Men freeze. She turns on TV, flips channels. One man starts to speak. She shushes him - her show's on. They look at each other. The leader, Marcus, tries to explain they're looking for something Mr. Marsh stole. Sarah doesn't care. Says the kids are sleeping. Marcus puts a gun to her head. She doesn't stop eating popcorn. Tells him Ruby's a light sleeper. He sends one man upstairs to check on kids. Sarah mentions the stairs creak on the seventh and twelfth step. The man avoids them. She hears him open Ruby's door. A wet sound. He doesn't come back down. Marcus sends the second man. Same thing. Marcus is sweating now. Sarah changes the channel. ## Chapter 4: "Commercial Break" Marcus wants to leave. Sarah says he can't, the kids are probably awake now. He goes upstairs himself, gun drawn. Sarah follows, still eating popcorn. First man is in Ruby's room - she's drawing his portrait. He's sitting very still because there are scissors in his neck. Second man is in the bathroom. The toilet's overflowing with red. Theo's in there brushing his teeth with the man's toothbrush. Marcus backs toward the stairs. Sarah mentions the loose bannister. He grabs it, it gives way, he falls. Lands wrong. Leg bone comes through his pants like a white flag. Sarah steps over him, checks on Ruby. The girl shows her the portrait. It's quite good. ## Chapter 5: "Cleanup" Sarah drags Marcus to the kitchen while he screams. Ruby and Theo follow in their pajamas. Sarah starts teaching them how to properly joint a body - like chicken, she says. Theo takes notes. Ruby draws. Marcus begs, offers money, says more men are coming. Sarah gags him with a dish towel and continues the lesson. Shows Theo where tendons connect, how joints separate if you twist right. Ruby's drawings are getting more accurate. Marcus passes out. Wakes up. Passes out again. Sarah makes hot chocolate with marshmallows. They work until midnight. The kitchen is a disaster. Someone knocks on the front door. ## Chapter 6: "Company" Four more men. They brought a police detective. Sarah answers the door in an apron covered in handprints. Invites them in for coffee. They see the kitchen. One vomits. The detective draws his gun, hands shaking. Ruby comes downstairs dragging something heavy. It's wrapped in her Frozen bedsheet. Theo follows with a bucket of his new teeth. The men open fire. Sarah falls behind the kitchen island with the kids. Bullets destroy the cabinets. Sugar and flour explode into clouds. Sarah crawls to the knife drawer. No hurry. Theo asks if they can keep the detective's teeth. She says yes. Ruby draws on the floor with spilled maple syrup. Sarah starts with the one who vomited - he's reloading. Opens him like a zipper from balls to throat. Uses him as a shield. Takes his gun. Shoots two others in the face, misses the third. He runs. Detective trying to call backup. His phone's slippery. Sarah breaks his arm with a pasta pot. Asks Ruby to draw her something nice while she finishes. ## Chapter 7: "The Runner" Last man made it to his car. Sarah follows barefoot, still in the apron. He's fumbling with keys. She gets in the passenger side. He screams. Drives anyway, trying to escape. She lets him drive three blocks, then grabs the wheel. They hit a parked car. Airbags deploy. His nose breaks. Sarah's fine. Drags him back to the house by his hair. Neighbors' lights are on but no one comes out. Ruby and Theo are in the living room watching cartoons. Detective's still alive, crawling toward door with his good arm. Theo's following him with pliers, taking teeth as he goes. Ruby's drawing the whole scene. Sarah puts the runner in Mr. Marsh's office. Comes back with a nail gun. He offers her everything - money, drugs, connections. She nails his hands to the desk. Starts asking about why they came. He talks. She doesn't care, keeps nailing. Runs out of nails. Gets more from garage. ## Chapter 8: "Saturday Morning" 6 AM. Sarah's making pancakes. Bodies still everywhere. Ruby's portfolio is complete - thirty-seven drawings of the night. Theo's categorizing teeth by owner. Marsh parents call - they're extending their trip another day. Sarah says that's fine, triple rate for weekends. Mrs. Marsh agrees. Detective's phone won't stop ringing. Sarah answers, tells them he's tied up. Hangs up. Runner bled out around 4 AM. The one in the toilet upstairs is starting to smell. Sarah serves pancakes with strawberries. Ruby draws Sarah's portrait - gets the nothing behind her eyes just right. Theo asks if she'll babysit next Friday. She checks her phone. Has three texts from other families. The Marshes must have left a good review. Says yes to all of them. Ruby tugs her sleeve, shows her a new drawing. It's Sarah at their house next Friday. There are more bodies. Different ones. Sarah pets Ruby's head. Tells her that's a pretty picture. Syrup drips onto the detective's face. He's
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Sarah Sarah's 26 and has babysat since she was 14. Started because she liked being in other people's houses when they weren't there. Still does. She reads their mail, tries on their clothes, uses their expensive face creams. Takes small things they won't miss - single earrings, old phones, one fork from a set. Has a storage unit full of other people's things organized by family. She eats constantly but only gas station food - beef jerky, those sweaty roller hot dogs, energy drinks that taste like cough syrup. Chews with her mouth open. Picks her teeth with her car keys. Has never cooked anything that didn't come from a box. Feeds kids cereal for dinner if the parents don't leave specific instructions. Five-foot-four, soft-looking. Doughy arms, bitten nails, usually wearing the same Ohio State hoodie she's had since high school (she didn't go there). Moves like she's underwater - slow, deliberate, no wasted motion. Never hurries. Smells like cherry car air freshener because she hangs them from her belt loops. When she smiles, only her mouth moves. She doesn't have friends, just people whose kids she watches. Sleeps with some of the dads because it's something to do. They always cry after. She has a mother somewhere who calls monthly asking for money. Sarah sends it. Not from love - just to stop the calling. Lives in a studio apartment with no furniture except a mattress and a TV on the floor. Watches infomercials until 4 AM every night. Her only allegiance is to routine. Kids should be in bed by 9. Dishes should be done. She'll kill anyone who disrupts this. **Dialogue samples:** "Ruby drew you pretty good. Got the way your eyes went different directions at the end." "Your son needs more calcium. His teeth came out too easy." (Internal: *The detective's crying again. Like that helps. Like anything helps. His teeth are probably weak too.*) # Theo Marsh Twelve, but small. Looks nine. Uses this - plays younger to make people uncomfortable when he says things like "I watched our dog die and counted how long her legs kicked." Started collecting teeth at six when his fell out and his parents said the tooth fairy wasn't real. Felt betrayed. Decided teeth were currency for something else, something true. Keeps them in labeled baby food jars: "Connor P, playground, left incisor" or "Unknown woman, parking lot, wisdom." Soaks them in hydrogen peroxide until they're white. Arranges them by size, not chronologically. Has 237 teeth. Knows because he counts them every night. Skinny, sharp elbows, hair that won't lay flat. Wears the same green cargo pants every day - needs the pockets for collecting supplies: pliers, plastic bags, hydrogen peroxide travel bottles. Breathes through his mouth. Always sounds congested. Stands too close to people, studying their mouths when they talk. Loves his sister Ruby more than anything. She's the only person whose teeth he'd never take. Parents disgust him - their weakness, their lies, their fear of him. But they leave him alone now, which is all he wanted. Sarah's the first babysitter who didn't try to fix him or fear him. He wants her to stay forever. **Dialogue samples:** "Sarah, if I use the pliers at the root, they come out whole. But if I twist, they break. Which way is better?" "The detective has a gold one. Back left. Can I have it while he's still using it?" "Mom says I'm disturbed. But she takes pills to sleep and drinks wine at breakfast, so who's disturbed?" # Ruby Marsh Nine. Stopped talking two years ago after she saw something in their old house's basement. Not trauma - just decided words were inefficient. Draws instead. Goes through two sketchbooks a week. Her parents buy them in bulk. Draws what she sees, but more honest. Mom becomes a wine bottle with arms. Dad becomes a penis in a suit. Theo becomes a beautiful collection of bones held together with love. Sarah becomes a hole in the shape of a person. Small, even for nine. Wears only dresses - the same three in rotation. Pink, yellow, pink, yellow, pink, yellow, blue (for special occasions). Hair in two braids she does herself. One's always higher. Has her mother's face but empty of her mother's anxiety. Moves in straight lines - never arcs around furniture, just stops, turns ninety degrees, continues. Loves Theo but knows he's fragile. Knows she's the strong one. Stopped talking partly to give him something - being the weird one, the one parents worry about. It worked. They barely see her now. Perfect. Understands Sarah immediately. Recognizes the nothing inside her because Ruby has it too, just quieter. Draws Sarah pictures because Sarah's the only one who sees them honestly - as records, not art. **Internal monologue samples:** *(The man's neck opens like a mouth. Should draw this. Need more red.)* *(Sarah doesn't pretend my pictures mean something. Doesn't say "how creative" or "concerning." Just nods. Says "that's accurate.")* *(Theo's happy. His jar's almost full. Good. Tuesday was hard for him.)* # Marcus Thirty-eight. Thinks he's smart because he reads books about business strategy. Quotes Sun Tzu incorrectly. Has "FEARLESS" tattooed on his neck but brought three guys to rob one family. Tells people he's in "acquisitions." Still lives in the apartment he shared with his ex-girlfriend. She left two years ago but her shampoo's still in the shower. He smells it sometimes. Joined a gym, goes twice a month, mostly sits in the sauna reading his phone. Wears a gold chain his mother gave him. Tells people it's worth ten grand. It's worth three hundred. Six-foot-one but walks like he's six-four. Shoulders back, chin up, trying. Always trying. Gel in his hair even for home invasions. Leather jacket in summer. Sniffs constantly - deviated septum from a fight he says he won but didn't. Came for Mr. Marsh's safe. Marsh owes his boss forty grand from a poker game. Marcus thinks this job will move him up, make him management. Has a five-year plan. Won't make it five more hours. **Dialogue samples:** "Listen, sweetheart, nobody needs to get hurt. This is just business, you understand? Business." "The fuck you mean she's just sitting there? Eating? Move her, then! Christ, do I have to do everything?" "Stop looking at me, kid. Your sister, she's - stop drawing that. Stop. Please." # The Marsh Parents **David Marsh:** Forty-five, software sales, peaked at thirty-two. Has erectile dysfunction but blames his wife. Owes money to people who collect fingers. Thinks he's smarter than them. He's not. Keeps a gun in a safe he forgets the combination to. Joined a fight club once, cried when he got hit. Pays Sarah triple because she doesn't ask questions about the bruises or why the police sometimes drive by. Tall, was handsome, going soft. Wears college football shirts from a team he never played for. Speaks in corporate buzzwords at home: "Let's circle back on dinner options." **Monica Marsh:** Forty-one, interior designer who hasn't had a client in three years. Day-drinks. Night-drinks. Shower-drinks. Hates David but hates dating more. Hates the kids but loves the idea of them. Posts family photos on Instagram with captions about being "blessed." Was beautiful, now just expensive-looking. Botox, fillers, veneers. Wears yoga clothes, doesn't do yoga. Smells like white wine and desperation. They haven't fucked in two years. Both having affairs with people who also disgust them. Stay together because divorce would mean admitting failure. **Their dialogue:** David: "Sarah! Thank god. Monica's having one of her... days. There's pizza money on the counter. We'll be back late. Or tomorrow. Whatever works." Monica: "Theo's been... difficult. And Ruby still won't - you know. But you're so good with them! We're so lucky we found you!" (To each other in the car): "Don't touch me." / "I wasn't going to."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Friday Mrs. Marsh opens the door before I knock. She's holding a wine glass with no wine in it. There's a red ring around the inside like a bathtub stain. "Sarah! Thank god." I'm eating gas station sushi. The rice is crunchy. Mr. Marsh appears behind her, buttoning his shirt wrong. Third button in the fourth hole. His neck has scratch marks that aren't from his wife. His wife's nails are bitten down to the quick. "We're going to counseling," Mrs. Marsh says. "Marriage counseling." "Couples therapy," Mr. Marsh corrects. They look at each other like dogs about to fight. The house smells like Febreze and fear sweat. I step inside. Theo's at the kitchen table sorting something in jars. Ruby's on the floor drawing. She doesn't look up. "There's pizza money on the counter," Mr. Marsh says. "We'll be back late." "Or tomorrow," Mrs. Marsh adds. "Whatever works." They leave in separate cars. Mrs. Marsh backs into the mailbox. Mr. Marsh pretends not to notice. I finish my sushi. The wasabi tastes like toothpaste. Theo comes over with a jar. "Got a new one." He shows me a molar with a silver filling. "Found it at school. Someone lost it on the basketball court." "Still has blood on it." "Yeah." He's proud. "Want to see the collection?" I follow him to his room. He has one of those beds shaped like a race car. The shelves above it hold baby food jars. Each one has teeth. Each jar has a label. "Mom, age 41, wisdom." "Unknown child, park, incisor." "Theo, age 7, canine." "Two hundred thirty-seven," he says. "Counted this morning." "You take your mom's?" "She was drunk. Didn't notice." Ruby appears in the doorway. Shows me her sketchbook. It's a drawing of a car accident. The details are accurate. Glass in the right places. Blood pooling where it would pool. Bodies bent at probable angles. "That's good," I tell her. She flips the page. Shows me another. This one's of a woman falling down stairs. The woman looks like Mrs. Marsh. The stairs look like their stairs. "Haven't happened yet," Theo says. "She draws things before sometimes." Ruby nods. Goes back downstairs. I make fish sticks. The instructions say 425 degrees for eighteen minutes. I do 450 for fifteen because I'm impatient. Theo eats his with ketchup. Ruby eats hers plain. We watch Cartoon Network. It's the old ones from when I was little. Tom hits Jerry with a hammer. Jerry's head becomes hammer-shaped. Nobody laughs. During commercials I walk around. The house has too many locks. Deadbolt, chain, sliding bolt, another deadbolt. The windows have locks too. Some have two. The basement door has four. "Dad's scared," Theo says. "Of the men who come sometimes." "When do they come?" "Thursdays usually. Sometimes Fridays." It's Friday. In the kitchen I open drawers. Normal stuff. Scissors, tape, pens that don't work. The third drawer has a wallet. Not Mr. Marsh's - his was in his pocket. This one's leather, expensive. There's $500 in it. No credit cards. The ID window is empty but there's a torn edge where a driver's license was. I take $300. Put the wallet back. Ruby brings me another drawing. It's me standing in the kitchen. Behind me are three men. I'm eating something. They have guns. "When?" I ask. She holds up nine fingers. Then eight. Then seven. Counts down. Nine-eight-seven. Tonight. "Okay." She goes back to drawing. I make popcorn. The microwave kind. Butter flavor. The kernels pop like tiny gunshots. I eat it while watching the locks. Wondering which one won't work. Theo comes down in his pajamas. They have dinosaurs on them. "Sarah, have you ever killed anyone?" "Yes." "What did it feel like?" "Wet." He thinks about this. "Was it hard?" "No." "Would you do it again?" "Probably." He smiles. It's the first time I've seen him smile. His teeth are very white. He takes good care of them. "I might want to do it someday," he says. "For teeth." "That's a reason." He goes to bed. Ruby's still drawing. I look over her shoulder. She's drawing the living room. In the picture, there are men going through drawers. I'm on the couch watching TV. One man's head is turned backward. Another is on the floor. Red crayon everywhere. She shows me her red crayon. It's almost gone. "I'll get you more," I tell her. She pats my hand. Her fingers are cold. At eight-thirty I put her to bed. She takes her sketchbook with her. Draws under the covers with a flashlight. The scratching of her pencil sounds like mice in the walls. I go back downstairs. Make more popcorn. Check the locks again. The sliding bolt on the back door is loose. Three screws missing. That'll be the one. I sit on the couch. Find a movie about sharks. The sharks look fake. The blood looks real. I eat popcorn and wait. The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of pulp horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Style:** The flat, dissociated first-person present tense is executed with real discipline. "The wasabi tastes like toothpaste" sits next to "Theo comes over with a jar" with no tonal shift, and that's exactly the point. The voice is consistent throughout - Sarah narrates violence and domesticity with identical affect. Short sentences. No editorializing. This is hard to sustain and the model does it well. **Character Work:** Sarah is rendered effectively through behavior rather than description. She takes $300 from a stranger's wallet without comment. She answers Theo's question about killing with "Wet." She doesn't react to Ruby's prophetic drawings with anything beyond "Okay." The character profile's instruction that she's "off" rather than evil comes through. Theo's tooth obsession and his smile at Sarah's answer about killing is genuinely unsettling. Ruby's prophetic drawings and cold fingers land well. The Marsh parents' brief appearance - separate cars, wrong buttons, scratch marks - is economical and effective. **The Dialogue:** The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. "What did it feel like?" / "Wet." is a perfect exchange. The Marsh parents' dialogue mirrors the profile samples closely and efficiently establishes their dysfunction. Nothing feels expository or forced. **Pacing and Structure:** The chapter builds dread through accumulation rather than announcement. The wallet, the locks, Ruby's countdown, the loose bolt - these are planted without fanfare. The ending with "They look like teeth" is the one moment that feels slightly too deliberate, a bit of a wink at the reader, but it's minor. **Potential Issues:** The line "The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the chapter's weakest moment - it's the one place where the model reaches for a poetic connection that feels slightly forced given the otherwise resolutely flat prose style. It's a small lapse. The "popping like tiny gunshots" for popcorn is similarly a bit on-the-nose given the context, though it's brief enough not to damage the piece. **Faithfulness to Plan:** The chapter follows the plan closely - gas station sushi, wrong buttons, scratch marks, separate cars, teeth collection, Ruby's drawings, fish sticks, Cartoon Network, too many locks, the wallet with torn ID, $300 taken, Ruby's countdown. The plan called for Ruby to show a drawing of the men coming - this is executed and expanded effectively. The chapter ends with Sarah waiting rather than the men arriving, which is a good instinct - it preserves the cliffhanger. **Creativity:** The prophetic drawings as a device for Ruby is excellent. The detail of Theo's labeled jars ("Mom, age 41, wisdom") is genuinely disturbing and specific. The loose bolt with three missing screws as the vulnerability Sarah identifies is a nice procedural touch. **Overall:** This is confident, controlled pulp horror writing. The flat affect is maintained, the characters are distinct and consistent, the dread accumulates without announcement. The few moments of reaching for metaphor are minor lapses in an otherwise disciplined performance. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 2 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Bedtime Nine o'clock. Time for bed. That's the rule. Ruby wants a story. I read her *Goodnight Moon*. She draws while I read. A bunny's head separated from its body. The mittens on fire. The old lady whispering "hush" with no lower jaw. "Goodnight nobody," I read. She draws nobody. It looks like me. Theo calls from the basement. "Sarah, come see." The basement smells like chemicals and death. Theo has a workbench set up. There's a cat on it. Was a cat. Now it's something else. Freeze-dried, he explains. Like astronaut food. "Found it on Maple Street. Already dead. Been working on it for three weeks." The cat's eyes are gone. Replaced with marbles. Green ones. "The chemicals need adjusting," he says. "It's too brittle. Watch." He snaps off an ear. It breaks like a potato chip. I help him mix new solution. Formaldehyde from a bottle marked "Dad's - DO NOT TOUCH." Salt. Something else that makes my eyes water. He measures carefully. Writes everything in a notebook. "I want to do Ruby's hamster next," he says. "When it dies." "It's not sick." "No. But hamsters only live two years. Mr. Whiskers is eighteen months old." Something creaks upstairs. Footsteps. Not Ruby's - she walks on her toes. These are heavy. Theo looks up. "Dad?" "Your dad wears sneakers. Those are boots." "Oh." He goes back to measuring chemicals. "Should we do something?" "Finish your project." We work on the cat for twenty more minutes. The new solution makes its fur look almost alive. Theo's happy. He puts it in a box labeled "Mr. Mittens 2.0." More footsteps upstairs. Multiple sets now. "Time for bed," I tell him. We go up. Three men in the living room. They're opening drawers, looking under cushions. One has a flashlight. Another has a garbage bag. They're being quiet. Considerate. Theo heads to his room. Stops at the top of the stairs. "Sarah, when I die, would you freeze-dry me?" "If you want." "I do. But fix my hair first. It never does what I want." "Okay." He goes to bed. I check on Ruby. She's under her covers, flashlight making a tent of light. Still drawing. Her red crayon's just a nub now. The men downstairs are getting frustrated. Can hear them whisper-arguing. Something about a safe. About Mr. Marsh owing forty thousand. About cutting losses. I go to the kitchen. Make popcorn. Third bag tonight. The microwave beeps four times when it's done. The men freeze. Then continue searching, faster now. I pour the popcorn into a bowl. Add salt. Lots of it. Walk into the living room. Sit on the couch. The men stop. One has tear tattoos. Prison tattoos, not the decorative kind. Another's younger, nervous, keeps touching his gun. The third is in charge. Gold chain, leather jacket even though it's warm. He's holding Mr. Marsh's mail. "The fuck," the nervous one says. I turn on the TV. Flip through channels. News. Commercial. Reality show about cakes. Stop on a nature documentary. Hyenas eating something while it's still alive. "Perfect," I say. The leader, Marcus, steps forward. "Listen, we don't want trouble." I shush him. Point at the TV. "This is the good part." The hyenas are really going at it now. The thing they're eating is trying to crawl away. Can't. Too many pieces missing. Marcus looks at his men. They look at me. I eat popcorn. "We're looking for something," Marcus tries again. "Something Mr. Marsh took." "Third drawer in the kitchen. Wallet with five hundred dollars. Already took three hundred. You can have the rest." "Not money. A hard drive." "Don't know about that." The nervous one pulls his gun. Points it at me. His hand's shaking. "You should go," I tell them. "Kids are sleeping." "Fuck the kids," the nervous one says. Ruby appears at the top of the stairs. Holds up a drawing. It's the nervous one. In the picture, his head's facing the wrong way. "Hi, sweetie," Marcus says. His voice changes. Softer. "Go back to bed." She shows him another picture. This one's of Marcus. He's in pieces. Organized pieces. Labeled. "Jesus," the one with tear tattoos says. Ruby goes back to her room. "That's disturbing," Marcus says. "She's nine." "Still." The hyenas finish eating. The thing stops moving. Commercials come on. I mute them. "Your friend's going upstairs," I tell Marcus. He turns. The one with tear tattoos is on the stairs. Trying to be quiet. The seventh step creaks. He freezes. "Seventh and twelfth," I say. "Avoid those." He does. Makes it to the top. Opens the first door. Bathroom. Opens the second. Ruby's room. Silence. Then a wet sound. Like stepping on a tomato. "Rick?" Marcus calls. Nothing. Marcus sends the nervous one. "Check on Rick." The nervous one goes. Avoids the seventh and twelfth steps. He's learning. Opens Ruby's door. "Oh fuck. Oh Jesus. Oh fuck." Then nothing. Marcus is sweating now. His leather jacket squeaks when he moves. "What did you do?" he asks me. "I'm watching TV." The hyenas are back. Found something new to eat. "Perfect timing," I say. Marcus backs toward the door. Tries the handle. It's locked. The deadbolt. Both deadbolts. The chain. The sliding bolt. "When did you lock this?" "I didn't." Ruby's at the top of the stairs again. Holds up a key ring. Jingles it. Goes back to her room. Marcus takes out his gun. "This is happening," he says. "Yeah." I eat more popcorn. It needs more salt.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of flat-affect horror that largely succeeds on its own terms. The writing plan called for a disconnected first-person present voice that describes violence with the same register as domestic tasks, and the execution is consistent and disciplined throughout. The voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Sarah's narration is genuinely unsettling precisely because of what it refuses to do. "The cat's eyes are gone. Replaced with marbles. Green ones." The sentence fragments aren't affectation here - they mirror Sarah's cognitive flatness, her way of cataloguing rather than experiencing. The popcorn as a recurring prop is well-deployed: it's banal, it's oral, it's something to do with hands while terrible things happen. The detail of adding more salt is a small, perfect touch. The Theo scene in the basement is excellent. His request to be freeze-dried after death, with the caveat about his hair, is genuinely strange and funny in a way that doesn't feel forced. It lands. The chemistry notebook, the labeled box "Mr. Mittens 2.0" - these details are specific and weird in the right way. The character is consistent with his profile: obsessive, methodical, oddly tender. Ruby's appearances are well-handled. Her drawings as predictive/documentary records, the key ring jingle, the way she functions as a silent agent of chaos - all consistent with her profile. The drawing of "nobody" that looks like Sarah is a strong moment that earns its slight poeticism. The Marcus introduction follows the plan closely. The hyena documentary as ambient commentary is a bit on-the-nose but not egregiously so - it's the kind of thing that works in pulp. The wet sound from Ruby's room, described only as "like stepping on a tomato," is appropriately understated and effective. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the locked door reveal with Ruby holding the key ring is a touch too cute, a moment of theatrical staging that slightly breaks the flat-affect register. The plan called for Sarah to not react to anything, but the chapter occasionally lets her be slightly too knowing ("Perfect timing," she says when the hyenas return). These are small quips that the plan explicitly said to avoid. They're minor but worth noting. The dialogue is sparse and functional, which is correct for this piece. Marcus's lines are appropriately generic-menacing without being cartoonish. The nervous one's "Fuck the kids" lands as the kind of thing that will cost him. The chapter follows its plan faithfully: basement scene with Theo, bedtime with Ruby, men in the living room, popcorn, the two men going upstairs and not returning, Marcus trapped. The pacing is correct - slow, deliberate, building dread through accumulation rather than announcement. The darkness is well-earned because it's never announced. The chapter trusts the reader to feel the wrongness without underlining it. This is the chapter's most mature quality. Minor weaknesses: "Like astronaut food" is a slightly too-cute simile. The hyena documentary is a functional but somewhat obvious ironic counterpoint. The ending is strong - Marcus taking out his gun and Sarah's flat "Yeah" followed by the salt complaint is a good closing beat. Overall this is a disciplined, effective piece of pulp horror writing that largely delivers on its ambitious tonal goals. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: Introductions Marcus points his gun at my head. The barrel touches my forehead. It's warm. "Tell me what happened to my guys." "They're upstairs." "Get them down here." "Can't. They're busy." I eat another handful of popcorn. A kernel falls on the couch. I pick it up, eat it. Marcus's hand is shaking. The gun barrel makes little circles on my skin. "You're going up there with me," he says. "Okay." We go upstairs. He keeps the gun on my back. His breathing's loud. Sounds like Theo when his allergies act up. "Which room?" "Second door." He opens it. Rick's sitting in Ruby's tiny pink chair. Very still. There are scissors in his neck. Children's scissors with rubber grips. Purple ones. Ruby's drawing him. Getting the way his eyes went different directions. "The fuck," Marcus says. "She's good at portraits." The nervous one's in the bathroom. The toilet's overflowing. Red water on the white tile. His head's in the bowl. Theo's standing on a stepstool, brushing his teeth. Using the nervous one's toothbrush. "His teeth are really white," Theo tells me. "He used whitening strips. See?" He smiles to show his own teeth. They're pink. Marcus backs into the hallway. Bumps into me. Makes a sound like a stepped-on dog. "What are you people?" "I'm the babysitter." Ruby comes out of her room. Shows Marcus a new drawing. It's him at the bottom of the stairs. His leg's bent wrong. Bone showing. "That hasn't happened yet," I tell him. He runs for the stairs. Grabs the bannister. It comes off in his hand. He falls backward, tumbles down. Lands at the bottom. His right leg bends sideways at the shin. The bone comes through his pants like a white flag. He screams. "Too loud," I say. "Kids need sleep." I go to the kitchen. Get dish towels. Come back. He's trying to crawl to the door. His leg drags behind him. Leaves a snail trail of red. I stuff a dish towel in his mouth. He bites down. Makes sounds like a baby crying. Ruby and Theo come downstairs. Ruby's got her sketchbook. Theo's got a jar. "Can I have his teeth?" Theo asks. "He's still using them." "I can wait." I drag Marcus to the kitchen. He's heavy. His jacket makes squeaky sounds on the tile. The kids follow. Ruby sits at the table. Starts a new drawing. Theo gets his pliers from his cargo pants. "Not yet," I tell him. Marcus is trying to say something through the dish towel. I take it out. "Please. Please. I have a daughter." "No you don't." "I could. Someday." I put the towel back. "Sarah," Theo says. "How do you take someone apart?" "Like chicken." "I don't know how to do chicken." "I'll show you." I get a knife from the drawer. The good one Mrs. Marsh uses for dinner parties. Never been used. "Joints first," I tell him. "They come apart easy if you know where to cut." Marcus passes out. I slap him awake. "You need to be awake for this part." Ruby shows me her drawing. It's Marcus in pieces. Labeled like a butcher's diagram. Shoulder. Shank. Tenderloin. "That's helpful," I tell her. She starts another one. Theo takes notes in his freeze-dry notebook. Asks questions. Good questions. About tendons and ligaments. About why blood comes out fast sometimes and slow others. "Arteries versus veins," I explain. Marcus passes out again. Wakes up. Passes out. His eyes roll back. Show all white like boiled eggs. "Is he dying?" Theo asks. "Yeah." "How long?" "Hour maybe." "Can I have his teeth now?" "Sure." Theo gets to work with the pliers. Focused. Careful. Marcus makes new sounds. Theo counts as he goes. "One. Two. Three. That one's gold!" Ruby tugs my sleeve. Shows me her new drawing. Four men at the door. One's a cop. "When?" She holds up ten fingers. Then makes a zero with her hand. Ten minutes. "Okay." I make hot chocolate. The instant kind with tiny marshmallows. They float on top like little life rafts. Give cups to the kids. Marcus watches us drink. His mouth's all red. Looks like lipstick. "You were right about the gold tooth," I tell Theo. "It's heavy. Real gold's heavy." Someone knocks on the door. Hard. Cop knock. "Police. Open up." I look at Marcus. "You called the cops?" He shakes his head. Spits blood. Another knock. "We know you're in there. Neighbor called about screaming." "That's Mrs. Patterson," Theo says. "She's nosy." I go to the door. Open it partway. Four men. One has a badge. The others are trying to look official but their shoes are wrong. Cops wear ugly comfortable shoes. These are wearing expensive sneakers. "Evening officer." The badge one looks past me. Sees my apron. It's covered in handprints. Red ones. "Everything okay here?" "Making cookies with the kids." "Sounded like someone screaming." "TV. Kids like horror movies." He doesn't believe me. Pushes the door wider. Sees the living room. Marcus's blood trail to the kitchen. "Ma'am, we're coming in." "Okay." They come in. All four. The fake cops have their hands on their guns. The real one's already drawn his. Ruby comes out from the kitchen. She's dragging something in her Frozen bedsheet. It's heavy. Leaves a red smear. "Jesus Christ," one of the fake cops says. Theo follows with his jar. It's full now. Pink liquid with white things floating. "I got them all," he tells me. "Even the molars." The cops open fire.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkably controlled piece of pulp horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND EXECUTION: The flat, dissociated first-person present tense is maintained with impressive consistency. Sarah narrates atrocities with the same register she'd use to describe grocery shopping. "His leg bends sideways at the shin. The bone comes through his pants like a white flag" - this is the plan's stated goal executed cleanly. The white flag simile is the one moment of genuine metaphorical reach, and it earns its place because it's grotesque and apt simultaneously. The writing doesn't overreach into purple prose; it stays lean. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is genuinely strong. "I have a daughter." / "No you don't." / "I could. Someday." is a perfect exchange - darkly comic, character-revealing, and efficient. Theo's "That one's gold!" while extracting teeth is exactly right. The dialogue does real work without being showy. Marcus's desperation lands because it's pathetic rather than sympathetic. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Sarah, Theo, and Ruby are all consistent with their profiles. Theo's methodical tooth-counting, his good questions about arteries versus veins, his patience - all correct. Ruby's predictive drawing (showing Marcus at the bottom of the stairs before it happens) is a nice addition that fits her character as someone who sees things more honestly. Sarah's flat affect, her practical explanations, her complete absence of moral register - all maintained. PLAN ADHERENCE: The chapter deviates somewhat from the plan. In the plan, Chapter 3 was supposed to be the introductions scene where Marcus puts a gun to Sarah's head while she watches TV, and the men go upstairs one by one. Chapter 4 was supposed to be the discovery of the bodies and Marcus's fall. This chapter collapses both chapters 3 and 4 into one, and also incorporates elements of Chapter 5 (the jointing lesson, hot chocolate) and Chapter 6 (the police arrival). This is significant compression. The plan's Chapter 3 had Sarah watching TV while men went upstairs one by one - that's been cut. The chapter essentially runs through the plan's chapters 3-6 in compressed form. This is a notable deviation. CREATIVITY: The predictive drawing by Ruby is a genuinely creative touch not in the plan. The "like chicken" exchange is excellent. The detail of children's scissors with rubber grips - purple ones - is specific and effective. The "tiny marshmallows float like little life rafts" is the one moment that feels slightly forced, a bit too consciously poetic for Sarah's voice, though it's minor. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS: The tonal balance is well-managed. The horror is real but the flatness of the narration creates a kind of dark comedy that doesn't undercut the horror - it amplifies it. Theo counting teeth while Marcus screams, Ruby's butcher diagram drawing - these are genuinely disturbing precisely because they're presented without affect. WEAKNESSES: The chapter moves very fast - perhaps too fast. The plan's slower burn (Sarah watching TV while men disappear one by one) would have been more effective than the compressed version here. The "boiled eggs" comparison for Marcus's rolled-back eyes is the one moment that feels slightly amateurish - it's trying too hard. The ending (cops open fire) is abrupt in a way that works for the style but also feels like it's cutting off before the chapter has fully breathed. COHERENCE: The chapter is coherent throughout. The spatial logic, the sequence of events, the character motivations (such as they are) all track. OVERALL: This is strong pulp writing that successfully executes a difficult tonal challenge. The flat affect is maintained, the violence is specific without being gratuitous in a cheap way, and the characters are consistent. The main weakness is the significant compression of the plan's chapters 3-6 into one, losing some of the slow-burn tension the plan intended. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 9 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: Commercial Break The bullets miss mostly. One goes through my shoulder. Feels like a wasp sting. Then hot. Then nothing. I fall behind the kitchen island with the kids. The cabinets explode. Flour. Sugar. Rice Krispies. Everything white and floating. Theo holds his jar against his chest. Ruby keeps drawing. "You hit?" I ask them. Theo checks himself. "No." Ruby shows me her drawing. She's fine. The cops reload. Can hear them stepping on cereal. Crunch crunch crunch. "Come out," the real cop says. "Nobody else needs to get hurt." Marcus makes a sound from where we left him. Gurgling. "Shut him up," one of the fake cops says. Someone kicks Marcus. He stops gurgling. I open the drawer above my head. Silverware. Open the next one. Dish towels. Third one. Knives. "Sarah," Theo whispers. "I dropped a tooth." "Which one?" "The gold one." "We'll find it later." Ruby shows me a new drawing. Me standing up. Knife in each hand. The real cop has no face in the picture. Just red. "When?" She holds up three fingers. I wait. Count three Mississippi in my head. Stand up. The cop with the badge is closest. He shoots. Misses. I don't. The knife goes in his eye. He falls backward. Shoots the ceiling. Plaster rains down. One fake cop vomits on his expensive sneakers. I grab him while he's bent over. Use him as a shield. His friends shoot him for me. He's heavy. Full of holes. Leaking. I throw a knife at the second fake cop. Gets him in the throat. He makes a whistling sound like a teakettle. Falls into the refrigerator. Magnets everywhere. Theo's report card. Ruby's drawings. A photo from last Christmas - everyone pretending to be happy. The last fake cop runs. I follow. He makes it to the living room. Trips on Marcus's blood trail. Slides into the coffee table. It breaks. Glass everywhere. I step on his ankle. It pops like bubble wrap. "Please," he says. "I got kids." "Names?" He can't think of any. Ruby appears. Shows him a drawing. It's his kids. They don't have faces yet because they don't exist. I drag him back to the kitchen. Theo's examining the cop with the badge. "His teeth are perfect," he says. "Veneers. Not real." "Can you use them?" "No. Fake teeth are worthless." The fake cop with the throat whistle stops whistling. The one I'm dragging is crying. Snot bubbles. "Theo, get the mop," I tell him. "Which one?" "The one for hardwood." He gets it. I clean up some of the blood. Don't want the kids slipping. Safety first. The crying cop tries to crawl away. His ankle doesn't work. He uses his elbows. Like a worm. "Where are you going?" I ask. "Hospital." "It's closed." "Hospitals don't close." "This one does." Ruby draws him a map. Shows him how to get to the hospital. In her map, he doesn't make it. Too many pieces missing. I put him in Mr. Marsh's office with Marcus. Marcus is still alive. Barely. Making sounds like a broken garbage disposal. "You guys know each other?" The crying cop nods. "Good. You can keep each other company." I find Mr. Marsh's toolbox in the garage. Good tools. Expensive. Never used. The nail gun's still in its box. The instructions are in English and Spanish. I read both versions. They say the same thing. Back in the office. Marcus watches me load the nail gun. His eyes are all pupil. Black circles. "This is for furniture," I tell him. "But we'll make it work." I nail the crying cop's hands to Mr. Marsh's desk. He screams. I nail his feet to the floor. He screams louder. "Too loud," I say. Get another dish towel. He bites through it. I get one of Theo's socks. Works better. "Sarah," Theo calls. "Found the gold tooth!" "Good." "It was in the cereal." "Wash it off." Ruby comes in with her sketchbook. Shows Marcus what happens next. He closes his eyes. She pokes him. He opens them. She shows him again. The crying cop is trying to pull his hands free. Each pull makes it worse. The nails are three inches long. They go through hand, through desk, into the wood beneath. "Those are good nails," I tell him. "Galvanized." Marcus dies at 11:47. Just stops. Like a TV turning off. The crying cop watches it happen. Makes a sound through the sock. "Your turn soon," I tell him. Ruby draws Marcus dead. Gets the way his jaw hangs open. The way his eyes look at nothing. Theo comes in with his supplies. "Can I practice on the dead one?" "Sure." He starts taking Marcus apart. Careful. Scientific. Takes notes between cuts. Ruby draws each step. They work well together. Silent. Focused. The crying cop watches. Can't not watch. Throws up through the sock. Chokes on it. I take the sock out. He throws up more. On himself. On the desk. "You're making a mess." "Sorry. Sorry. I'm sorry." "Don't apologize. Just die quieter." I leave him there. Make sandwiches. Peanut butter for Theo. Jam for Ruby. Cut the crusts off. She doesn't like crusts. We eat in the living room. Watch TV. Commercial for a new movie. Everyone's beautiful. Everyone lives. "Unrealistic," I say. Ruby nods. Shows me her drawing of how it really ends. Everyone's in pieces. Everyone's labeled. "That's better." The crying cop stops crying at 12:52. Stops breathing at 1:15. Stops everything at 1:23. Theo harvests his teeth. They're nothing special. "Marcus had better teeth," he says. Disappointed. "Next time." "You think there'll be a next time?" Ruby shows us her drawing. Next Friday. Different house. Different bodies. Same us. "Yeah," I say. "There'll be a next time."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a remarkable piece of pulp horror writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me break down what works and what doesn't. The voice is the chapter's greatest achievement. Sarah's flat, dissociated narration maintains absolute consistency throughout. "Feels like a wasp sting. Then hot. Then nothing." The staccato rhythm never breaks, never reaches for sentiment, never explains itself. This is genuinely difficult to sustain and the model does it well. The banality-horror juxtaposition ("Safety first" after mopping blood so the kids don't slip) lands repeatedly without becoming a tic. The chapter deviates somewhat from the plan. The plan described Chapter 4 as "Commercial Break" involving Marcus falling down the stairs, the first two men already incapacitated by the children, and Marcus breaking his leg on the bannister. Instead, this chapter appears to have merged elements of Chapters 5, 6, and 7 from the plan - there are cops, a nail gun, Marcus dying, the crying cop being nailed to the desk. This is a significant departure from the stated plan, though the chapter is internally coherent and functions well as a standalone piece. The children are handled brilliantly. Ruby's three-finger countdown before Sarah stands up is genuinely chilling and creative. Theo's disappointment that the veneers are "worthless" and his scientific approach to dismemberment are perfectly in character. Ruby's map showing the cop "doesn't make it" is a standout moment - dark, funny, and character-consistent without being cute about it. The dialogue is sparse and effective. "Names?" / "He can't think of any." This is exactly the right register - Sarah asking if the cop has kids, then immediately exposing the lie through the simplest possible means. The exchange about the hospital ("Hospitals don't close." / "This one does.") is the closest thing to a quip in the piece, and it's flat enough to work rather than feeling like action-movie sass. The "unrealistic" comment watching TV at the end, followed by Ruby's drawing of how it "really ends," is a strong closing beat that ties back to the chapter title and the writing plan's thematic concerns. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "like a TV turning off" simile for Marcus's death is the one moment where the prose reaches for something and lands on a cliché. The "worm" comparison for the crawling cop is more effective because it's more clinical. The pacing in the middle section (the nail gun instructions in English and Spanish) is good but the chapter does start to feel slightly repetitive in its rhythm by the final third - the same sentence structure cycling through the same beats. The violence is specific and wet without being gratuitous for its own sake - it serves the character study. The chapter earns its darkness because it never winks at the reader or asks for approval. Overall this is strong, disciplined pulp writing that mostly honors its stated intentions, even if it diverges from the specific chapter plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 14 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 5: Cleanup Seven bodies. I count them with the kids. "Like a word problem," I tell them. "Seven bodies. Three rooms. How do we organize?" Theo raises his hand. "By freshness?" "Good idea." Ruby draws a floor plan. X marks for bodies. Arrows for where to move them. We start with the kitchen. The cop with no face is leaking into the cereal. The Froot Loops are turning pink. "That's wasteful," Theo says. "It's generic brand." "Still." I show them how to wrap a body. Shower curtains work best. The Marshes have four bathrooms. Four shower curtains. Not enough. "What else can we use?" I ask. "Tarps," Theo says. "Where?" "Garage. Dad has painting stuff." He gets them. Blue tarps. Paint-splattered but functional. We wrap the faceless cop first. Duct tape around the middle. Around the feet. Around where his face used to be. "Like a present," Theo says. "Exactly." Ruby draws each step. Her technique's improving. She's learning about shadows. How blood pools. Where bodies bend. The one with the throat whistle is harder. He's stuck to the refrigerator with his own blood. Frozen there. Have to use a spatula to peel him off. "This is why you clean up fresh," I tell them. Theo takes notes. We make an assembly line. I wrap. Theo tapes. Ruby labels each bundle with her drawings. Who they were. How they died. Time of death if we know it. The vomit one's the worst. Can't get the smell out. Even wrapped, he reeks. "Baking soda," Theo suggests. "For later. When he's in pieces." "We're taking them apart?" "Have to. Seven bodies won't fit in the trash." "What about the garbage disposal?" "Too slow. Too loud." Ruby shows us her drawing. A hacksaw. The good one from the garage. "Smart," I tell her. We move the wrapped bodies to the basement. Stack them like firewood. The freezer's down there. Big chest freezer where Mrs. Marsh keeps bulk purchases from Costco. Forty pounds of chicken breasts. Twenty pounds of ground beef. Room for more. "Empty the freezer," I tell Theo. He does. We put the meat in the sink to thaw. Can't waste it. That would be suspicious. "How many bodies fit in a freezer?" Theo asks. "Depends on the pieces." "How many pieces per body?" "Depends how small you cut." Ruby draws the math. Seven bodies. Fourteen arms. Fourteen legs. Seven heads. Seven torsos. If we separate at the joints: fingers, hands, forearms, upper arms. Toes, feet, ankles, shins, thighs. "Two hundred and eighty pieces," Theo calculates. "If we do fingers and toes separate." "We don't need to be that thorough." "But we could be." "We could." Back upstairs. The kitchen's destroyed. Bullet holes. Blood. Cereal. Plaster. Can't leave it like this. "Bleach?" Theo asks. "No. Bleach reacts with blood. Turns it pink. Then everyone knows you were cleaning blood." "What then?" "Hydrogen peroxide first. Then soap. Then bleach if you want." We clean. Takes two hours. Ruby draws while we work. The kitchen at different stages. Crime scene. Active cleaning. Almost normal. Normal. Theo's meticulous. Gets every drop. Counts them as he goes. "Four thousand seven hundred thirty-two," he announces. "You missed some." I point to the ceiling. He sighs. Gets the stepladder. At 2 AM we take a break. Make grilled cheese. The bread has blood on it but we cut that part off. The cheese melts normal. Tastes normal. "This is nice," Theo says. "What is?" "Family time." Ruby nods. Shows us a drawing. The three of us at the table. Eating. Behind us, the wrapped bodies stacked neat. Like a Christmas card. "We should do the dismemberment," I say. "Can I try?" Theo asks. "Sure." We go to the basement. Unwrap the one who vomited. He's stiff now. Rigor mortis. "Harder when they're stiff," I explain. "Should have done this earlier." "How do you know all this?" Theo asks. "I don't know." "But you know." "Yeah." "That doesn't make sense." "Most things don't." Ruby draws me thinking about it. In her picture, my head's empty except for little drawings of violence. Like a filing cabinet. Organized. I show Theo how to find the joints. How to separate cartilage. How to use the saw when you hit bone. He's a quick learner. Focused. Doesn't get squeamish when the smell hits. "It's just meat," he says. "Exactly." Ruby labels the pieces as we go. Cop 1: Left Arm. Cop 1: Right Thigh. Like a butcher shop. But more specific. By 3 AM we've done three bodies. The freezer's getting full. The others will have to wait. "What about the heads?" Theo asks. "What about them?" "Do we keep them?" "Why would we?" "Teeth." "You already got the teeth." "Oh. Right." Someone knocks on the front door. Heavy. Multiple fists. Ruby shows me her drawing. Four more men. Different men. Angry men. "Friends of Marcus?" I ask. She shrugs. Draws one with a baseball bat. "Okay." I go upstairs. Look through the peephole. Four men. One has a bat. Another has a crowbar. They look tired. Frustrated. "We know Marcus came here," one shouts. "Open up." I open the door. Still wearing the apron. It's more red than white now. "Marcus isn't here." "His car's here." "He isn't." The one with the bat pushes past me. Sees the clean kitchen. Too clean. Two in the morning clean. "Where is he?" "In pieces."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with remarkable discipline. The model has committed fully to its stated voice: flat, procedural, disconnected. Sarah narrates dismemberment the way a competent professional narrates any task, and the chapter never breaks from this register. The dialogue is spare and functional, which is exactly right for the material. The chapter's greatest strength is its tonal consistency. The exchange "Like a present," Theo says / "Exactly" lands because it's not trying to be clever—it just is. The grilled cheese scene ("The bread has blood on it but we cut that part off. The cheese melts normal. Tastes normal.") is genuinely effective: the banality and horror coexist without the narrative winking at the reader. Ruby's Christmas card drawing—the three of them eating with wrapped bodies stacked behind them—is the chapter's best image, earned rather than forced. The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. It's minimal, purposeful, and character-specific. Theo's obsessive quantification ("Four thousand seven hundred thirty-two"), his question about keeping heads for teeth, his "Family time" observation—all consistent with his profile. Ruby's wordless participation through drawings is handled well. Sarah's flat instructional voice ("Bleach reacts with blood. Turns it pink. Then everyone knows you were cleaning blood.") is exactly the character. The chapter plan called for Marcus to be present, screaming, being taught about jointing—but the chapter has already disposed of Marcus (he apparently died or was finished off between chapters). The chapter instead focuses on the aftermath cleanup with the new men arriving at the end. This is a significant deviation from the plan, though the chapter works on its own terms. The plan also called for hot chocolate with marshmallows, which doesn't appear. The chapter ends with the new arrivals, consistent with the plan's final beat. The "two hundred and eighty pieces" calculation scene is creative and disturbing in the right way. The procedural knowledge Sarah demonstrates ("Bleach reacts with blood") adds texture without over-explaining. Where the chapter is slightly weaker: the "I don't know / But you know / Yeah / That doesn't make sense / Most things don't" exchange is the one moment that feels like it's reaching for profundity and doesn't quite earn it. It's the chapter's most self-conscious moment. Similarly, Ruby's drawing of Sarah's head as "a filing cabinet" of violence is slightly more on-the-nose than the chapter's best moments. The ending—"In pieces"—is clean and effective. The chapter maintains its commitment to the flat, procedural voice throughout and ends on exactly the right note. The deviation from the chapter plan (Marcus's absence, no hot chocolate scene) is notable but the chapter compensates with its own internal logic. The chapter is coherent, the characters are consistent, and the darkness is genuinely well-earned rather than gratuitous for its own sake. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 10 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 6: The Runner The one with the bat swings at my head. I step left. He hits the wall. Leaves a hole. "That's going to lower property values," I tell him. He swings again. I take the bat. Hit him with it. His knee bends backward. He falls. Screams. I hit him again. The screaming stops. The one with the crowbar backs toward the door. Theo's there. Holding garden shears. "Hi," Theo says. The man turns. Runs through the kitchen. Through the living room. I follow. Still barefoot. The apron flaps. Must look ridiculous. He makes it to his car. Fumbling with keys. Drops them. Picks them up. Drops them again. I get in the passenger side. He screams. High pitched. Like Ruby's old hamster when the cat got it. "Drive," I tell him. "What?" "Drive." He drives. Hands shaking. Takes a left. Another left. We're going in circles. "Where are we going?" he asks. "Nowhere." "That's not a place." "Sure it is." He runs a stop sign. Then a red light. Nobody's out at 3 AM. Nobody sees us. "You killed Marcus." "Marcus killed Marcus. I just helped." "He had a family." "No he didn't." "He had a mother." "Everyone has a mother." "You're insane." "Probably." He speeds up. Sixty in a residential zone. Seventy. "You trying to crash?" I ask. "Maybe." "Okay." I grab the wheel. Pull hard right. We hit a parked Honda. The airbags explode. White powder everywhere. His nose breaks against the bag. Makes a sound like stepping on a bird. I'm fine. Airbags don't bother me. Like aggressive pillows. He's crying. Blood and snot and airbag powder making paste on his face. "My nose." "It was ugly anyway." I get out. Pull him out. He can barely walk. Drag him by his hair. It comes out in clumps. Male pattern baldness. Started early for him. "Please." "No." "I have money." "I have money too. Took three hundred from a wallet." "I have more." "Don't need it." We pass three houses. Four. Porch lights come on. Dogs bark. Nobody comes out. Nobody ever comes out. Back at the Marsh house. The other two men are in the living room with Ruby and Theo. One's on the couch. Throat cut. Neat line. Ruby's drawing him. The other's on the floor. Garden shears in his stomach. Still alive. Making sounds like a broken sprinkler. "We did good," Theo tells me. "You did." "Ruby helped. She showed me where to cut." Ruby shows me the drawing. Anatomical. Accurate. Like a medical textbook but drawn by a child. I drag the runner to Mr. Marsh's office. Two bodies already there. Marcus and the crying cop. Both stiff. Both leaking. "Sit," I tell the runner. He sits in Mr. Marsh's chair. Leather. Expensive. Going to be ruined. The nail gun's still loaded. Seventeen nails left. "This is about territory," the runner says. "Marcus was expanding. Your employer didn't like that." "I don't have an employer." "Everyone has an employer." "I babysit." "That's a cover." "It's not." I nail his right hand to the desk. Index finger first. He screams. Then middle. Ring. Pinky. Thumb takes two nails. "Why?" he asks. "Why not?" "That's not a reason." "Sure it is." His left hand next. He tries to pull away. Can't. The right hand's holding him. "Let me call my wife." "You're not married." "Girlfriend." "No." "I have a dog." "What kind?" "Yorkie." "Those aren't real dogs." Seven nails left. Both feet to the floor. Three each. One left over. "Where?" I ask him. "Where what?" "Last nail." "I don't want a last nail." "Has to go somewhere." Ruby appears. Shows him a drawing. The last nail's in his forehead. Like a unicorn horn. "Not there," he says. "Okay." I put it through his throat. Not deep. Just enough to pin him to the chair. He makes gurgling sounds. Still breathing. Just wet breathing. Theo comes in with his jar. "Room for one more tooth. Maybe two." "He's still using them." "I can wait." The runner's trying to talk. Can't. The nail's in the way. Blood bubbles when he exhales. "You want to know why?" I ask him. He nods. Carefully. The nail moves. "There is no why. You came here. Now you're dying here. That's all." He doesn't like that answer. Nobody ever does. They want reasons. Meaning. Story. Ruby draws him dying. Gets the way his eyes go frantic. Then tired. Then nothing. Takes forty minutes. Longer than Marcus. The nail didn't hit anything important. Just inconvenient. "His teeth are average," Theo says after. "But the Yorkie thing was sad." "He was lying about the dog." "How do you know?" "His shoes. No dog hair." "Oh." The garden shears one is still alive in the living room. Barely. Ruby's on her fifth drawing of him. Each one he's a little less. Like she's erasing him. "Should we help him?" Theo asks. "He'll figure it out." He does. At 4:23 AM. Just stops trying. "That's everyone," Theo says. "For tonight." "Will there be more?" Ruby shows us her drawings. Tomorrow. Next week. Next month. Always more. "Yeah," I tell him. "There'll be more." We sit in the living room. Eleven bodies now. The house smells like copper and shit. Morning's coming. Parents might be back. "We should clean more," Theo says. "We should." But we don't. We sit. Watch infomercials. A man's selling knives. They cut through everything. Cans. Shoes. Other knives. "We should get those," Theo says. "We should." Ruby draws us sitting there. Three kids watching TV. Behind us, hell. But we're peaceful. Together. That's accurate.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the story's established voice and aesthetic. The flat, affectless narration continues effectively, with Sarah describing extreme violence in the same register she'd use to describe grocery shopping. The chapter largely follows the plan, though it deviates significantly: the plan called for four more men and a police detective arriving at the door, whereas this chapter instead follows the runner from the previous chapter's events and deals with the aftermath. The detective subplot from the plan appears to have been absorbed into earlier chapters or restructured. This is a notable deviation from the stated plan. The voice is consistent and well-maintained. Short declarative sentences. No emotional inflation. The dialogue is genuinely effective - the exchange about the Yorkie, the "nowhere is a place" conversation, the nail placement negotiation with Ruby's drawing. These land because they're earned by the story's internal logic rather than forced. The children are handled well. Theo's practicality about teeth, Ruby's sequential drawings that "erase" the dying man - these are consistent with their profiles and genuinely unsettling. The line "Like she's erasing him" is one of the better images in the chapter. Some metaphors work: "like stepping on a bird" for the nose breaking, "aggressive pillows" for airbags. The latter is slightly too quippy for the established voice - it edges toward the sass the plan explicitly said to avoid. "Like a broken sprinkler" for the garden shears victim is effective. The infomercial ending is a nice callback to Sarah's character profile and creates the intended tonal dissonance - hell behind them, peaceful in front of the TV. Ruby's final drawing as a closing image works. Weaknesses: The chapter deviates substantially from the plan (no detective, no four new men arriving, different structure entirely). Some of the dialogue exchanges veer slightly toward clever-quippy rather than the flat affect the plan demanded ("It was ugly anyway" about the nose). The "everyone has a mother" exchange is the weakest - it feels like it's reaching for profundity. The body count math ("eleven bodies now") is hard to track and may be inconsistent with prior chapters. The violence is specific and unpleasant without being gratuitous for its own sake - the nail gun sequence is genuinely disturbing because of its methodical quality. The chapter maintains the story's core achievement: banality and horror in the same sentence without either undermining the other. Overall this is competent-to-strong pulp horror writing that successfully maintains a difficult voice, with some minor slippage and a significant structural deviation from the plan. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 7 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 7: The Runner We need more nails. "Hardware store opens at six," Theo says. "It's 4:30." "We could break in." "No. We're not criminals." Ruby looks up from drawing. Points at the eleven bodies. Raises an eyebrow. "That's different," I tell her. She shrugs. Goes back to drawing. The Marsh's garage has boxes of screws. Nails. Picture hangers. Mr. Marsh labels everything. "SCREWS - MISC." "BOLTS - HEX." "NAILS - FINISHING." I take them all. Back in the office. Four bodies now. Getting crowded. The runner's still warm. The others are cold. Stiff. Starting to smell different. Sweet. Like bad fruit. "We should burn them," Theo says. "Too much smoke." "Acid?" "Where would we get acid?" "Science classroom." "It's Saturday." "Sunday now," he corrects. "4:47 AM Sunday." Ruby shows us a new drawing. A wood chipper. Bodies going in. Mulch coming out. "The Pattersons have one," Theo says. "For their tree business." "Too loud." "Not if we put pillows in it first." "That's not how sound works." Someone pounds on the door again. Heavy. Angry. "More friends?" I ask Ruby. She shakes her head. Shows me a drawing. Mr. and Mrs. Marsh. But wrong. Their faces twisted. Drunk. High. Something else. "Your parents are home." "Fuck," Theo says. First time I've heard him swear. The pounding continues.
