Mrs. Marsh's voice: "Open the fucking door. Lost my keys." Mr. Marsh: "You didn't lose them. You threw them at me." "That was your keys." "Those were my work keys." "Same thing." I open the door. They stumble in. Mrs. Marsh's dress is inside out. Mr. Marsh has lipstick on his collar. Not his wife's color. They stop. Look at me. The apron. The blood. "What—" Mr. Marsh starts. "Baking," I say. "Baking," Mrs. Marsh repeats. Laughs. High pitched. Manic. "At five AM?" "Kids couldn't sleep." She pushes past me. Sees the living room. Too clean. Smells the bleach. The copper underneath. "What did you do?" "My job." Mr. Marsh goes to his office. Opens the door. Sees the bodies. Doesn't scream. Just stands there. "Sarah," he says. Quiet. "What did you do?" "They came for you. About the forty thousand." "I don't owe anyone forty thousand." "They thought you did." "So you killed them?" "Yes." He laughs. Not happy laughing. The kind before crying. Mrs. Marsh is in the kitchen now. Opens the freezer. Sees the parts. Wrapped neat. Labeled. "Are those—" "Yes." She vomits. Red wine and something chunky. Maybe shrimp. Rich people party food. Theo comes downstairs. "Mom. Dad. You're home early." "Theo, go to your room," Mr. Marsh says. "No." "Theo—" "No. Sarah's teaching us things." Ruby appears. Shows them a drawing. Them at their separate hotels. What they did there. Who they did it with. Details. "How do you know that?" Mrs. Marsh asks. Ruby shrugs. Draws more. "We're calling the police," Mr. Marsh says. "Okay." He gets his phone. Dials. Stops. "What do I tell them?" "The truth." "Which is?" "Eleven men broke in. I killed them." "They won't believe that." "They will when they see the bodies." He hangs up. Sits on the couch. Head in hands. "This is insane." "Yes." "You're insane." "Probably." Mrs. Marsh is drinking straight from a wine bottle she found somewhere. Always has wine hidden. Emergency wine. "We have to do something with them," she says. "We are. Cutting them up. Freezing them." "Then what?" "Garbage day's Tuesday." "We can't put bodies in the garbage." "Not whole bodies. Parts." "That's worse." "It's more efficient." Mr. Marsh stands up. "We're leaving. Kids, get your things." "No," Theo says. "This isn't a discussion." "We're not done learning." Ruby shows them another drawing. Police arriving. Arresting the parents. Not me. Them. "Why us?" Mrs. Marsh asks. Ruby draws Mr. Marsh's safe. The real one. Behind the fake one. Full of things that shouldn't exist. Things that explain why men come looking for him. "You don't know about that," Mr. Marsh says. Ruby draws the combination. 36-18-42. He tries it. It opens. Inside: money. Drugs. Photos of people who aren't alive anymore. A hard drive. "The forty thousand's there," I tell him. "You did owe it." "How do you know?" "I don't. But it makes sense." Mrs. Marsh laughs again. Drinks more. "We're all going to prison." "No. Just you two." "You killed them." "To protect your children. Because you put them in danger." She throws the wine bottle. Misses. It breaks against the wall. Another stain. "Fuck you." "Okay." Theo's studying the safe's contents. "Dad, is this cocaine?" "Don't touch that." "Why do you have cocaine?" "Business." "What business?" "Complicated business." Ruby draws the business. It's not complicated. It's simple. Mr. Marsh moves things for people. Mrs. Marsh cleans the money. Has been for years. "We're not bad people," Mrs. Marsh says. Nobody responds. The sun's coming up. Light through the windows. Shows everything. The missed blood spots. The covered bullet holes. The truth. "Sarah," Mr. Marsh says. "What do you want?" "For what?" "To fix this." "It's already fixed." "No. The bodies—" "Will be gone by Tuesday." "The police—" "Won't know if you don't tell them." "And you?" I check my phone. Three new babysitting requests. "I have other jobs." "You're just going to leave?" "At nine. When you get back from counseling." "We didn't go to counseling." "I know." They stare at me. At their kids. At their ruined house. At their ruined lives. "This can't be happening," Mrs. Marsh says. But it is.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter represents a significant deviation from the stated plan. Chapter 7 was supposed to follow "The Runner" — the last man who escaped to his car, whom Sarah pursues barefoot, drags back, and nails to the desk with a nail gun while extracting information. Instead, the chapter pivots entirely to the Marsh parents returning home unexpectedly, which was not in the plan at all. The nail gun scene is referenced but compressed into backstory, and the chapter becomes a domestic confrontation scene. This is a substantial failure to follow the chapter plan. That said, the writing itself has considerable strengths. The voice is remarkably consistent — Sarah's flat, affectless narration maintains its deadpan quality throughout. The dialogue is genuinely sharp and often funny in a deeply uncomfortable way. The exchange about garbage day ("Garbage day's Tuesday" / "We can't put bodies in the garbage" / "Not whole bodies. Parts" / "That's worse" / "It's more efficient") is exactly the kind of banal horror the plan called for, and it lands well. The Marsh parents' arrival is actually a strong creative choice — their dysfunction (dress inside out, wrong lipstick color, separate hotels) is efficiently sketched. Ruby's drawings as exposition devices work well here, particularly the drawing of the combination to the safe. The safe reveal adds a layer of plot coherence that ties the story together. Character consistency is strong. Theo's first swear word being noted, Ruby's systematic drawing-as-communication, Sarah's complete emotional flatness — all maintained. The parents match their profiles: David's corporate-speak has dissolved into panic, Monica reaches for wine immediately. The dialogue is one of the chapter's genuine strengths. Short, punchy exchanges that reveal character without explaining it. "Baking." / "Baking." / "At five AM?" — this works. The rhythm is controlled. Where the chapter falters: the nail gun scene from the plan (which was viscerally specific and central to the chapter's identity) is essentially absent. The chapter promised "The Runner" and delivered "The Parents Come Home." The violence that was supposed to be the chapter's core is reduced to aftermath. This is a significant structural betrayal of the plan. The ending is somewhat anticlimactic compared to what was planned. The plan called for Sarah to be mid-action; instead she's checking her phone for babysitting requests, which is a good image but feels like it belongs in Chapter 8. The prose avoids purple prose almost entirely. The one moment — "Shows everything. The missed blood spots. The covered bullet holes. The truth." — is slightly overwrought but brief. The writing is lean and controlled throughout. The "wet fruit" smell description for decomposing bodies is accurate and specific without being gratuitous — exactly the right register. Overall: strong execution of voice and character, genuinely creative in places, but a significant departure from the stated chapter plan that changes the chapter's fundamental nature. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 14 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 14 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 14 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 5 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 15

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: Saturday Morning Six AM. I'm making pancakes. The bodies are still everywhere. Mrs. Marsh is passed out on the couch. Empty wine bottle in her hand. Another by her feet. Mr. Marsh is sitting at the kitchen table, staring at nothing. He's been there an hour. Haven't seen him blink. "How many pancakes?" I ask him. He doesn't answer. "I'll make twelve." The batter sizzles. Bubbles form. Pop. I flip them. Perfect circles. Golden brown. Theo comes in with his teeth. Has them sorted by type. Molars in one jar. Incisors in another. The gold one gets its own baby food jar. "Two hundred and seventy-three total," he says. "Best night ever." Ruby's at the table with her drawings. Thirty-seven of them. The whole night documented. She's adding details. Getting the morning light on the blood puddles. The way rigor mortis makes fingers curl. "These are good," I tell her. She nods. Knows they are. The detective's phone rings again. Been ringing all night. I answer. "Where's Detective Morrison?" A woman's voice. Worried. "Tied up." "He was supposed to be home hours ago." "He's staying late." "Who is this?" I hang up. Mr. Marsh finally speaks: "They'll come looking." "Probably." "What then?" "Not my problem." "You killed them here." "You invited them here. With your life. Your choices." He goes quiet again. I serve the pancakes. Stack of three for each kid. Butter. Syrup. Strawberries on top. Ruby eats around the strawberries. Saves them for last. Theo dissects his pancakes. Studies the air pockets. "Perfect consistency," he says. "Thanks." Mrs. Marsh wakes up. Sees us eating breakfast. The bodies visible from where she's lying. Starts laughing. Can't stop. "Shut up," Mr. Marsh tells her. She laughs harder. Ruby draws her laughing. Gets the hysteria in her eyes. The snot coming out her nose. My phone buzzes. Text from Mrs. Patterson next door: "Hi Sarah! Heard you're wonderful with the Marsh kids. Would love to book you for Friday. Tommy's birthday party. Twenty kids. Double rate?" I text back: "Yes." Another text. The Chens from two streets over: "Free Thursday? Date night." "Yes." Another. The Washingtons: "Need overnight sitter Saturday." "Yes." Theo sees me texting. "New jobs?" "Three." "Will they be like this?" "No." Ruby tugs my sleeve. Shows me a drawing. Me at the Patterson house. Tommy's birthday. Bodies in party hats. Blood on the cake. "Maybe," I correct myself. Someone knocks on the door. Light. Polite. Mr. Marsh jumps. "Who now?" I look through the peephole. Woman with a clipboard. Blazer. Smile. "Jehovah's Witness," I tell him. "At six AM?" "They're committed." I open the door. "Good morning!" she says. "Have you accepted Jesus Christ as your personal savior?" Behind me, eleven bodies. A woman laughing. A man destroyed. Two kids eating pancakes. "No thank you." "I have some literature—" "No." She sees my apron. The blood. Keeps smiling. "I'll pray for you." "Okay." She leaves. I close the door. "How?" Mr. Marsh asks. "How did she not—" "People see what they expect." "She expected blood?" "She expected a tired babysitter." The runner in the office starts to smell. Sweet. Rotten. Like the others but fresher. "We should move them," Mr. Marsh says. "Where?" "I don't know. Away." "This is away. Away from somewhere else." Mrs. Marsh stops laughing. Starts crying. Huge sobs. Theo watches her. "Mom's leaking," he says. "She'll stop." She doesn't. Just cries harder. Ruby draws her collapse. Every stage. Like time-lapse photography. "I want to die," Mrs. Marsh says. "Okay." "Kill me." "No." "You killed them." "They weren't paying me." She grabs a knife from the kitchen. Holds it to her wrist. Shaking. "Do it," Mr. Marsh says. She looks at him. Drops the knife. Goes back to crying. Seven AM. I make more pancakes. These are for me. I eat them standing up. Watch the Marshes dissolve. "We could run," Mr. Marsh says. "Pack up. Leave." "And go where?" "Anywhere." "There's nowhere that's not here." Ruby shows him a drawing. Them in a car. Police behind them. Ahead of them. Everywhere. "We're trapped," he says. "Everyone's trapped." Theo counts his teeth again. "Two hundred and seventy-three. Want to make it three hundred by lunch." "We're not killing anyone else," Mr. Marsh says. "You're not." "Neither are you." Theo looks at me. "Are we?" "Not today." "Tomorrow?" "Maybe." Eight AM. Sun's fully up. Neighbors walking dogs. Normal Sunday. I clean the dishes. Put them in the dishwasher. Add soap. Press start. The machine hums. "You're just doing dishes?" Mrs. Marsh asks. "Like nothing happened?" "Something happened. Now dishes." "You're insane." "You said that." Ruby shows me a new drawing. Me. But really me. The nothing inside visible. The hollow. She got it perfect. "That's very good," I tell her. She tears it out. Gives it to me. I fold it. Put it in my pocket with the three hundred dollars. "Nine AM," I tell the Marshes. "I leave at nine." "Then what?" Mr. Marsh asks. "You clean up. Or don't. Call police. Or don't. Kill yourselves. Or don't." "You don't care?" "No." The detective's phone rings again. Let it ring. Eight-thirty. Theo and Ruby are watching cartoons. Tom and Jerry. The old ones. Tom gets hit with an anvil. Flattens. Pops back. "Unrealistic," Theo says. "It's a cartoon." "Still." Mrs. Marsh throws up again. Nothing left. Just bile. Yellow. Foamy. Mr. Marsh goes to his safe. Takes out the money. Counts it. "Thirty-seven thousand," he says. "Not forty." "I spent three." "On what?" "Doesn't matter now." Eight forty-five. Almost done. I take off the apron. Underneath, my Ohio State hoodie. Still clean. Always keep something clean. Ruby's drawing me leaving. Walking out. Getting in my car. Driving away. In the last picture, the house is
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes the plan's vision with remarkable consistency and discipline. The flat, affectless narration voice is maintained throughout without slipping into self-parody or melodrama. Sarah's disconnection from the horror around her is rendered through precise, short declarative sentences that treat pancake-flipping and body disposal with identical emotional weight. This is the chapter's greatest strength: the voice never breaks. The Marsh parents' dissolution is handled well. Mrs. Marsh's laughing-to-crying arc feels earned given her character profile - the wine-drunk, emotionally hollow woman finally confronting consequences. Mr. Marsh's corporate-speak emptiness ("There's nowhere that's not here") lands as genuinely bleak rather than forced. The knife scene where she threatens suicide and he says "do it" is a small, ugly moment of marital truth that works. Theo and Ruby remain perfectly in character. Theo's "Two hundred and seventy-three total. Best night ever" is exactly right - enthusiastic, clinical, wrong. Ruby's drawings as documentation rather than art continues to function as the chapter's visual counterpoint. The drawing of Sarah's portrait - "the nothing behind her eyes" - pays off the plan's intention and the character profile's description of Ruby seeing Sarah as "a hole in the shape of a person." The Jehovah's Witness bit is the chapter's cleverest moment and earns its lightness. "People see what they expect" / "She expected a tired babysitter" is the kind of flat-affect wit the story does best. It doesn't overstay its welcome. The ending - Ruby drawing Sarah leaving, the house is... - cuts off mid-sentence as planned, and it works. The incompleteness mirrors the story's refusal of closure. Where the chapter is weaker: some of the Marsh parent dialogue edges toward expository ("You killed them here" / "You invited them here. With your life. Your choices.") - Sarah's response here is slightly too articulate, too thematic, breaking the flat-affect rule. The line "There's nowhere that's not here" is the kind of pseudo-profound statement that almost works but tips slightly into forced profundity. The "People see what they expect" exchange is good but the explanation that follows ("She expected a tired babysitter") slightly over-explains what the scene already showed. The chapter follows its plan closely: Marsh parents calling to extend their trip is replaced by them being present (a deviation - the plan had them calling, not present), which actually works better dramatically. The detective's phone ringing is included. Ruby's portrait of Sarah is included. The ending mid-sentence is executed. The pancakes, the teeth count, the new babysitting texts - all present. The violence's aftermath is handled with the same flat register as the violence itself, which is the story's core achievement. The chapter doesn't moralize, doesn't provide catharsis, doesn't let anyone off the hook. The Marshes are pathetic rather than sympathetic. Sarah is neither triumphant nor troubled. Minor weaknesses: "Sweet. Rotten. Like the others but fresher" is a slight purple moment. The runner "started to smell" description is fine but the elaboration ("Sweet. Rotten.") edges toward atmospheric posturing. The "Everyone's trapped" line is the chapter's most overtly thematic moment and feels slightly out of character for Sarah's pure behaviorism. Overall, this is a disciplined, effective execution of a genuinely unusual creative vision. The voice is consistent, the characters are true to their profiles, and the chapter achieves its intended effect of making the mundane and the monstrous indistinguishable. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 12 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely distinctive piece of pulp fiction that commits hard to its aesthetic and largely succeeds. The story follows a babysitter named Sarah who dispatches a series of criminals with cold efficiency while two deeply strange children—Theo, a teeth-collecting amateur taxidermist, and Ruby, a prophetic artist—assist or observe. The tone is deadpan ultraviolence in the vein of early Cormac McCarthy crossed with a Tarantino script, and the model executes this register with considerable skill. STRENGTHS: The voice is the piece's greatest achievement. The flat, declarative sentences ("It's Friday." "Wet." "Probably.") create a genuinely unsettling effect that feels earned rather than affected. The minimalism is consistent and purposeful—it mirrors Sarah's hollowness and creates tonal contrast with the extreme content. This is not easy to sustain across eight chapters, and the model largely manages it. The children are the story's most creative element. Theo's teeth collection and Ruby's prophetic drawings are genuinely original conceits that serve both plot and atmosphere. Ruby's drawings functioning as foreshadowing is clever and well-integrated. Theo's clinical curiosity about death and dismemberment is disturbing in a way that feels purposeful rather than gratuitous. The dialogue is often excellent. The exchange "What did it feel like?" / "Wet." / "Was it hard?" / "No." is perfectly calibrated. The Jehovah's Witness scene is a small gem of dark comedy. The Marcus negotiation scene works well. The pacing in the early chapters is strong. Chapter 1 builds dread effectively through accumulation of strange details. The reveal of the men is well-timed. WEAKNESSES: The piece runs out of steam around Chapter 5-6. The escalation from three criminals to four more to the parents to four more becomes repetitive. Each new wave of antagonists follows the same pattern: they arrive, they threaten, they die. The nail gun sequence in Chapter 4 is essentially a repeat of the scissors/toilet sequence in Chapter 3. The story's structure is additive rather than escalating in any meaningful dramatic sense. The parents' arrival in Chapter 7 is the most interesting development—it recontextualizes the whole night—but it's handled too quickly and the Marshes are underdeveloped. Their revelation as criminals is told rather than shown, and their collapse is rushed. Some metaphors don't land: "Like a TV turning off" for death is clichéd. "Like a worm" for crawling is weak. "Like aggressive pillows" for airbags is trying too hard. The piece occasionally reaches for poetry and misses—"The TV light makes shadows on the walls. They look like teeth" is the kind of line that sounds good but doesn't quite mean anything. The story ends mid-sentence, which is either a deliberate artistic choice (Ruby's drawing of Sarah leaving, the house is...) or a generation cutoff. Given the context, it's likely a cutoff, which is a significant flaw in a piece that otherwise shows strong craft. The "Unearned Transformations" question is interesting here: Sarah is never explained, and the story seems to deliberately refuse explanation ("I don't know. But you know. Yeah. That doesn't make sense. Most things don't."). This works as a stylistic choice but also means there's no character arc whatsoever. Sarah is the same at the end as the beginning. This is intentional but limits emotional engagement. The piece's darkness is well-earned in the sense that it's consistent and purposeful, but the children's participation in dismemberment and murder (Theo using pliers on a living man, Ruby apparently killing two men with children's scissors) pushes into territory that the story doesn't quite reckon with. It treats child violence as cool rather than disturbing, which is a tonal miscalculation. The world-building is thin but appropriately so for pulp fiction—the Marsh household is sketched with enough detail to feel real. OVERALL: A strong, distinctive piece with a genuinely original voice that loses momentum in its second half and ends abruptly. The best pulp fiction in this register (think early Barry or Westlake) has more structural discipline. This is impressive but unfinished. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 11 Nuanced Characters: 10 Pacing: 12 World Building: 13 Compelling Plot: 12 Emotionally Engaging: 13 Coherent: 13 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 5 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 8 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17
Avg Final Item Score: 66.4/100
+ Queer Neo-Noir Romance — Cigarette Smoke & Bad Ideas (Chapter Avg: 76.8 | Final: 74.3)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas.
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Queer Neo-Noir Romance Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas. -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Character Concepts - **The Narrator**: Could be a private investigator, bartender, journalist, ex-cop, artist, or someone trying to go straight after a criminal past. Gender-fluid, trans man, cis man - various possibilities. Someone who thinks they know better but gets pulled in anyway. - **"He" (The Love Interest)**: The dangerous one. Could be a criminal, a corrupt cop, a nightclub owner with mob ties, an art forger, a con artist, or someone with a mysterious past. Charming, self-destructive, magnetic. - **Supporting Cast**: Ex-lovers, criminal associates, family members who disapprove, a best friend who sees disaster coming, rival love interests, authority figures closing in. ## Setting Options - Rain-slicked city streets, 24-hour diners, underground clubs - Contemporary setting with noir aesthetics - Coastal city with fog and shipping yards - Desert city with casinos and neon - Art world backdrop - galleries, forgeries, theft - Music scene - jazz clubs, recording studios with shady dealings ## Plot Directions 1. **The Heist Romance**: Narrator gets pulled into one last job by the love interest 2. **The Investigation**: Narrator is investigating something that leads to the dangerous love interest 3. **The Double Cross**: One or both are playing the other, but real feelings develop 4. **The Redemption Arc**: Trying to save each other from their worst impulses 5. **The Fugitive Love**: On the run together from something/someone 6. **The Revenge Plot**: United by a common enemy or cause ## Tone & Style Ideas - First-person retrospective narration ("I should have known...") - Present tense immediacy with flashbacks - Hardboiled prose mixed with vulnerable romantic moments - Focus on sensory details - smoke, neon, rain, whiskey, cologne - Morally grey characters making bad choices for understandable reasons ## Story Beat Possibilities Over 8 Chapters ### Arc 1: Slow Burn Beginning - Ch 1: Meeting in a dangerous/compromising situation - Ch 2: Resistance and attraction, establishing the stakes - Ch 3: First kiss/first mistake - Ch 4: Point of no return - getting deeper into trouble - Ch 5: Temporary happiness/false safety - Ch 6: Everything falls apart - Ch 7: Desperate attempts to fix things - Ch 8: Bittersweet or tragic ending ### Arc 2: In Media Res - Ch 1: Start in the middle of danger, flashback to meeting - Ch 2-3: Alternating past/present showing how they got here - Ch 4: The job/crime/mistake that changed everything - Ch 5-6: Consequences closing in - Ch 7: Betrayal or sacrifice - Ch 8: Resolution (together or apart) ### Arc 3: Cat and Mouse - Ch 1-2: Narrator hunting/investigating the love interest - Ch 3-4: Tables turn, roles reverse - Ch 5-6: Forced to work together - Ch 7: Truth revealed - Ch 8: Choose each other or justice ## Specific Scene Ideas - Meeting at a crime scene - Sharing cigarettes on a fire escape - A chase through rain-wet streets - Tending wounds after violence - Dancing in an empty bar after hours - Loading guns while arguing about feelings - A motel room confession - Saying goodbye at a train station/airport - One visiting the other in prison ## Ending Options - Tragic: One dies saving the other - Bittersweet: They part for each other's safety - Hopeful: They escape together but future uncertain - Noir twist: The narrator was playing him all along - Redemptive: They choose love over crime # Reflection Looking at these ideas, I'm drawn to a combination of elements that would create the most compelling narrative tension while staying true to the neo-noir romance genre. **What works:** - The contrast between a narrator who's trying to live a cleaner life and a love interest who embodies danger is classic noir with great romantic tension - Setting it in the contemporary art world could provide fresh angles while maintaining noir aesthetics - forgeries, wealthy collectors, underground galleries - First-person retrospective narration would let us feel the narrator's regret and longing while building suspense about what went wrong - A structure that alternates between their developing relationship and a present-tense frame where everything has already fallen apart would create natural suspense **What might not work:** - Pure cop/criminal dynamics might feel too familiar - Ending with death might be too tragic for a romance, even noir romance - Starting with them already together removes the pleasure of watching them fall for each other - Too much focus on crime plot could overshadow the romance **Most compelling direction:** I'm leaning toward a story about a narrator who's trying to go straight (maybe an ex-forger now working as a legitimate art restorer) who gets pulled back into the criminal world by a dangerously charming art thief. The art world setting provides glamour and sophistication while maintaining noir's criminal elements. The 8-chapter structure would allow for a slow burn romance while building toward a heist that goes wrong, forcing them to choose between self-preservation and love. The key is balancing the noir elements (moral ambiguity, stylized prose, criminal activity) with genuine romantic development and queer themes (chosen family, outsider status, the additional dangers of being visibly queer in dangerous situations).
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention I will write a queer neo-noir romance set in the contemporary art world, told in first-person retrospective narration by Jamie, a reformed art forger now working as a legitimate restorer who gets pulled back into crime by Mikhail, a dangerously charismatic art thief. **Voice & Style:** The prose will be hardboiled but intimate, mixing noir's cynical edge with vulnerable romanticism. I'll use sensory details heavily—cigarette smoke, turpentine, rain on gallery windows, expensive cologne mixing with leather. The retrospective frame allows for both regret and tenderness: "I knew what he was the moment he walked into my studio, but knowing and caring are different beasts." **Structure:** Each chapter will blend present-tense scenes of their relationship developing with hints of a frame narrative where Jamie is dealing with the aftermath of their story. This creates dual tension—we're watching them fall in love while knowing something went wrong. **Plot Focus:** The story centers on Jamie being drawn into "one last job"—stealing a painting from a dangerous collector who destroyed Mikhail's mentor. The heist plot serves the romance, not vice versa. Their relationship is the core conflict: Jamie choosing between the safety of their reformed life and their consuming attraction to Mikhail. **Themes to Explore:** The additional vulnerabilities of being queer in criminal spaces, choosing chosen family over blood family, the intoxication of being seen and wanted by someone dangerous, redemption vs. corruption, how love makes us both stronger and weaker. **What I'll Avoid:** Death as ending (too tragic for romance), pure victim/predator dynamics (both characters have agency), excessive focus on crime mechanics over emotional development, coming out trauma (these characters are comfortable in their queerness), heteronormative relationship dynamics. **Ending Intention:** Bittersweet but hopeful—they must part for their mutual safety but there's a suggestion they'll find their way back to each other when the heat dies down. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "Turpentine and Smoke" (1000 words)** Jamie works late in their restoration studio when Mikhail appears with a damaged stolen painting, seeking help. Sexual tension immediate but Jamie refuses. Establishes Jamie's reformed life, their expertise, and Mikhail's dangerous charm. Frame hints: Jamie is now alone, packing up the studio. **Chapter 2: "The Devil's Persistence" (1000 words)** Mikhail keeps appearing—at Jamie's coffee shop, outside their apartment, smoking on their fire escape. Each encounter chips at Jamie's resistance. We learn Jamie's past: their mentor/ex Leo is in prison because of a job gone wrong. Mikhail reveals he needs Jamie specifically—only they can forge a convincing copy of a particular artist. Frame: Jamie burns old forgeries. **Chapter 3: "Weak Creatures" (1000 words)** Jamie agrees to hear Mikhail out. Over drinks in a underground queer bar, Mikhail reveals the target: Victor Castellano, who killed Mikhail's mentor and now owns the painting. First kiss in the alley behind the bar, Jamie pulling Mikhail against the brick wall. They go to Jamie's apartment but Jamie stops before sex—not until they decide about the job. Frame: Jamie's best friend Chen warns them the cops are asking questions. **Chapter 4: "The Perfect Mark" (1000 words)** Jamie agrees to the job but sets boundaries—just the forgery, no active participation in theft. Working together in the studio, creating the fake. Intimate moments: Mikhail watching Jamie paint, sharing cigarettes, hands touching over brushes. They sleep together, tender but desperate. Jamie realizes they're already in too deep. Frame: Jamie discovers Mikhail left them something. **Chapter 5: "What We Deserve" (1000 words)** Planning the heist while navigating their intensifying relationship. Meeting Mikhail's crew—found family of queer criminals. Jamie feels seduced not just by Mikhail but by belonging. Mikhail reveals more vulnerability: Castellano has leverage over him. Jamie decides to participate fully in the heist, not just create the forgery. Frame: Jamie visits someone in prison. **Chapter 6: "Beautiful Disasters" (1000 words)** The heist begins. Everything goes perfectly until it doesn't—Castellano was expecting them. Violence erupts. Jamie must choose between escape and saving Mikhail. They choose Mikhail, getting them both out but not unscathed. Blood and confessions in a safe house bathroom, stitching wounds and admitting love. Frame: Jamie testifies somewhere official. **Chapter 7: "The Price of Smoke" (1000 words)** Castellano's reach is longer than expected. They're being hunted. Jamie must use old criminal contacts, burning their clean identity. Chen offers them a way out but only for one person. Mikhail tries to sacrifice himself; Jamie refuses. They make a desperate plan to turn Castellano over to the FBI in exchange for immunity. Frame: Jamie says goodbye to the studio. **Chapter 8: "After the Fire" (1000 words)** The deal works but requires them to separate—witness protection for Jamie, Mikhail takes the fall for the crew but gets reduced sentence. Their last night together before Jamie disappears. Two years later: Jamie has a new name, new city, new studio. A customer comes in with a damaged painting. "Hello, stranger," Mikhail says, cigarette between his lips, free and finding them against all odds. They've both paid their prices. Now they can choose each other freely.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer would likely start with **visceral personal experience**—maybe a specific person who was bad for them, the actual smell of cigarettes on someone's jacket, the real feeling of making terrible decisions at 3 AM. They'd probably write from the gut first, not worrying about structure. The prompt "He was all cigarette smoke and bad ideas" would evoke a specific memory or person, and they'd chase that feeling. Humans would also bring **subcultural authenticity**—real queer spaces, not generic "underground queer bars." They'd know whether their characters go to leather bars or drag bars, whether they say "partner" or "boyfriend," the specific anxieties of being visibly queer in different contexts. They'd include messy insider details that can't be researched. Most importantly, humans write with **genuine horniness and hunger**. Not euphemistic "sexual tension" but the actual physical want—the specific way someone's hands look holding a cigarette, how their mouth tastes like smoke and whiskey, the particular vulnerability of wanting someone you shouldn't. # Common LLM Failings That Don't Serve This Piece **1. Sanitized Danger:** LLMs make "bad boys" safely bad—they smoke and have mysterious pasts but never do anything genuinely morally compromising. Real noir needs characters who do actual bad things for understandable reasons. **2. Therapy-Speak Emotional Processing:** LLMs have characters understand their feelings too clearly. Real people in toxic relationships don't think "I'm choosing between safety and desire"—they just show up at the person's apartment at 2 AM. **3. Over-Structured Plot:** LLMs love clear three-act structures with setup/payoff. Real noir is messier—threads that go nowhere, violence that erupts from nowhere, resolutions that don't resolve. **4. Purple Prose Syndrome:** "Sexual tension immediate but Jamie refuses" / "tender but desperate"—LLMs reach for literary-sounding abstractions instead of specific, bodily details. **5. Representative Queerness:** LLMs include queerness as identity markers rather than lived experience. Real queer stories include the specific exhaustions, inside jokes, and survival strategies of queer life. # Deep Critique of My Plan **The Frame Device:** This is such an LLM move—adding structural complexity to seem sophisticated. A human might just write it chronologically and trust the reader to feel the doom approaching. The frame creates distance when this story needs immediacy. **Jamie the Reformed Forger:** This is too neat—a criminal with a heart of gold who's trying to go straight. Humans would make Jamie messier. Maybe they're still forging, just lying to themselves about it. Maybe they miss crime more than they admit. **The Heist Plot:** I've made it too noble—avenging mentors, taking down bad guys. Real criminals steal because they want money or because they're addicted to the thrill. The righteousness makes it too safe. **The Romance Arc:** It's too balanced and mutual. Real toxic attraction is usually uneven—someone who loves too much, someone who can't love enough. The push-pull should be more jagged. **That Ending:** "They've both paid their prices. Now they can choose each other freely." This is LLM redemption brain—everything resolved, lessons learned. Real noir romance would end with them choosing each other DESPITE nothing being resolved. **The Sex:** "They sleep together, tender but desperate"—this is LLM euphemism. A human would write the actual sex or skip it entirely, not gesture vaguely at it. # Ideas to Make It More Human **Voice Improvements:** - Start with a specific sensory memory: "He tasted like Parliament Lights and poor decisions" - Include contradictions: Jamie saying they're done with crime while actively planning crimes - Add mundane specifics: arguing while one person tries to parallel park, sex interrupted by a neighbor's music - Real dialogue with interruptions, people talking past each other, inside jokes we don't fully understand **Character Mess:** - Jamie is still forging, just small stuff, telling themselves it doesn't count - Mikhail isn't mysterious—he's transparent about being bad news, which is worse - They've definitely fucked before the story starts—in a bathroom at someone's opening, both pretending it didn't happen - Jamie's "reformed life" includes selling coke to art students **Plot Adjustments:** - No noble mission—Mikhail just wants to steal something expensive - The heist goes wrong because Jamie and Mikhail are too busy fucking to notice obvious problems - No FBI deals—just messy, unsatisfying choices - They don't learn anything or grow—they just choose each other anyway **Queer Specificity:** - Include real community dynamics—exes who are now friends, everyone having slept with everyone - Specific gay male culture: apps, PrEP anxiety, generational trauma - The particular vulnerability of being clockable criminals AND visibly queer - Inside references—Grindr notifications during crimes, jokes about U-Hauls but for gay men **Tonal Shifts:** - Less "hardboiled poetry," more "tired and horny" - Include boring moments—waiting for paint to dry, bad coffee, scrolling phones while planning crimes - Let funny moments be actually funny, not just wry - Real arguments about nothing that are actually about everything **Structural Changes:** - Drop the frame or make it weirder—maybe Jamie is telling this story to someone specific for a reason - Let chapters be uneven—one might be just one long sex scene, another might be three days compressed - Include genuinely unclear moments—did that really happen or is Jamie lying to us? - End mid-sentence, mid-scene—not everything gets wrapped up **Physical Reality:** - Specific body details: Mikhail's particular way of holding cigarettes between his middle and ring finger - Real sex logistics: lube, positions, who does what - Actual violence consequences: concussions that last weeks, hands that shake after fights - Mundane body stuff: someone's lactose intolerant, someone needs reading glasses but won't admit it The key is embracing the messiness, the horniness, the bad decisions that feel good, the specific subcultural knowledge, and the moral ambiguity without redemption. Less structure, more feeling. Less explanation, more action. Less safety, more genuine danger—emotional and physical.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This is a story about knowing someone will ruin your life and fucking them anyway. Jamie isn't reformed—they're just tired and lying about it, still doing small forgeries while pretending to restore paintings. Mikhail doesn't seduce Jamie back to crime; Jamie was always criminal, just bored. **Voice:** First-person present tense, immediate and hungry. Not hardboiled poetry but exhausted horniness. Jamie notices bodies first—Mikhail's hands, his mouth, the way he takes up space. Short sentences when things matter, run-ons when Jamie's lying to themselves. Specific sensory details: Parliament Lights, oil paint under fingernails, cum and turpentine, the specific weight of guilt at 4 AM. **Structure:** Linear but jagged. Some chapters are single scenes, others skip weeks. No frame device—we're in it as it happens, making the same bad decisions. Each chapter ends mid-thought or mid-action, pulling us forward. **What I'm Avoiding:** Redemption arcs, mutual healing, clear moral lines, euphemistic sex, generic queerness, anyone learning anything, structured heist plotting, noble motivations. **What I'm Aiming For:** The specific exhaustion of fucking someone you've already fucked before, knowing how it ends. Bad sex that's still hot. Real dialogue where people don't say what they mean. Specific gay male culture—PrEP paranoia, everyone knowing everyone, the particular danger of being clockable. Crime that's boring until it's not. Love that doesn't fix anything. **The Core:** Mikhail is exactly what the prompt says—cigarette smoke and bad ideas. Jamie knows this, has always known this, chooses him anyway because being ruined by him is better than being safe without him. # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "Parliament Lights" (1000 words)** Jamie's in their studio at 2 AM, doing a "restoration" that's actually forgery, when Mikhail lets himself in with the key he kept from when they were fucking three years ago. He needs a Basquiat copied. Jamie's already saying yes while insisting they're saying no. They fuck against the desk, quick and angry. Mikhail tastes exactly the same—Parliament Lights and that specific cologne from duty-free. He leaves before Jamie finishes. Jamie goes back to painting. **Chapter 2: "Thursday Nights" (1000 words)** Jamie at their regular bar, the one where the bartender's their ex and everyone knows everyone's business. Mikhail shows up because of course he does—this was always their place. Three shots in, Jamie's explaining why they can't do this again while already planning to leave with him. Mikhail's honest: "I'm going to fuck up your life again." Jamie: "I know." They don't leave together but Jamie goes to his apartment anyway. The sex is better than Jamie remembered, which is worse. **Chapter 3: "The Actual Job" (1000 words)** Two weeks later, Jamie's made three Basquiats. Mikhail explains the real job over post-fuck cigarettes: steal from Victor Castellano, who owns half the galleries in Chelsea and definitely killed someone in Miami in the 90s. Jamie's not even pretending to refuse anymore. They meet Mikhail's crew in a warehouse—his ex Andre who still loves him, some tweaker kid named Pilot, a trans woman named Rosa who's the only professional. Everyone can tell Jamie and Mikhail are fucking. Everyone knows it's a bad idea. **Chapter 4: "Interlude: Grindr" (1000 words)** Just a normal Tuesday. Jamie trying to work while Mikhail's on Grindr next to them, occasionally showing them pics. They're not together-together. Jamie's still on PrEP from before Mikhail, stays on it after. They fight about nothing—whose turn to buy cigarettes, why Mikhail leaves the window open. Fuck again. Mikhail mentions his father's dying. Jamie doesn't know how to respond so they don't. That night Jamie paints Mikhail sleeping, catches the specific angle of his jaw. Deletes it immediately. **Chapter 5: "Castellano's Gallery" (1000 words)** The opening where they'll case the place. Jamie in a suit that costs more than their rent, Mikhail in leather because he doesn't care about blending in. Castellano hits on Jamie, hand on their lower back. Mikhail watches from across the room, not jealous, just calculating. Later, doing coke in Castellano's office bathroom while he's giving a speech. Mikhail's explaining guard rotations while Jamie's thinking about how his mouth tastes metallic. They fuck in the bathroom, Jamie watching themselves in the mirror thinking: this is it, this is how I die. **Chapter 6: "It Goes Wrong" (1000 words)** The heist, but told in fragments. Andre's jealousy making him sloppy. The tweaker kid OD'ing in the van. Rosa trying to hold it together. Jamie realizing Mikhail's been lying about something fundamental—he's not stealing from Castellano, he's stealing FOR him. The sick realization that Jamie's been played, maybe, or Mikhail's been played, or everyone's playing everyone. Violence erupts. Not choreographed, just messy and awful. Someone Jamie didn't expect to die dies. **Chapter 7: "After" (1000 words)** Jamie and Mikhail in a motel off I-95, Andre's blood still under their fingernails. They're not talking about what happened. Watching local news, waiting to see their faces. Mikhail's smoking in bed, dropping ash on the polyester comforter. Jamie wants to ask if any of it was real but knows better. They fuck anyway, desperate and guilty. Mikhail cries after, won't say why. Jamie counts their remaining cash, calculates how far it'll get them. Not far enough. **Chapter 8: "Cigarette Smoke" (1000 words)** Six months later. Jamie's in Portland or Minneapolis, somewhere with different light. Still forging, not even pretending otherwise. New name, same habits. Working on a Rothko when they smell it—Parliament Lights in the hallway. Could be anyone. Jamie doesn't look up when the door opens. Mikhail doesn't apologize or explain. Just: "I brought coffee." They don't talk about Andre or the money or who betrayed who. Mikhail's got new scars. Jamie's hands shake now, makes the forgeries harder. They drink the coffee. Mikhail lights another cigarette. Jamie doesn't ask him to stay. Doesn't ask him to leave. The painting can wait.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Jamie Chen Jamie's thirty-four and been forging since art school, learned from their ex Leo who's doing seven years in Rikers for a Monet that Jamie actually painted. Half-Chinese, half-Irish, grew up in Flushing where their dad still thinks they're a graphic designer. Started testosterone five years ago, stopped last year, started again last month—can't decide if they want to pass or just want to want something. They're brilliant at forgery because they can't make original work—tried for years, every canvas came out looking like someone else's. Tells themselves they restore paintings but hasn't done legitimate work in eighteen months. Addicted to the focus that comes with matching someone else's brushstrokes, the meditative emptiness of becoming nobody. Smokes weed every night to sleep, grinds their teeth, has a stress fracture in their right hand from punching a wall after Leo's sentencing. **Physicality:** 5'7", soft around the middle from takeout and irregular T cycles. Perpetual paint under their nails, even after scrubbing. Terrible posture from hunching over canvases. Wears the same three black t-shirts in rotation, expensive jeans that are paint-splattered, Red Wing boots they've had since 2015. Chest surgery scars they touch when nervous. Moves economically in their studio, wastefully everywhere else. **Motives/Relationships:** Sends half their forgery money to Leo's commissary, the other half to their sister who thinks they're still at Christie's. Still fucks their ex from art school, Marcus, every few months when lonely enough. Their dealer Chen (no relation) is their only real friend, knows about the forgeries, keeps trying to get them to try therapy. Jamie wants to want normal things—stability, safety, a boyfriend who texts back—but really just wants to be left alone to paint and not think about why they can't stop lying. **Voice:** "I'm not drinking tonight. I'm not fucking drinking tonight. Okay, one drink, but I'm not—fuck." "The thing about Basquiat is he was angry at the right things. I'm just angry at myself, which makes copying him feel like borrowing someone else's justified rage." # Mikhail Volkov Mikhail's thirty-seven, been stealing since he was fourteen and his father needed money for dialysis. Russian-Jewish, grew up in Brighton Beach where his mom still lives in the same apartment, thinks he works in insurance. His dad died anyway, slow and angry, teaching Mikhail that being good doesn't mean you get good things. He's not a mastermind, just smart enough and lucky enough and willing to do things other people won't. Steals art because it's light and expensive, not from any aesthetic appreciation. Can't tell a Monet from a Manet but knows what they're worth. Has a tremor in his left hand from getting jumped in 2018, hides it by always holding something—cigarette, coffee, someone's wrist. **Physicality:** 6'1", rangy in that way where you can see his hip bones. Premature grey at the temples that he'd dye if he cared. Sharp features that photograph badly but look good in person. Wears designer clothes badly—expensive shirts untucked, Prada boots that are scuffed to shit. Moves like he's always leaving, even when arriving. Cigarette burns on his fingers from nodding off high. When nervous, traces old track marks on his forearms that have faded but not disappeared. **Motives/Relationships:** Owes Castellano forty thousand from a job that went bad, interest accumulating. His ex Andre still loves him but Mikhail only fucks people he can leave. Sends his mom flowers every Friday, calls her every Sunday, lies about everything. Has a half-sister in Miami he's met twice. Doesn't want things, just wants to stop owing them. The only time he's not calculating exits is when he's high or fucking, which is why he does both too much. **Voice:** "I'm not asking you to trust me. I'm asking you to make bad decisions with me. There's a difference." "My father used to say the only difference between a con man and a businessman is the businessman has better lawyers. Then he died broke anyway, so what the fuck did he know." # Andre Kozlov Andre's twenty-eight, Mikhail's ex who can't let go. Beautiful in that way that makes people stupid, including himself. Byelorussian, overstayed his student visa, can't go back. Met Mikhail at a warehouse party in Bushwick three years ago, fell in love with how Mikhail didn't give a shit about his beauty. Good at theft because people don't suspect someone who looks like a model. But getting sloppy—doing too much coke, showing up to jobs high, posting Instagram stories from places they've robbed. Jealous in the way that makes him mean. Knows four languages but can't say what he needs in any of them. **Physicality:** 5'11", built like a dancer because he was one until he tore his ACL. Cheekbones that could cut glass, lips that make straight men uncomfortable. Bleaches his hair every three weeks, dark roots always showing. Moves like he's performing even when alone. Beautiful hands that shake now from the coke. **Motives/Relationships:** Wants Mikhail back but would settle for Mikhail dead. Hates Jamie with the specific venom of someone who recognizes themselves. His mother in Minsk doesn't know he's gay, sends him money he doesn't need. Fucks older men who look nothing like Mikhail, cries after. **Voice:** "You think you're special because he came back to you? He comes back to everyone. The cigarettes, the stray cats, the bad ideas. He always comes back until he doesn't." # Rosa Martinez Rosa's forty-one, the only professional in this mess. Trans woman who transitioned fifteen years ago when it was harder and lonelier. Does this because she's good at it and the money pays for her life—hormones, surgeries, the apartment where she feels safe. Started as a safecracker, moved into planning because she got tired of being the one taking the actual risk. Smarter than everyone else in the room, patient enough not to mention it. Has a girlfriend who thinks she's in private security, which isn't entirely a lie. **Physicality:** 5'9", solid muscle under feminine curves. Surgical scars she paid extra to minimize. Wears business casual like armor—blazers, silk blouses, pants with sharp creases. Hair in a perpetual bun. Carries herself like someone who's fought for every inch of space she takes up. **Motives/Relationships:** Saving to disappear—has three passports, four bank accounts, an exit strategy for every situation. Loyal to Mikhail because he never misgendered her, not even at the beginning. Thinks Jamie's an amateur, Andre's a liability, but works with them anyway because Mikhail asks. **Voice:** "The problem with men who think they're dangerous is they never notice the actual danger in the room." "I don't do this for the thrill. I do this so I can stop doing this. There's a difference, not that any of you would understand."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Parliament Lights The lock turns at 2:47 AM and I don't even look up from the canvas. Only one person still has a key, and he'd never knock. "You changed the brand," Mikhail says, meaning the turpentine. He's right. I switched to Weber because it smells less like poison, more like pine trees, as if that matters when I'm huffing it six hours a day. I keep working on the Motherwell I'm "restoring." Actually painting a new one from scratch because the original's in some oligarch's panic room, but the insurance company doesn't need to know that. My hand's steady on the black curve, the zinc white going down clean. I'm good at this. At becoming nobody. "Jamie." "Working." "I need a favor." "No." He laughs, that specific exhale that's not quite humor. I hear his lighter click. Still the same Zippo with the dent from when he threw it at my head three years ago. Missed, hit the wall, we fucked against that same wall twenty minutes later. "You can't smoke in here," I say, still not looking up. "The paintings—" "Fake paintings." "Restored paintings." "Sure, baby." The smoke hits me before I cave and turn around. Parliament Lights, same as always. He looks like shit, which means he looks good—hollow cheeks making his face sharper, three-day stubble, that grey at his temples spreading. Leather jacket that costs more than most people's rent, t-shirt that's either designer or from Target. With Mikhail you can never tell what's calculated. "Basquiat," he says. "Just one." "No." "The Skull piece. 1981. You love that one." I do love that one. Painted a perfect copy two years ago for Leo, right before his arrest. The skull grins at you like it knows what you did last night, what you'll do tomorrow. "Get out." He doesn't move. Takes another drag, ashes into my coffee mug from this morning. Or yesterday morning. Time stops meaning things when you paint through it. "Three days," he says. "Fifty thousand." "I don't need money." "Everyone needs money." "I need you to leave." But I'm already calculating—how long for the underlayers, whether I have the right cadmium red. Basquiat's reds are specific, angry in a way that takes practice to replicate. My hand's still on the brush but I've stopped painting. Mikhail moves closer. I can smell him now under the cigarette smoke—that cologne he gets at duty-free, something French and expensive that shouldn't work with Parliament Lights but does. Sweat underneath it. He's nervous, which means this is about more than money. "Who wants it?" "Does it matter?" "It does if you're nervous." His left hand trembles, just slightly. The old tell. He covers it by taking another drag. "Castellano." "Fuck no." "Jamie—" "He collects fingers, Mikhail. Actual human fingers. Has them in jars in his office like fucking pickles." "Rumors." "You told me that. You literally told me about the fingers." He's close enough now that I can see the burst blood vessel in his left eye, the scar on his jaw from some fight he never explained. Close enough that muscle memory kicks in—three years since we fucked and my body still knows exactly how far to lean to meet his mouth. "I don't do this anymore," I say, but I'm already stepping back, letting him follow. Classic Jamie, saying no while my body says yes, yes, fuck, yes. "You're literally doing it right now." He gestures at the Motherwell. "Unless that's suddenly real?" "That's different." "How?" I don't have an answer that isn't a lie, so I kiss him instead. Tastes exactly the same—Parliament Lights and that metallic tang from coke drip. His hand goes to my throat, not squeezing, just holding. Possessive in that way that made me lose three years to him. We fuck against the desk, quick and angry and not quite enough prep but I don't care. His jacket stays on. My pants around my ankles, palette knife still in my left hand getting paint on his shoulder. He's saying something in Russian I don't understand and don't need to. I know what this is. What we are. He comes first, pulls out immediately. Doesn't finish me, just zips up while I'm standing there exposed, hard, furious. "Three days," he says. Lights another cigarette. "I'll send the specs." "I didn't say yes." He looks at me—pants around my ankles, his cum dripping down my thigh, already planning which canvas to use—and almost smiles. "Sure, baby." The door closes. The lock turns. I stand there another minute, cock going soft, hating him and myself in equal measure. Then I pull up my pants, wake my laptop, start researching Basquiat's 1981 period. The Motherwell can wait. It's not going anywhere. Neither am I. I make new coffee, roll a joint, put on the playlist that's all songs Mikhail showed me that I pretend to hate. Text Marcus that I can't see him this week, something's come up. Text my sister I'm fine, work's good, stop worrying. The skull's already forming in my mind—the way Basquiat let paint drip because perfection was another kind of lie. I can give Mikhail that. I can make something so real it becomes true. That's all I'm good for anyway.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me work through the key elements. **Voice and Style:** The first-person present tense is immediate and effective. The prose has genuine texture—"becoming nobody," the Zippo with the dent from when he threw it at Jamie's head, the burst blood vessel in Mikhail's eye. These are specific, earned details rather than generic noir atmosphere. The rhythm shifts between short declarative sentences and longer run-ons work well, particularly in the sex scene where Jamie's self-deception accelerates. The plan called for "exhausted horniness" rather than "hardboiled poetry," and the chapter mostly achieves this. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is genuinely good. "Sure, baby" as a repeated beat lands both times. The exchange about the turpentine brand as an opener is clever—it establishes intimacy and surveillance without stating either. "He collects fingers, Mikhail. Actual human fingers. Has them in jars in his office like fucking pickles" is funny and character-revealing simultaneously. The dialogue avoids the trap of people saying what they mean while still communicating clearly to the reader. **Character Consistency:** Jamie is well-rendered—the self-deception is shown rather than told, the body betraying the stated refusals. Mikhail's nervousness revealed through the trembling hand, the tell he covers with a cigarette drag, is economical and effective. Both characters match their profiles. **Where It Falters:** A few moments tip toward the expected. "Tastes exactly the same—Parliament Lights and that metallic tang from coke drip" is solid, but "His hand goes to my throat, not squeezing, just holding. Possessive in that way that made me lose three years to him" is slightly on-the-nose—the explanation of what the gesture means undercuts the showing. The ending paragraph with the playlist and texts to Marcus and his sister is functional but slightly mechanical, a checklist of character details from the profile being deployed. The line "I can make something so real it becomes true" is the chapter's weakest moment—it reaches for profundity and lands on a cliché about forgery-as-metaphor that the rest of the chapter is smart enough to avoid stating directly. **Sex Scene:** The plan called for "bad sex that's still hot" and the chapter delivers—the quick anger, the incomplete finish, Mikhail zipping up while Jamie's still exposed. This is genuinely uncomfortable and revealing without being gratuitous or euphemistic. **Followed Plan:** The chapter hits all its marks: 2 AM studio, Mikhail with the key, Basquiat request, fucking against the desk, Mikhail leaving before Jamie finishes, Jamie going back to painting. The Castellano mention is included. The chapter ends mid-action as planned. **Purple Prose/Forced Metaphor:** Largely avoided. The prose is controlled. "Hollow cheeks making his face sharper" is good. The Basquiat skull description ("grins at you like it knows what you did last night") is slightly overwrought but earns its place given the context. **Overall:** This is accomplished work. It's not a masterpiece—the ending reaches slightly too hard, and there are a few moments where the author explains what they've already shown—but it's a confident, specific, genuinely noir chapter that avoids most of the genre's pitfalls. The queerness is specific rather than decorative. The crime is mundane. The desire is real. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Thursday Nights Thursday at Marie's, which hasn't been called Marie's since 2009 but nobody calls it The Deep End because that's a stupid fucking name for a bar. Marcus is bartending, because of course he is. My ex who I still fuck sometimes, pouring drinks for my ex who I can't stop fucking. "You look like shit," Marcus says, sliding me a whiskey. Jameson, neat, because he knows me better than I'd like. "Thanks. Love you too." "He's back, isn't he?" I don't answer. Don't need to. The whole bar probably knows—Chelsea queers gossip like it's cardio. Marcus tops off my glass without asking, his wedding ring catching the light. Yeah, he got married. Yeah, we still fuck. His husband travels for work, don't ask. Three drinks in, I'm explaining to this twink named Connor why Basquiat wasn't a graffiti artist when I smell Parliament Lights. Not the old stale smoke that clings to everything in here, but fresh. Current. Him. "You said Thursdays were safe," I tell Marcus when Mikhail sits two stools down, orders vodka neat like the Russian stereotype he pretends not to be. "You said you were done with him." "I am." "Sure, honey." The twink's still talking about street art but I've stopped listening. Mikhail's watching the Knicks lose on the TV above the bar, acting like he doesn't know I'm here. His jacket's different from two nights ago. This one's wool, expensive, with a rip in the lining he probably doesn't know about. Or maybe he does. Maybe everything's calculated, even the flaws. "—don't you think?" the twink asks. "What?" "That commodification ruined authenticity." "Everything ruins authenticity," I say. "That's what authenticity is for." Mikhail snorts. The twink looks confused, starts to say something else, but then sees the way I'm not looking at Mikhail and gets it. Everyone always gets it. He mumbles about finding his friends and disappears. "You scared off your admirer," Mikhail says, still watching the game. "He's twenty-three." "So were you once." "Yeah, and look how that turned out." Now he looks at me. Really looks. Takes in the paint still under my nails, the three-day-old shirt, the way I'm drinking like it's my job. "You're painting it." "No." "You're a terrible liar." "I'm an excellent liar. I'm just not lying to you." Marcus refills Mikhail's vodka, gives me a look that says *do you need me to kick him out?* I shake my head. Marcus sighs, moves to the other end of the bar where Andre's holding court, all cheekbones and cocaine confidence, pretending he doesn't see Mikhail. This bar's too small for all our histories. "I'm going to fuck up your life again," Mikhail says. Not a warning, just a fact. Like telling me it might rain. "I know." "I'm not good for you." "I know." "You should tell me to leave." "Leave." He doesn't move. Neither do I. The Knicks are down by twelve. Someone's playing Mitski on the jukebox, which should be illegal. Marcus is watering down Andre's drinks, good for him. "I have the specs," Mikhail says. "At my place." "Email them." "You should see the source material." "I've seen the original." "In person?" No. In books, online, in my dreams sometimes. But admitting that feels like losing. "I'm not going to your apartment." "Okay." He pays his tab, leaves Marcus a twenty percent tip like he's not a criminal. Puts on his coat, that rip in the lining gaping. I think about telling him. Don't. "See you around, Jamie." "No you won't." He's almost at the door when I catch up. Not following, just leaving at the same time. Pure coincidence that I turn the same direction, walk the same three blocks, climb the same four flights to his studio that still smells like cigarettes and oil paint from when I used to work here too. "You kept the couch," I say. "You loved that couch." I did. Ugly green velvet thing we found on the street, carried up together sweating and laughing. We fucked on it so many times the left cushion sags. He hands me a folder—photos of the Basquiat, providence documents, measurements. I pretend to study them but I'm already thinking about his mouth, the way his hand shook at the bar, how his apartment feels more like home than mine. "Jamie." "I'm looking at them." "Jamie." The papers fall. We're on the couch, then the floor, then his bed that's just a mattress on the ground because he never got a frame. He takes his time now, makes me ask for it. I do. Twice. His mouth on my neck, my chest, lower. When he fucks me it's slow, deliberate, watching my face like he's memorizing it. After, he traces the surgery scars on my chest. Light touch, careful. The tenderness is worse than violence would be. "I missed this," he says. "My tits? They're gone, baby." "You." "Don't." "Okay." But his hand stays on my chest, over my heart that's beating too fast. We smoke his cigarettes. Watch the news on mute. His phone buzzes—Castellano's name flashing. He doesn't answer. "This is a mistake," I say. "Yes." "We're going to regret this." "Probably." "I hate you." "I know." I kiss him again, already tasting tomorrow's regret. He tastes better than Marcus, better than the twink whose name I forgot, better than anyone should at 3 AM on a Thursday that's technically Friday now. The Basquiat watches us from the folder on the floor, skull grinning like it knows how this ends.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of the writing plan, demonstrating genuine craft in several areas while having a few minor weaknesses worth examining. **Voice and Style:** The first-person present tense voice is consistently maintained and genuinely effective. The prose has the "exhausted horniness" quality the plan called for—it's not trying to be hardboiled poetry, it's trying to be honest about desire and self-deception. Lines like "Pure coincidence that I turn the same direction, walk the same three blocks" are doing real work: showing Jamie's self-deception without announcing it. The short sentences when things matter ("I know." "Leave." "Okay.") land well. **Dialogue:** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. The exchange "I'm going to fuck up your life again" / "I know" / "I'm not good for you" / "I know" / "You should tell me to leave" / "Leave" is genuinely good—it's people not saying what they mean while saying exactly what they mean. The Basquiat/authenticity exchange with the twink is sharp. Marcus's "Sure, honey" is economical and characterful. The dialogue avoids the trap of being too clever or too on-the-nose. **Character Consistency:** Jamie reads consistently with the profile—the self-deception, the paint under the nails, the complicated relationship with Marcus, the chest surgery scars. Mikhail's calculated flaws (the rip in the lining), his tenderness that's "worse than violence," his phone buzzing with Castellano's name—all consistent. The detail about Mikhail's hand shaking at the bar (the tremor from the profile) is well-integrated. **Sensory Specificity:** The plan called for specific sensory details, and the chapter delivers: Parliament Lights, the ugly green velvet couch, the mattress on the floor without a frame, the Basquiat skull grinning from the folder. These feel earned rather than decorative. **Weaknesses:** The "Basquiat watches us from the folder on the floor, skull grinning like it knows how this ends" closing line is the chapter's most obvious reach—it's a bit too neat, too literary, slightly purple. The plan warned against "generic queerness" and mostly avoids it, though the Marcus-married-husband-travels-for-work detail feels slightly convenient rather than fully realized. The Andre appearance is brief but functional. The twink Connor scene is efficient but Connor himself is barely a character. **Forced Poetry:** Mostly avoided. The prose doesn't overreach. "Chelsea queers gossip like it's cardio" is a good line that earns its cleverness. The ending image is the one place where the writing reaches for significance a bit too obviously. **Plot:** The chapter does what it needs to—establishes the bar dynamic, shows Jamie's capitulation, advances the Basquiat job, and deepens the emotional stakes. The plan's goal of Jamie "explaining why they can't do this again while already planning to leave with him" is executed well. **Darkness:** The tenderness after sex—"The tenderness is worse than violence would be"—is genuinely earned. The "I hate you" / "I know" exchange avoids being a Twilight reference by being grounded in the specific context. **Minor Issues:** "providence documents" should be "provenance documents"—a notable error for a story about art forgery. The Mitski jukebox line ("which should be illegal") is a bit of a cheap cultural signifier, though it's brief. Overall, this is confident, controlled writing that executes its plan faithfully and demonstrates real understanding of the characters and tone. It's not a masterpiece but it's genuinely good work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Actual Job Two weeks and I've made three Basquiats. The first one, too careful, looked like what it was—someone trying to be Basquiat. Burned it in the bathtub, set off the smoke alarm. The second, better, angrier. But the proportions were wrong, skull too centered. Basquiat never centered anything, liked his subjects falling off the canvas. The third one's perfect. So perfect I sign it. Not his signature, mine, tiny in the corner where nobody will look. Stupid, but I need some part of this to be real. "You're overthinking it," Mikhail says, ash falling on my chest. We're in his bed, afternoon light making everything look like a mistake. "Castellano won't know the difference." "Castellano owns four real ones." "So?" "So he knows what they look like." "He knows what they're worth. Different thing." He's probably right but I'm still adding another layer tonight, making the red angrier, more accusatory. The skull needs to look like it's judging you. That's what Basquiat understood—every painting is really a mirror. Mikhail lights another cigarette from the end of the first. Chain smoking means he's nervous, means this is about more than the fifty thousand. "What does he have on you?" "Nothing." "Mikhail." "It's not important." "It is if I'm—" "You're not doing anything. Just the painting." "Right. Just the painting. Which is why I need to know about the guards, the cameras, the—" "That's not your problem." But he's explaining anyway, because we both know I'm already in. The real Basquiat's in Castellano's private gallery in Red Hook. Six other pieces, total value around forty million. Motion sensors, pressure plates, the whole paranoid setup. But Castellano's arrogant, likes showing off his collection. Has viewings every month for potential buyers, select crowds, champagne and cocaine and NDAs. "Next one's in three days." "And you're invited?" "Not exactly." His phone buzzes. Andre's name. He doesn't answer but texts back, Russian I can't read. "Your ex is involved?" "He's good at this." "He's good at being beautiful." "That too." I'm already jealous, which is stupid. Mikhail's not mine. Was never mine even when we were whatever we were. But watching him text Andre makes me want to mark him, leave bruises everyone can see. "There's a meeting tonight," he says. "You should come." "I'm just doing the painting." "Right." "I mean it." "Okay." But I'm already getting dressed, his cum still drying on my stomach because I'm too lazy to shower. He watches me from the bed, not moving to get ready. This is Mikhail—never rushed, even when the building's burning. The warehouse in Bushwick looks abandoned from outside, which is the point. Inside, Andre's doing lines off a Scarface mirror he probably bought as a joke. A kid who can't be older than nineteen is taking apart and reassembling a lock, fingers twitching. Rosa's reading something on her phone, ignoring everyone. "This is Jamie," Mikhail says. "The forger," Andre says. Not looking at me, looking at Mikhail. "How cozy." His accent's thicker than Mikhail's, Belarusian smoothing the edges of his English. He's too beautiful for this room—cheekbones that belong on magazine covers, bleached hair with dark roots, moves like a dancer. I hate him immediately. "You're late," Rosa says. First time I've heard her voice. Deeper than expected, careful pronunciation like she's thought about each word. "Traffic," Mikhail lies. She looks at me, takes in the sex hair, the paint under my nails, the way I'm standing too close to Mikhail. "This is stupid." "Noted." "No, I need you to understand. Bringing personal shit into professional shit is stupid. Castellano isn't some Chelsea gallery owner who'll call the cops. He's a collector." "I know about the fingers." "Fingers?" The tweaker kid looks up. "What fingers?" "Forget it, Pilot," Rosa says. "Are you high right now?" "No." "Don't lie to me." "Only a little." "Jesus Christ." Andre does another line, still watching Mikhail. There's history there, messy and recent. I can see it in how they don't quite look at each other, how Andre's body angles toward him even while pretending indifference. "The viewing's at nine," Rosa says. "Mikhail and Andre go in as buyers. Pilot handles the van. During the tour, you clone the security badge from the guard. Next night, we go back." "What about me?" I ask. "You've done your part." "But—" "You're not crew," Andre says. "You're just the artist." He makes artist sound like whore. Maybe it is. Mikhail's hand brushes mine, barely there but enough. Andre sees it, his jaw tightening. "I want to see the space," I say. "Make sure the painting will read right in that light." Rosa laughs, short and bitter. "You think this is about artistic integrity?" "I think if we're doing this, we do it right." "We?" Andre stands, all six feet of barely controlled cocaine energy. "There's no we. There's Mikhail and whoever Mikhail's fucking this week." "Andre," Mikhail says. Warning in it. "What? It's true. Last month it was that dealer from Miami. Before that, the married one with the—" "Enough." The room goes quiet except for Pilot's lock clicking. Rosa's watching everything, calculating.
