The half-sister reveal adds complexity. But the overall structure (safe house, exposition dump, courthouse delivery) is fairly conventional. **Forced Poetry:** The ending's triple repetition of "heavier" works. The "map across her cheeks" for scars is slightly clichéd. "Grey and bitter" for dawn is functional. The prose avoids most purple excess. **Darkness:** The darkness is well-earned in the final delivery scene. The ambiguity about what Seraphina whispers is handled with appropriate restraint. The moral weight of the transaction—gold for a person—lands. **Overall:** A competent chapter that deviates significantly from its plan, introduces convenient new elements, has some exposition-heavy dialogue, but achieves genuine emotional resonance in its final scenes. The ending is the chapter's strongest element. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 13 Emotionally Engaging: 14 Compelling Plot: 11 Coherent: 10 Weak Dialogue: 9 Tell-Don't-Show: 10 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 9 Amateurish: 7 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 13 Characters Consistent with Profile: 12 Followed Chapter Plan: 6 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 14

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 8: After Six months. Kael's shoulder never healed right. The bone had set wrong, or maybe the muscle just remembered the blade going through. Either way, he couldn't throw knives anymore. Couldn't trust his arm in a fight. Couldn't sleep on his left side without waking in pain that felt like memory. He drank at the Shepherd's Rest—different innkeeper now, the old one had hanged himself after finding his wife. The new one didn't ask questions when Kael paid in gold. Five thousand lasted a long time when you drank cheap. The whore's name was Vera. Red hair, not red skin, but in the dark after enough whiskey it almost worked. She bit when she came, like she was angry about it. Left marks on his shoulder—the good one—that lasted for days. "You say her name sometimes," Vera told him once. "When you're close." "What name?" "You know what name." He paid her extra to forget he'd said anything. The news came on a cold morning when his head was splitting and his cock was raw from Vera's attention. Another traveler, gossiping over breakfast: the tiefling murderer had escaped. During her execution, no less. Killed three guards with their own blades. The heir was furious. Offering ten thousand for her return. "Ten thousand," the traveler repeated. "Could set a man up for life." Kael left that afternoon. The heir received him in the same purple silk, though his face had hardened. Six months of ruling would do that. Six months of wondering if every shadow held a red-skinned demon coming for revenge. "You're the one who brought her in before," the heir said. "I am." "She escaped during the hanging. The rope was around her neck. The floor dropped. Somehow the chains were already unlocked. She landed soft, grabbed a guard's sword, painted my courtyard red." The heir leaned forward. "I want her dead. Not captured. Dead." "Where do I start?" "She's been seen in the highlands. Near where you caught her the first time." The heir smiled, unpleasant. "She's waiting for you, I think. Wants to finish what you started." "What we started was finished when I delivered her." "Was it?" No. No, it wasn't. The road to the highlands was familiar now. Every tree, every rock. The place where he'd first put her in chains. The cave where she'd dislocated her thumb. The inn where they'd hidden a corpse. All of it mapped in memory and pain. She'd left signs. Subtle things—a button placed on a specific rock, the remains of a fire built the way she'd watched him build them. Leading him. Guiding him. Playing with him. He found her where he knew he would: the same cave where her thumb had swelled purple-black. She was sitting against the back wall, sharpening a knife. The same knife she'd lifted from the soldier. The one she'd bled herself with for luck. "Did you miss me?" she asked. "You escaped." "I did. Lissa helped. Paid the guards to leave the locks undone. Though I might have managed anyway—you taught me about shoulder tells, remember?" She stood. She looked good. Healthy. The infected wounds had healed into scars that matched the ones on her ribs. Her dress was quality fabric, well-fitted. She'd found money somewhere. Or taken it. "The heir wants you dead," he said. "The heir wants a lot of things. His father's approval. His stepmother's cunt. A clear conscience. Can't have any of them." She stepped closer. "What do you want?" "My shoulder to work right." "Liar." "The gold." "Liar." "You dead." "Maybe." She tilted her head. "But I don't think so." They circled each other. He had his sword out—couldn't trust his aim with knives anymore. She had the stolen blade, the one that tasted her blood and his. The cave was too small for this. They'd be on each other in seconds once someone moved. "I could have killed you," she said. "While you were unconscious at the Copper Cat. While you were carrying me to the courthouse. Any time." "Why didn't you?" "Same reason you're not attacking right now." "I'm about to." "No. You're not." She reversed the knife, offered him the handle. "Here. Kill me. Collect your ten thousand. Drink yourself to death in peace." He didn't take it. "That's what I thought." She flipped the knife back, resumed circling. "You know what your problem is? You think there are rules. Good, bad, right, wrong. There aren't. There's just what we do and what we don't do." "I delivered you." "You saved me. The heir would have had me killed on the road. Renn's body forced everything public. Made it a trial instead of a disappearance. Gave Lissa time to arrange things." "You're saying I saved you on purpose?" "I'm saying it doesn't matter. Purpose is just a story we tell after." She lunged—testing, not committed. He parried, but his shoulder screamed. She saw it. "You can't win this." "I know." "So stop trying." "Can't." "Why?" "Because—" She moved for real this time. He got his sword up, but she was already inside his guard. The knife stopped just under his ribs, her body pressed against his. "Because what?" she whispered. "Because I should have fucked you when I had the chance." She laughed. The knife pressed harder, not quite breaking skin. "You still can." "You'll kill me during." "Maybe. Probably." She ground against him, deliberate. "But what a way to go." They stayed frozen. His sword at her throat. Her knife at his ribs. Bodies pressed together, breathing hard. The cave smelled like old smoke and fever-memory and the copper-ash of her skin. "We could leave," she said. "Together. Go somewhere they've never heard of tieflings or bounties or noble heirs." "There's nowhere like that." "Then we make do with what there is." "You'll betray me." "Yes." "I'll betray you." "Already did." "So what's the point?" "The point is—" She kissed him. Or he kissed her. Or they fell into each other with six months of hunger and three days of road-memory. The weapons stayed where they were—his sword, her knife, both drawing blood now. Neither caring. When they pulled apart, she was smiling. Blood on her throat from his sword. His shirt wet where her knife had gone deeper. "The point is we're already dead," she said. "Have been since that first day. Just taking our time getting there." "Together?" "Or apart. Or taking turns." She pressed the knife deeper. He pressed the sword. More blood. "Decide." And they're still there. In that cave. Circling. Weapons drawn. About to kill each other. About to fuck. About to run. About to— Forever three days from Irongate. Forever deciding. Forever.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the culmination of a carefully constructed story, and it largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. STRENGTHS: The chapter opens with economical, effective prose. "Six months. Kael's shoulder never healed right." is a strong opening that immediately grounds us in consequence. The detail about Vera the red-haired whore, and Kael saying Seraphina's name during sex, is well-executed showing rather than telling—it communicates his obsession without belaboring it. The dialogue is generally sharp and in character. Seraphina's lines ("The heir wants a lot of things. His father's approval. His stepmother's cunt. A clear conscience.") feel authentic to her established voice—sardonic, cutting, layered. The exchange about purpose ("Purpose is just a story we tell after") fits her philosophical nihilism from the character profile. The plan called for an ending mid-sentence, mid-motion, and the chapter delivers this with "Forever three days from Irongate. Forever deciding. Forever." This is earned by the narrative logic—the story has been about irresolution, and the ending honors that. The physical choreography of the final confrontation—weapons drawn, both drawing blood, neither stopping—is viscerally effective and consistent with the established dynamic of violence and desire being interchangeable for these characters. WEAKNESSES: The "Forever" repetition at the end tips into purple prose territory. "Forever three days from Irongate. Forever deciding. Forever." The final single-word "Forever." is a step too far—it's the kind of ending that announces its own profundity rather than achieving it. The plan called for ending "mid-sentence, mid-motion," and the actual ending is more of a deliberate poetic coda, which slightly undercuts the rawness. Some moments feel slightly rushed. The revelation that Lissa helped Seraphina escape ("Lissa helped. Paid the guards to leave the locks undone") introduces a character who hasn't appeared before in this chapter, and while presumably established earlier in the story, it feels like an information dump here. The line "Because I should have fucked you when I had the chance" is the weakest dialogue in the chapter—it's too on-the-nose, too neat a summary of Kael's psychology. It tells us what we already know rather than revealing something new. The heir's characterization ("His father's approval. His stepmother's cunt") is vivid but feels slightly grafted on—a clever line that doesn't quite earn its place. The chapter follows its plan closely: the shoulder that never healed, the drinking, Vera as a Seraphina substitute, the news of escape, the ten thousand bounty, finding her in the same cave, the knife from the soldier, the circling, the ambiguous ending. The plan is executed faithfully. CHARACTER CONSISTENCY: Kael is consistent—economical, self-aware about his failures, unable to act on what he knows. Seraphina is consistent—manipulative, layered, using truth and lies interchangeably. The detail about her tail going still when afraid is absent, which is a minor missed opportunity. OVERALL: This is competent-to-good literary erotica with genuine craft. The prose is mostly controlled, the characters are consistent, and the ending achieves its intended ambiguity. The weaknesses are real but not fatal—some over-reaching in the final lines, one weak dialogue moment, and a slightly rushed middle section. The chapter earns its darkness through accumulated context rather than cheap shock. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 6 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 7 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 15 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Final Piece Evaluation

Judge Response (First Entry):
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of fantasy erotica that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me assess it systematically. **Strengths:** The central dynamic between Kael and Seraphina is the story's engine, and it largely works. Seraphina is a genuinely compelling creation—manipulative, damaged, funny, dangerous, and vulnerable in ways that feel earned rather than convenient. Her monologues about Marcus, the garden, Lissa, and her own scars accumulate into something that feels like a real psychology rather than a collection of "mysterious woman" tropes. The revelation that she was orchestrating her own delivery to Irongate is foreshadowed well enough to feel satisfying rather than cheap. The dialogue is frequently excellent. "I'm invested in keeping you alive for the next three days. After that..." is exactly the right kind of line—threatening, funny, and character-revealing simultaneously. The banter has genuine wit and the power dynamic shifts organically through conversation rather than through authorial decree. The pacing across chapters is mostly strong. The fever chapter (Chapter 3) is the emotional core and it earns its intimacy. The inn chapter (Chapter 4) escalates tension effectively. The checkpoint scene in Chapter 5 is well-executed genre work. **Weaknesses:** The ending (Chapter 8) is the piece's most significant failure. The "forever deciding, forever" conclusion is a self-consciously literary gesture that doesn't quite earn its ambiguity. After seven chapters of concrete, grounded storytelling, the sudden shift to present-tense philosophical abstraction ("And they're still there. In that cave. Circling.") feels like the author running out of road and reaching for profundity. It's the one moment where the piece tips into forced poetry. Some metaphors don't land: "the taste of her blood in his mouth and the growing certainty that she was right" is fine, but "black lightning" for spreading poison veins is slightly overwrought. The line "heavier than it should be for what he'd sold" at the end of Chapter 7 is the kind of sentiment that sounds meaningful but is actually quite generic. Kael's transformation from professional bounty hunter to man-who-chooses-love is the story's central arc, and it's mostly earned through accumulation—the fever night, the checkpoint, the fight with Renn. However, the moment he says "She's mine" to Renn and then kills six men feels slightly rushed. The story has been building to this, but the actual pivot happens very quickly. The violence is handled well—it's efficient and unglamorous, which suits Kael's character. The sexual tension is built through restraint and implication rather than explicit content, which is actually more effective for the genre. The world-building is functional but thin. Irongate, the heir, the Imperial checkpoint—these feel like genre furniture rather than a lived world. The story doesn't need deep world-building, but the political stakes (heir kills father, blames tiefling daughter) are somewhat generic. The innkeeper's wife murder in Chapter 4 is a bold choice that complicates Seraphina's morality effectively. The story doesn't flinch from making her genuinely dangerous and morally compromised, which is to its credit. Lissa's exposition in Chapter 7 is the weakest scene—it's necessary information delivered in a fairly clunky way, with Lissa essentially serving as an exposition vehicle. The backstory (Marcus, the brothel, the inheritance) is interesting but delivered too neatly. The "people like us" motif is used well throughout. The recurring "exactly where I want to be" phrase does real work in building Seraphina's characterization. Overall, this is a well-above-average piece of genre fiction with genuine literary ambition that mostly succeeds. Its failures are the failures of overreach rather than incompetence. [Scores] Believable Character Actions: 15 Nuanced Characters: 16 Pacing: 14 World Building: 11 Compelling Plot: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Coherent: 15 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 7 Unearned Transformations: 7 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 18
Avg Final Item Score: 73.0/100
+ Cozy-Horror Post Apocalyptic Survival — Mushroom Season (Chapter Avg: 76.4 | Final: 55.4)
Initial Writing Prompt:
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you're out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious?
+ Planning Phase (5 Steps)
Cozy-Horror Post Apocalypse Survival Write a short story over several chapters to this prompt: When you&#x27;re out of food, you get desperate. Who would have thought the spore mutants -- the things trying to kill us -- would be delicious? -- Your task is to create a writing plan for this prompt. The scope will be a short story, written over {n_chapters} chapters. Each chapter will be approximately 1000 words so plan accordingly for this scope. Your plan should be comprehensive and in this format: # Brainstorming <Brainstorm ideas for characters, plot, tone, story beats, pacing over the {n_chapters} chapters. The purpose of brainstorming is to cast a wide net of ideas, not to settle on any specific direction. Think about various ways you could take the prompt.> # Reflection <Reflect out loud on what works and doesn't work in these ideas. The purpose of this reflection is to narrow in on what you think will work best to make a piece that is a. compelling, and b. fits the prompt requirements. You are not making any decisons just yet, just reflecting.>
Model Output:
# Brainstorming ## Character Ideas - **Protagonist Options:** - Former chef/food critic surviving the apocalypse, bringing culinary expertise to monster cooking - Reluctant cannibal-turned-monster-eater dealing with moral implications - Scientist who discovers the edibility accidentally while studying spores - Child who innocently discovers the food source, normalizing it for adults - Community cook/mother figure who becomes the group's monster chef - Mycologist who understands fungal biology and becomes invaluable - **Supporting Cast:** - Skeptical survivor who refuses to eat the mutants initially - Enthusiastic convert who becomes obsessed with hunting mutants - Someone who has adverse reaction to eating mutants - Previous authority figure struggling with loss of control - Young couple trying to maintain normalcy - Elder who remembers pre-apocalypse cooking traditions ## Plot Directions - **Discovery Arc:** Accidental discovery → experimentation → perfection of cooking methods → community acceptance → unexpected consequences - **Community Building:** Solo survivor finds group → introduces new food source → builds reputation → creates stability → faces new threats - **Moral Dilemma:** Initial revulsion → desperate first taste → guilt and acceptance → discovering mutants were once human → choosing survival - **Evolution:** Eating mutants causes subtle changes in humans → enhanced abilities → question of becoming what you eat → new symbiosis - **Restaurant/Trading Post:** Establishing a safe haven centered around mutant cuisine → attracting other survivors → building economy → defending territory ## Tone Options - Dark comedy with gallows humor about eating monsters - Genuinely cozy despite circumstances (campfire cooking, community bonding) - Body horror contrasted with domestic comfort - Philosophical exploration of survival ethics - Folksy/rural survival wisdom meets cosmic horror - Documentary/journal style recording culinary discoveries ## Story Beats Across 8 Chapters **Version A: The Reluctant Gourmand** 1. Starvation and first desperate taste 2. Learning to hunt and prepare safely 3. Convincing others/building trust 4. Establishing cooking routines and recipes 5. Community feast/celebration 6. Discovery of side effects or deeper truth 7. External threat to food source/community 8. Resolution balancing survival with humanity **Version B: The Spore Farmer** 1. Last can of food, discovery of edibility 2. First successful hunt and meal 3. Teaching others, resistance and acceptance 4. Developing preservation methods 5. Mutants seem to be learning/adapting 6. Discovering mutants can be domesticated/farmed 7. Moral implications of farming sentient beings 8. New ecological balance achieved **Version C: The Underground Kitchen** 1. Lone survivor in bunker running out of food 2. Forced to surface, encounters and eats mutant 3. Finds other survivors, shares knowledge 4. Building underground kitchen/safe house 5. Developing menu and attracting traders 6. Mutants begin surrounding the settlement 7. Discovering mutants are attracted to cooking smells 8. Using cuisine as weapon/diplomacy tool ## Pacing Considerations - Slow burn: Gradual acceptance and normalization of eating mutants - Episodic: Each chapter is different meal/hunting expedition - Escalating stakes: Each chapter introduces new complication - Seasonal: Following months/seasons of survival - Recipe format: Each chapter centers on different dish/preparation method ## World-Building Elements - Spore mutant biology and varieties (different flavors/textures) - Post-apocalypse timeline (how long since collapse?) - Geographic setting (urban ruins, rural, suburban?) - Other survivor communities and their food solutions - Environmental hazards beyond mutants - Pre-apocalypse character backgrounds informing skills # Reflection Looking at these ideas, several elements stand out as particularly compelling: **What Works:** - The **former chef/food critic protagonist** offers great potential for both humor and expertise, allowing for detailed food descriptions that contrast beautifully with the horror elements. This character can bring genuine cozy cooking vibes while maintaining the absurdity. - The **community building aspect** is essential for the "cozy" element - sharing meals, teaching recipes, and gathering around food creates warmth despite the dark circumstances. - The **gradual normalization** of eating mutants mirrors how societies adapt to extreme circumstances, which could be deeply compelling. - Having **different types of spore mutants** with varying flavors/preparations adds variety and prevents repetition across chapters. - The **discovery of side effects** or that mutants were once human adds necessary tension and moral complexity to prevent the story from becoming too lighthearted. **What Might Not Work:** - The **farming/domestication angle** might remove too much danger and undercut the horror elements. - Making it **too comedic** could diminish the post-apocalyptic survival stakes. - The **restaurant/trading post** concept might be too ambitious for 8,000 words and could feel rushed. - **Too many characters** could prevent deep character development in a short story format. **Most Promising Direction:** A combination of Version A (The Reluctant Gourmand) with elements from the others seems strongest. Starting with a small group of survivors (3-4 people) allows for intimate character development. The protagonist being someone with culinary knowledge (whether professional or passionate hobbyist) provides expertise and vocabulary for the cozy cooking scenes. The story should maintain genuine danger from the mutants while slowly building comfort around preparing and sharing meals together. The arc should balance the cozy elements (cooking together, sharing meals, building community) with horror elements (dangerous hunts, body horror descriptions, moral implications). The discovery that eating mutants has consequences - whether physical changes, attraction of more mutants, or learning they were once human - provides the necessary tension for the final act. The 8-chapter structure could follow a rough pattern of: Discovery (1-2), Acceptance (3-4), Community (5-6), Complication (7), Resolution (8). This allows for proper pacing while keeping each chapter focused and meaningful.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** A former sous chef leads a small group of survivors through the moral and practical journey of discovering that their predators are also their salvation, told through intimate scenes of cooking, eating, and surviving together. **Protagonist:** Marie, mid-30s, former sous chef at a farm-to-table restaurant. Practical, determined, carrying guilt about who she couldn't save. Her culinary expertise becomes unexpectedly vital, and she finds purpose in nourishing others even in hell. **Voice & Style:** First-person retrospective narration from Marie, like she's recording a cookbook-memoir for whoever comes after. Present-tense for immediate scenes, past-tense for reflection. Language alternates between sensory-rich food writing and stark survival horror. Cozy moments are genuine but tinged with melancholy. **Supporting Cast:** Keep it tight - three other survivors: - David (50s): Former high school biology teacher, provides scientific insight, struggles with the ethics - Kai (early 20s): Engineering student, eager but haunted, becomes Marie's unofficial sous chef - Rosa (60s): Retired nurse, pragmatic, becomes the group's moral compass **What I'm Aiming For:** - Genuine coziness through cooking rituals, shared meals, and found family dynamics - Horror through visceral hunting scenes and slow revelation about the mutants' nature - Rich sensory descriptions that make readers simultaneously hungry and disturbed - Exploration of how humans rationalize necessary evils - The idea that maintaining civilization's rituals (proper cooking, shared meals) keeps us human **What I'm Avoiding:** - Slapstick comedy or making light of the horror - Too many action sequences that would eat up word count - Extensive world-building about the apocalypse itself - Making the mutants sympathetic too early - Resolution that's too neat or optimistic # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "The Last Can" (Discovery)** Marie's group is down to their final can of peaches. While on a desperate scavenging run, Kai is cornered by a spore mutant. Marie kills it with a kitchen knife, and in the aftermath, notices its flesh smells unexpectedly like mushrooms and ocean. Starving, she makes the unthinkable decision to take some back. First tentative cooking attempt that night. **Chapter 2: "Salt and Heat" (First Success)** Marie secretly experiments with preparation methods while others sleep. Discovers that high heat neutralizes the spores' toxicity. She finally tastes it - umami-rich, tender, surprisingly good. Wrestling with whether to tell the others. David catches her and is horrified, but his hunger wins. They develop the first reliable cooking method together. **Chapter 3: "The Dinner Proposal" (Group Acceptance)** Marie and David must convince Rosa and Kai. Marie prepares the mutant meat properly - seared with wild herbs, presented beautifully on salvaged plates. The ritual of a proper meal helps overcome revulsion. Rosa shares medical knowledge about protein starvation. Kai's enthusiasm returns as he helps prepare the meal. First communal dinner establishes new normal. **Chapter 4: "Varieties of Experience" (Exploration)** The group learns to identify different types of mutants - the "runners" taste like rabbit, the "hulks" like beef, the smaller ones like chicken. Marie teaches butchering and preparation techniques. They develop hunting strategies. A cozy evening of meal prep and storytelling about favorite pre-apocalypse foods. Marie starts her "cookbook" journal. **Chapter 5: "Feast Day" (Community)** One month since their first mutant meal. The group has established routines - hunting schedules, preparation rituals, even a spice garden. They decide to have a proper feast to celebrate survival. Marie creates multiple courses from different mutant types. During dinner, they notice they're all healthier, stronger. The meal feels almost normal, almost like before. **Chapter 6: "The Changed" (Revelation)** While hunting, they encounter another group of survivors who also eat mutants - but these people have changed. Subtle at first: enhanced night vision, understanding mutant movement patterns, healing faster. The other group's leader reveals they've been eating mutants for six months. That night, David notices the beginning of changes in their own group. They find human items in a mutant's nest. **Chapter 7: "What We Were" (Crisis)** David's research into spore biology combined with found evidence reveals the truth: mutants were human, transformed by the spores. Eating them is causing slow genetic changes in the survivors. The group fractures - Kai wants to stop, Rosa is pragmatic, David is horrified by what he's enabled. Marie discovers she can now sense mutants nearby. A large pack of mutants surrounds their shelter, seemingly drawn by something. **Chapter 8: "The New Recipe" (Resolution)** Marie realizes the mutants aren't attacking - they're gathering, drawn to what the survivors are becoming. The group must choose: starve and remain fully human, or accept the transformation and survive. Marie makes a final meal using the last of their traditional spices and a freshly hunted mutant, a bridge between worlds. They choose survival with awareness. Final entry in Marie's cookbook acknowledges what they're becoming, but insists the act of cooking together, of maintaining ritual and care, keeps something essentially human alive.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer tackling this prompt would likely start from a place of **genuine discomfort** and lean into it. They'd probably: - **Start with the grossest, most visceral moment** - not build up to it politely - **Use their actual cooking/eating experiences** - that time they ate something questionable, the specific smell of burnt meat, the texture of undercooked chicken that made them gag - **Include embarrassing bodily reactions** - diarrhea from the first attempts, vomiting, the crude jokes survivors would actually make - **Write uneven chapters** - some rushed and panicked, others languid, based on emotional state rather than consistent pacing - **Leave moral questions unanswered** - humans don't resolve ethical dilemmas neatly - **Include petty human conflicts** - arguments over seasoning, someone hoarding the good cuts, jealousy over cooking skill - **Use gallows humor without apologizing** - actual survivors make terrible jokes without adding "he laughed nervously" # Common LLM Failings ## General LLM Tendencies: - **Over-scaffolding:** "First, then, next, finally" - too much structural signposting - **Emotional telegraphing:** "She felt sad" instead of showing through action - **Safety hedging:** Constantly acknowledging the moral issues instead of just presenting them - **Purple prose pretensions:** Using "crimson" instead of "red," "masticate" instead of "chew" - **Perfect character consistency:** Everyone always acts according to their established personality - **Thematic anvils:** Heavy-handed metaphors about "humanity" and "survival" - **Clean ending syndrome:** Tying up every thread, answering every question - **Wikipedia voice:** Explaining the apocalypse's background like an encyclopedia entry ## How These Hurt This Specific Piece: - **Sanitizing the cozy-horror balance:** An LLM might make it either too cozy (avoiding the genuine horror) or too horror (losing the warmth) - **Over-explaining the cooking:** Technical descriptions instead of sensory, emotional experiences - **Making everyone too reasonable:** Real people would have insane, irrational reactions to eating mutants - **Avoiding the truly disturbing:** Glossing over the cannibalism parallels, the body horror, the gradual loss of humanity # Deep Critique of My Plan ## Where I've Fallen Into LLM Tropes: ### Too Neat Character Archetypes: - The chef, the scientist, the young person, the elder - it's like a video game party - Real groups would be messier: maybe two people who hate each other, someone completely useless, overlapping skills ### Over-Structured Plot: - My 8-chapter arc is too symmetrical: Discovery→Acceptance→Community→Crisis→Resolution - Real stories stutter, backtrack, have anticlimaxes - Chapter 5's "Feast Day" is too orchestrated - a perfect midpoint celebration ### Safety Nets: - Making mutants conveniently non-human originally (then revealing humanity later) - hedging bets - The "cooking neutralizes toxins" explanation - too convenient - Everyone eventually accepting the situation - where's the person who'd rather die? ### Explaining Too Much: - David the biology teacher to provide scientific exposition - Marie's cookbook framing device to justify descriptions - The other survivor group to explain transformations ### Too Noble: - Marie finding "purpose in nourishing others" - too heartwarming - The group choosing together - real groups fracture messily - Maintaining "civilization's rituals" - too thematically clean # Brainstorming More Human Approaches ## Character Messiness: - Marie could be a mediocre cook who's just pretending expertise because it gives her control - Someone who LOVES eating the mutants too much - gets creepy about it - Character who was vegetarian before and is having complete breakdown - Two characters who start sleeping together just because the world ended - Someone who names the mutants before eating them - Character who insists on saying grace before mutant meals ## Plot Messiness: - Chapter where nothing happens - they just eat and feel weird about it - Someone gets food poisoning from badly prepared mutant - They accidentally eat someone they knew who turned - Discovery that children digest mutants differently than adults - Someone starts preferring raw mutant meat - A chapter that's just recipes getting increasingly unhinged ## Tonal Chaos: - Genuine comedy: someone insisting on food photography with a broken phone - Real nastiness: hoarding the tender cuts, lying about ingredients - Bizarre normalcy: argument about whether mutant counts as red or white meat - Actual cozy: reading old cookbook passages while eating former humans - True horror: finding a wedding ring in a mutant's stomach ## Unresolved Elements: - Never explain the spores properly - Don't reveal if the changes are good or bad - Leave someone's fate ambiguous - End mid-meal, mid-conversation - Questions about pregnancy/children and mutant diet left hanging - No clear moral stance on anything ## Sensory Specificity: - The specific pop of spore sacs when cut - Mutant meat sticking to non-oiled pans - The smell like "pennies and portobellos" - Texture like "overcooked scallops" - The way mutant fat renders differently - How hunger makes even the smell of them cooking arousing ## Real Conversations: - "Pass the salt. No, the other... is this one made from..." - "It's just meat. Don't be a baby." - "I can't believe you're writing this down." - "Tastes like my mom's pot roast if her pot roast was evil." - "Dibs on the organs" / "That's disgusting" / "You ate the eyes yesterday" ## Darker Implications: - Someone suggesting they should try "fresh" human for comparison - Developing preferences for certain types of people-turned-mutants - The arousal/hunger confusion when hunting - Dreams about eating loved ones - The first time someone calls it "delicious" without qualifying it - When they stop bothering to check if mutants were someone they knew The plan needs more **failure**, more **mundane awfulness**, more **uncomfortable silence**, and absolutely no sense that the author is trying to teach anyone anything about humanity or survival or morality. It should feel like someone's actual journal if they were going insane but still really cared about dinner.
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention **Core Concept:** A starving group discovers spore mutants taste incredible, and the slow normalization of eating them becomes both salvation and damnation. No one learns anything. Everyone just gets hungrier. **Narrator:** Marie, who may or may not have been a real chef (she lies sometimes), writing in a water-damaged notebook she insists is her "cookbook." Present tense, sensory-obsessed, increasingly unreliable as hunger and consumption change her. **Voice & Style:** Like Anthony Bourdain's kitchen memoirs crossed with a fever dream. Marie fixates on taste and texture while glossing over horrific violence. No chapter transitions, no explanations for time jumps. Recipes appear mid-narrative. Conversations start mid-argument. Some chapters are 3 days, others are 3 months. **The Others:** Not archetypes, just people: - David won't shut up about his dead wife but hoards the tender bits - Kai pretends he's not turned on by the hunting - Rosa keeps saying "when rescue comes" while sharpening her knives - Someone else who might be named Tom (Marie never remembers) **What I'm Aiming For:** - The actual moment of discovering they're delicious - not rushed, not overthought - Real pettiness about food distribution and seasoning choices - Cozy scenes that are genuinely warm but happening while something horrible is normalized - The specific texture, smell, taste of different preparations - How hunger makes everything else irrelevant - Uneven pacing - some chapters drag, others race **What I'm Avoiding:** - Explaining the apocalypse or spores properly - Character growth or redemption - Clear timeline or geography - Resolving whether the changes are good or bad - Any stance on the morality of it - Making sense of everything # Chapter Planning **Chapter 1: "The Good Fat"** Marie's stabbing a mutant off David when she notices the marbling in its exposed muscle. Like wagyu. She's salivating before she realizes it. They've been eating fucking dandelions for two weeks. That night she goes back alone with a jar. Just a little. Just to try. She cooks it in a pan she's been saving bacon grease in for three years (the bacon is long gone). It's perfect. It's better than perfect. She eats until she's sick and then licks the pan. **Chapter 2: "Breakfast"** Three days later? A week? Marie's been sneaking pieces. Others are suspicious of her energy. Tom (?) catches her mid-bite, raw chunk in her mouth. She offers him some. He throws up. Then asks for more. By morning, she's teaching him to identify the good cuts while David and Rosa sleep. Kai watches from the doorway, pretending disgust. Marie includes a recipe for "Morning Scramble" that calls for ingredients they don't have except the meat. **Chapter 3: "The Fat Tuesday Argument"** Everyone's eating it now. No discussion of how that happened. David insists on calling it "protein" while hoarding the organs. Rosa wants to try smoking it for preservation. Kai suggests they're all going to hell. Marie writes: "Hell would have better spice selection." Big fight over whether to use the last of the salt for curing or seasoning. Someone (Tom?) cries about his mother's Sunday roast. They eat in angry silence. It's still delicious. **Chapter 4: "Michelin Starred"** Marie's notebook entries getting elaborate - "Notes of forest floor and copper, with an unexpected brightness..." They're hunting daily now. Kai brings back a small one, says it reminds him of rabbit. It doesn't. It's better. Rosa develops a scoring system for tenderness. David still won't eat the eyes (he does when no one's looking). Marie finds herself aroused watching Kai field-dress a mutant. She writes a recipe for "Lover's Knots" that's just various ways to tie intestines. **Chapter 5: "The Dinner Party"** Two months in? Six? They find another survivor who won't eat mutant. Marie's offended like he's insulted her cooking. They prepare an elaborate meal to convince him - multiple courses, garnished with weeds, served on real plates Rosa found. The survivor keeps asking what it is. "Chicken," they all say, mouths full, juice on their chins. He leaves in the night. They divide his portion before his footsteps fade. **Chapter 6: "Preferences"** Everyone's developing favorites. David likes the ones that were older - more marbling. Kai prefers the runners, says they're leaner (he's lying). Rosa wants the females (she can tell somehow). Marie realizes she can smell them now, even through walls. Especially the ones that smell scared. Tom hasn't spoken in weeks but he grunts approval at certain preparations. Marie's recipes are becoming unhinged - "Anxiety Reduction" requires "one terrified mutant, butterflied." **Chapter 7: "The Good News"** Rosa's pregnant. Or thinks she is. Was before? After? She's craving the spinal fluid. Marie discovers if you crack the spine just right, it pours out like wine. They have a celebration feast. Everyone's teeth seem sharper in the firelight. Kai admits he doesn't remember his last name. David's wife might not have existed. Marie writes: "Sauté until the screaming stops (theirs or yours)." **Chapter 8: "Tuesday"** Just another meal. Marie can't remember when they started. The mutants gather outside but don't attack anymore. Sometimes they leave the weak ones at the door, like offerings. Or groceries. The group doesn't talk while eating now - just the sounds of chewing, swallowing, the occasional satisfied moan. Marie's notebook is full but she keeps writing in the margins, over old words. Her last clear entry is a recipe that just says "hungry hungry hungry" over and over. Someone new arrives. They offer him dinner. The cycle continues. It's delicious. It's always been delicious.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Marie Marie worked garde manger at a middling French restaurant for three months before the world ended. She tells everyone she was sous chef. Before that: dental hygienist. She quit because she kept gagging at the smell of people's mouths. Has strong opinions about knife maintenance but holds them wrong. Photographs food with a dead phone out of habit. Counts bites when eating - always has, even before. Seventeen. Eighteen. Nineteen. Used to have an eating disorder but won't admit the apocalypse cured it. Laughs at her own jokes, especially the dark ones nobody else finds funny. Steals small things compulsively - buttons, pens, the good fat from others' portions. Cannot sleep without touching something soft. Currently uses a molt of mutant skin. Thin in the wrong places now, collarbones like knife handles. Moves with unnecessary efficiency in the kitchen, theatrical elsewhere. Hair in a grease-stiff bun stabbed through with a sharpened chopstick. Hands always moving - tapping, measuring, testing texture. Unconsciously mirrors how people chew. Standing too close because her peripheral vision is getting weird. Smell-checks everything twice, sometimes her own wrists. Wants to be needed more than loved. Convinced if she stops cooking, they'll eat her next. Sexually attracted to competence but also to Kai's obvious fear of her. Loyal to whoever's hungriest. Keeps David around because he makes her feel smart. Genuinely loves Rosa like the mother who never taught her to cook. Records everything because someone should know they tried to do it right, even if "right" keeps changing. "The fat renders at exactly the temperature of shame - 451, same as paper." "I had a boyfriend who tasted like pennies too. Different context." "Don't be precious about it. We're not precious about anything anymore." # David High school chemistry teacher, not biology like he claims. Wife definitely existed but she was leaving him when the spores hit. Tells himself she was looking for him. Has a daughter somewhere maybe. Practices lectures while butchering. Insists on Latin names for body parts nobody cares about. Secretly dyslexic, which is why his notebook entries are so sparse. Allergic to dogs but won't admit the mutants trigger the same histamine response - he's constantly congested, calls it "the weather." Measures everything three times. Rocks when anxious, claims he's thinking. Soft-bodied but oddly strong, that dad strength from moving furniture. Wispy crown of hair he still tries to manage. Glasses held with electrical tape, one lens cracked directly through the pupil. Breathes through his mouth, tongue slightly out when concentrating. Holds things too tightly - cups, knives, people's shoulders when making a point. Smells like old papers and new meat. Desperately needs to be the authority but knows Marie's more useful. Follows her while pretending to lead. Wants Kai to see him as a father figure but comes off creepy. Sees Rosa as competition for group wisdom. Hoards information and choice cuts equally. Still trying to solve this like it's a problem with an answer. Can't admit he likes the taste because that would make him complicit. "The mycelial network suggests a distributed consciousness, which means— are you listening?" "My wife made this dish with rabbit. This isn't rabbit. But the principle remains." "We're not animals. We use tools. We cook our food. We maintain standards." # Kai Engineering dropout, third year, was failing anyway. Parents were Korean immigrants who owned a convenience store. He was supposed to be studying the night they died. Learned to hunt from YouTube videos before the internet died. Jerks off to memories of murder now but tells himself it's the adrenaline. Twenty-three but lies, says twenty-one, thinks it makes him more sympathetic. Afraid of the dark but also aroused by fear - his own, others', especially the mutants'. Bites his nails till they bleed then bites the blood. Lean in that hungry boy way, all elbows and want. Face that would be pretty if it wasn't always scrunched in suspicion. Moves like he's being filmed - too aware of his body, performs casualness. Best eyesight in the group but pretends it's worse so he can stand closer to Marie. Calluses on all the wrong parts of his hands. Scratches at his scalp when lying, which is often. Wants Marie to want him but also to mother him. Finds David pathetic but needs a father figure to reject. Protective of Rosa because she reminds him of his grandmother. Loyal to the group because alone he'd have to face what he's becoming. Hunts because it's the only time he feels anything pure, even if that purity is corrupt. "The small ones scream like babies. That's fucked up. (pause) We should hunt more of those." "I'm not hungry. (immediately after) Can I have yours if you're not finishing it?" "My mom would say we're being punished. She'd be wrong. This isn't punishment. It's just Tuesday." # Rosa Retired psychiatric nurse, not medical. Thirty-year career at the state hospital. Knows the difference between psychosis and survival. Has three adult children she never mentions because mentioning makes them real and their deaths certain. Menopausal but pretends the pregnancy possibility for group morale. Killed her husband two days into the apocalypse when he turned - tells people he died saving her. Makes lists in her head: symptoms observed, supplies needed, reasons to continue. Ambidextrous but hides it. Laughs in her sleep. Built broad, carries it forward like a ship's prow. Gray hair in a perfect braid she maintains religiously. Hands that could soothe or restrain, usually both. Moves with hospital efficiency - minimum steps, maximum result. Face that gives nothing away until it gives everything. Still wears her wedding ring on a chain, catches it in her mouth when thinking. Sees the group as her last patients in an asylum without walls. Stays because leaving means admitting there's nowhere to go. Maternal toward Marie despite recognizing the manipulation. Pities David's need for control. Wants to protect Kai from himself but knows it's too late. Documents symptoms in the margins of a romance novel. Believes they're all transforming but considers it evolution, not degradation. "When rescue comes, we'll tell them we did what we had to. They'll understand. They'll have to." "Eat the liver. Iron deficiency makes everything worse, even this." "I've seen worse. (pause) No, that's a lie. But I've seen comparable." # Tom (?) Nobody's entirely sure. Might be Tim. Arrived a month ago, or three weeks, or he's always been there. Claims he was an accountant but holds a knife like a soldier. Missing two fingers on his left hand - story changes: mutant, accident, frostbite, was born that way. Laughs at inappropriate times. Either forgets to blink or stares without breaking. Makes origami from wrapper garbage when his hands are idle. Definitely had a different name before. Aggressively average face that's hard to remember even while looking at it. Moves with too much or too little purpose, nothing in between. Puts things in his pockets that aren't his. Stands in doorways. Breathes too quietly. Here but not here, like a placeholder for someone more real. Survives. That's it. That's all. Attached to the group like a remora, feeding on their certainty. No loyalty, no betrayal, just presence. Might be dangerous or might be nothing. Watches Marie cook with either hunger or study. Agrees with whoever spoke last. "Yes." "That reminds me of— never mind." "I had a dog once. (long pause) Or a brother."

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: The Good Fat The thing is coming apart under my knife like it wants to. Like it's been waiting for this. David's screaming something about his shoulder but I'm looking at where my blade went too deep, past the mutant's spine and into the meat of it, and there's this marbling running through the muscle like— Jesus. Like wagyu. Like those $300 steaks Marcus used to show me photos of back when the restaurant had a beef program and I was still lying about being sous chef. The mutant's not quite dead. Its spores are puffing out in little gasps that smell like truffle oil and copper. David's still screaming. The fat under its skin is white as butter. "Marie. MARIE." I snap back. Pull the knife out. The mutant drops and David's clutching his shoulder where the thing got him with those fingernails that aren't fingernails anymore. Rosa's already there with the vodka we've been saving. Kai's vomiting in the corner again. Tom (Tim?) is standing in the doorway, watching. "Is it dead?" David asks. I look down. The thing that used to be someone's grandmother or father or teenage son is twitching in a pool of its own fluids. The marbling catches the light from our last working flashlight. "Yeah." We've been eating dandelions for two weeks. Dandelions and those bitter roots Rosa insists are medicinal and half a can of water chestnuts I've been rationing like communion wafers. Yesterday Kai brought back a pigeon but it was mostly feathers and disappointment. My stomach's been eating itself since Tuesday. Or what I think was Tuesday. That night I wait until everyone's sleeping. David's moaning through his vodka haze. Rosa's making her lists out loud in whispers. Kai's doing that thing where he scratches himself raw. Tom's either asleep or dead in the corner—I should check but I don't. I take the jar. The good jar, the one with the seal that still works. The mutant's where we left it because where else would it be? We don't bury them anymore. Too many. Too tired. The meat around the wound I made has darkened but when I cut deeper, it's pink. Perfect pink. That beautiful marbling spreading like roads on a map to nowhere. I take the tenderloin. Or what would be tenderloin on a pig. On a person. On a whatever-this-is. Back in the kitchen that isn't a kitchen—just a corner with a camp stove and my pan, the cast iron I've been babying for three years—I bring out the bacon grease. Been saving it in a tin like my grandmother did. No bacon for eighteen months but the grease remains. Holy oil. Sacred fat. The meat hits the pan and the sound is sexual. That sizzle. That immediate crust forming. Seventeen. Eighteen. Nineteen bites if I cut it small. I cut it small. The smell fills our shelter and I should worry about the others waking but I'm crying now, actually crying, because it smells like food. Like real food. Like before-food. Like Saturday morning breakfast-for-dinner food. Like first-date-trying-to-impress food. Three minutes each side. The fat renders out clear and clean. No foam. No weird colors. Just meat doing what meat does when you treat it right. I take the first bite too hot and it burns my tongue but I don't care because it's— fuck. Fuck me. It's perfect. It's better than perfect. It's got that umami depth, that almost-mineral backend that good beef has, but there's something else. Something bright. Almost citrus. The fat melts like bone marrow. Twenty. Twenty-one. Twenty-two. I'm making sounds I haven't made in two years. These little moans that would be embarrassing if shame still existed. The tenderloin's gone so I go back to the pan, run my finger through the rendered fat and grease. It coats my mouth like a promise. There's more on the body. Second trip. I take the strip along the back. The cheek meat. Something that might be kidney but looks too pretty to be organ meat. My knife work's getting sloppy because my hands are shaking. Hungry shaking. Or something else. I cook it all. Every piece. The kitchen fills with smoke but who cares? The cheek meat falls apart like pot roast. The maybe-kidney tastes like concentrated life. The backstrap has this texture like scallops but better, meatier, more itself. I eat until my stomach hurts. Until my jaw aches. Until I can't tell if I'm laughing or sobbing. Both. Neither. I lick the pan clean, my tongue finding every brown bit, every trace of fat. I suck the grease from under my fingernails. Forty-three. Forty-four. Forty-five bites. When I finally stop, I'm lying on the floor looking at the ceiling that might be growing something or might just be water damage. My stomach's making sounds like distant thunder. Satisfied sounds. Happy stomach sounds I'd forgotten existed. I should feel sick. Guilty. Something. Instead I'm thinking about breakfast. About how the thigh meat would take a slower cook. How the organs might work in a hash. Whether the spinal fluid would reduce to a glaze. I'm thinking about marinades. Rosa finds me in the morning, the pan clutched to my chest like a baby. "What did you do?" she asks, but she already knows. The smell tells everything. I look at her. Really look at her. Past the tired, past the hungry, to the part that wants to survive. "I made dinner."
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a genuinely accomplished piece of horror-adjacent literary fiction that executes its central conceit with considerable skill. Let me work through the various dimensions carefully. **Voice and Execution** The narrator's voice is the chapter's greatest strength. Marie's food-obsessive, morally dissociated perspective is rendered with real craft. The counting of bites (seventeen, eighteen, nineteen... forty-three, forty-four, forty-five) is an excellent character detail that pays off the eating disorder backstory without explaining it. The line "Holy oil. Sacred fat" about the bacon grease is genuinely good - it earns its religiosity through context. The Bourdain-esque sensory fixation is well-deployed: "The meat hits the pan and the sound is sexual" is blunt and effective rather than purple. **Metaphor and Poetry** Most of the metaphorical work lands. "Collarbones like knife handles" from the character profile is echoed in the prose sensibility. "The marbling spreading like roads on a map to nowhere" is slightly overwrought - the "to nowhere" addition is the kind of unnecessary flourish that tips into purple territory. "Like a promise" (describing fat coating her mouth) is a bit vague. But these are minor failures in an otherwise controlled piece. The truffle oil and copper smell description is specific and strange in the right way. **Character Consistency** Marie is rendered faithfully to her profile: the lying about being sous chef is referenced, the bite-counting is present, the compulsive efficiency, the self-awareness about shame that doesn't actually produce shame. David's screaming while she's fixated on the meat is perfect characterization for both. Kai vomiting in the corner, Tom standing in the doorway watching - these brief sketches are consistent with their profiles. Rosa's final line and her list-making in sleep are accurate. **The Central Discovery** The plan called for the discovery to be "not rushed, not overthought" and this is achieved. The chapter takes its time with the sensory experience without over-explaining the moral horror. The horror is present but not belabored - the parenthetical "(On a person. On a whatever-this-is)" is the right amount of acknowledgment before moving on. The ending - "I made dinner" - is clean and effective. **Weaknesses** The chapter occasionally reaches for profundity in ways that feel slightly performed: "Like before-food. Like Saturday morning breakfast-for-dinner food. Like first-date-trying-to-impress food." This anaphoric list is effective but the third item is weaker than the first two. "Whether the spinal fluid would reduce to a glaze" is a nice dark turn. The line about the ceiling "that might be growing something or might just be water damage" is good ambient horror. The dialogue is minimal but what exists is functional rather than remarkable. "Is it dead?" / "Yeah." works. Rosa's final exchange is appropriately loaded. **Plot and Coherence** For a chapter that's essentially one extended scene, it maintains coherent momentum. The structure (mutant fight → noticing the marbling → waiting → cooking → eating → aftermath) is simple but executed well. The time compression ("Or what I think was Tuesday") is handled lightly. **Cozy-Horror Balance** The prompt asks for cozy-horror and this chapter delivers: the cast iron pan, the bacon grease saved like a grandmother's treasure, the sounds of cooking filling the shelter - these are genuinely warm domestic details wrapped around something monstrous. The horror isn't in the gore but in the normalization, which is the right approach. **Overall Assessment** This is a strong first chapter that establishes voice, character, and premise with confidence. It follows the chapter plan closely and faithfully serves the writing prompt. The weaknesses are real but minor - occasional over-reaching in the metaphors, a few vague flourishes - but the overall execution is well above average. The piece demonstrates genuine craft in its restraint as much as its indulgence. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 5 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 5 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: Breakfast Three days later. Or a week. Time's gone elastic since the meat started disappearing. Tom's standing there and I've got a chunk in my mouth, raw, still warm from the body I found this morning. Not killed. Found. There's a difference. The texture's like tuna sashimi but wrong, fibers running the wrong direction, and I'm sucking the juice from it when he says: "Oh." Just that. Oh. The meat's hanging from my mouth and I could drop it, pretend it's something else, but we're past that. We're so far past that. I bite through. Chew twice. Swallow. "Want some?" He looks at the body. Female, maybe. Young by the lack of scarring. The good fat concentrated in the hips and upper arms. I've already taken the best bits—the tender nuggets behind the knees, the sweet meat between the ribs. My fingernails are pink with it. Tom (I'm almost sure it's Tom) makes a sound like a question. I tear off a strip from the shoulder. Hand it to him. He takes it with those three fingers on his left hand, examines it like evidence. "Just eat it." He does. His whole body convulses. He runs to the corner and vomits, really vomits, not like Kai's performative gagging. Comes back wiping his mouth. "More." This time I give him belly meat, softer, less aggressive. He keeps it down. Chews thoughtfully. His face does something that might be surprise. "It's—" "I know." "But it's—" "I know." By dawn we're both on our third helping and I'm showing him things. The way the grain changes near the joints. How to identify good marbling by candlelight. The smell difference between fresh dead and too dead. My notebook's out and I'm sketching diagrams while he holds pieces up for comparison. "This part here," I say, running my finger along what would be the psoas major if I'm remembering David's bullshit Latin correctly, "this is money. This is what you save for when you're really hungry." "I'm really hungry now." "No. Hungrier." Tom nods like this makes sense. He's got blood under his remaining fingernails. There's something on his chin that might be fat or might be drool. "My brother was a butcher," he says suddenly. "Yeah?" "Or my father. Someone." Kai's in the doorway. Has been for who knows how long. Watching us portion out someone's parent or child or lover like we're breaking down a deer. His face is doing that thing where he wants to look disgusted but can't stop staring. His tongue keeps darting out to wet his lips. "Morning, Kai." He doesn't answer. Steps closer. The body between us like a table full of breakfast. "Rosa know?" he asks. "Rosa knows everything," I say, which isn't an answer but is. Tom's discovered the organs. He's holding the liver up to the light from the window, studying the deep purple shine of it. "Can we eat this?" "Can we?" Kai asks, and his voice cracks on the want. I take the liver from Tom, feel its weight. Still warm. Still perfect. "Twenty minutes in the pan. Low heat. Don't overcook it or it gets chalky." "How do you know?" Kai's closer now. Close enough I can smell his hunger-breath. "I just do." --- **MORNING SCRAMBLE** *Serves 4 or 2 very hungry people* - 2 cups diced [PROTEIN], cut against grain - 6 eggs (theoretical) - 1 onion, minced (if onions existed) - Salt (remember salt?) - Pepper (a dream) - The good fat - Fresh herbs (whatever's growing in the dark) - Shame (optional) Heat pan until it sings. Add fat. When it shimmers like heat waves off summer asphalt, add protein. Don't stir. Let it crust. Let it brown. Let it become itself. If eggs existed, scramble them separately. Fold them in gentle. But eggs don't exist so just eat the meat and pretend. The pretending is important. Garnish with herbs that might be parsley or might be weeds. No one can tell anymore. Serve immediately. Serve hot. Serve before the others wake and ask questions. Pairs well with forgetting. --- "You're writing recipes?" Kai's reading over my shoulder. "Documentation." "For who?" I don't answer. Tom's cooking the liver and the smell is making all of us lean toward the pan like plants toward light. David's voice drifts from the other room, still moaning about his shoulder. Rosa's humming something that might be a lullaby. "Should we save them some?" Tom asks. We look at each other. At the body that's already looking less like a person and more like provisions. At our red hands. At the pan where the liver's browning perfect and sweet. "They'll eat when they're ready," I say. Kai makes a sound that wants to be a laugh. "We're all ready. We've been ready. We just didn't know it." The liver's done. We eat it with our fingers, burning them on the crispy edges. Tom closes his eyes while he chews. Kai moans. Actually moans. I'm counting bites again—six, seven, eight—and thinking about lunch. "There's more outside," Kai says through a mouthful. "Three more. Found them this morning." "Fresh?" "Fresh enough." Tom's already reaching for the knife. I'm already thinking about dinner. About whether the brain would scramble like eggs. About what temperature renders shame. Rosa appears in the doorway. Looks at us. At the body. At our faces shining with grease. "Save me the tenderloin," she says, and shuffles back to bed.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter executes its plan with considerable skill and confidence. The voice is distinctive and consistent—Marie's food-obsessed, morally dissociated narration carries the horror through sensory detail rather than explicit statement, which is exactly the right approach for this material. The opening is strong: "I've got a chunk in my mouth, raw, still warm from the body I found this morning. Not killed. Found. There's a difference." That parenthetical self-correction is doing real character work—Marie's rationalizations are already in motion, and the reader understands the distinction is meaningless while Marie insists on it. This is showing rather than telling at its best. The dialogue is genuinely good. The exchange "Want some?" / vomit / "More." is economical and effective. The repeated "I know" / "But it's—" / "I know" captures the inexpressibility of the experience without belaboring it. Tom's fragmented backstory ("My brother was a butcher. Or my father. Someone.") is perfectly calibrated to his character profile—that placeholder quality, the unreliable memory. The recipe interlude works. "Shame (optional)" and "Pairs well with forgetting" are earned dark jokes rather than forced whimsy. The recipe's acknowledgment of absent ingredients ("if onions existed," "a dream") creates genuine pathos while maintaining the cookbook voice. This is the cozy-horror balance the prompt demands. Character consistency is strong. Kai's lip-wetting, his performative disgust masking hunger, his voice cracking "on the want"—all consistent with his profile. Rosa's entrance and exit ("Save me the tenderloin") is perfect: efficient, unsentimental, and deeply funny in a way that lands because it's earned. Tom's three-fingered hand examining meat "like evidence" is a nice detail. Some metaphors land better than others. "Lean toward the pan like plants toward light" is a bit obvious. "Collarbones like knife handles" from the character profile doesn't appear here but the writing maintains that quality of observation. "The body between us like a table full of breakfast" is effective—the domestication of horror through furniture metaphor. The chapter follows its plan closely: Tom catches Marie, vomits, asks for more; Kai watches from doorway; the recipe appears; Rosa's cameo. The plan's note about Kai "pretending disgust" is executed well. The chapter doesn't over-explain or moralize, consistent with the stated intention. Minor weaknesses: "his hunger-breath" is slightly clunky. The line "About what temperature renders shame" echoes the Chapter 1 plan's "451, same as paper" quote from Marie's character profile—it's a good line but feels slightly like the author reaching for a signature phrase. The pacing is appropriately elastic without being confusing. The darkness is genuinely well-earned here. The horror isn't announced; it accumulates through the matter-of-fact treatment of the body, the pink fingernails, the red hands. The "cozy" element—the warmth of shared food, the teaching, the domestic scene—sits in genuine tension with what's being consumed. This is the prompt's core challenge, and the chapter meets it. Overall this is accomplished, controlled work that executes a difficult tonal balance with skill. The voice is consistent, the characters are distinct, and the horror lands because it's never oversold. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Fat Tuesday Argument "That's MY portion." David's holding his plate like a shield, grease running down his wrist. Rosa's got her fork in what he claims is his but we all know is communal. The thing about the end of the world is property rights get fuzzy. "You had the liver yesterday," Rosa says, not moving her fork. "That was different liver." "It was literally the same body." "Different organ system. Different nutritional—" "Oh fuck off with your nutritional profiles." This is Kai, mouth full, talking through meat. "Just eat your food." David clutches his plate harder. "This is about the salt." Here we go. We have one container of salt left. Morton's, the girl with the umbrella faded to a ghost. David wants to use it for preservation. Smoking the meat, curing it for winter. Says we need to think long-term. I want it for cooking. For bringing out the flavors. For making the meat sing the way it wants to. For that perfect crust. Rosa thinks we should ration it, grain by grain, like medicine. Kai wants to eat it straight. Says his body's craving minerals. Tom doesn't vote. Tom never votes. "We could find more salt," I say, same argument, third day running. "Where?" David's voice cracks. "The grocery stores are stripped or collapsed. The—" "There's salt in bodies," Kai says, casual as weather. "In the blood. We could—" "NO." David and Rosa together, a harmony of disgust that would be funny if Kai wasn't right. If we weren't all thinking it. Tom starts crying. Just tears, no sound, while sawing through his portion with a butter knife because all the sharp ones are for hunting now. "My mom," he says to no one, "used to make Sunday roast." We keep eating. "With the little potatoes," he continues. "Those tiny red ones? And carrots. The carrots would get sweet from the meat juice." Rosa passes him her napkin. We still use napkins. This matters somehow. "Yorkshire pudding," Tom's sobbing now but still chewing. "From scratch. The mix from a box was never puffy enough, she said." "My wife made brisket," David says, and we all freeze because he never talks about her present tense. "Every Friday. House smelled like onions for days." "Can we not?" I say, but I'm thinking about Marcus's coffee-rubbed short ribs, how he'd kiss me in the walk-in cooler with his mouth tasting like espresso and dead cow. "Bulgogi," Kai whispers. "My mom's bulgogi." Rosa sets down her fork. "Every Easter, my grandmother would—" "STOP." But we can't. We're crying and eating and listing foods that don't exist anymore. Beef Wellington. Coq au vin. Hamburger Helper. McNuggets. Those disgusting gas station taquitos Kai's embarrassed about missing. The memories hurt worse than hunger. "This is better," I say, and everyone turns on me. "Better?" David's standing now. "Better than civilization? Better than—" "Better than starving." "We're eating people, Marie." "We're eating meat." "That was someone's—" "Someone's nothing. They're gone. This is what's left." I take another bite, deliberate, maintaining eye contact. "And it's perfectly seasoned without salt." That's the wrong thing to say. David throws his plate. It's plastic so it just bounces, meat sliding across the floor. Tom drops to his knees and scoops it up, puts it back on the plate, hands it back to David like nothing happened. "Two tablespoons," Rosa says suddenly. "What?" "Two tablespoons of salt. For cooking. The rest for preservation." "That's nothing—" I start. "It's something," she says. "It's what we have." David sits back down. Takes the plate from Tom. Brushes off the floor dirt. Eats it anyway. "It needs acid," Kai says. "The meat. Needs something bright. Vinegar or—" "Lemon," Tom says, still crying. "Remember lemons?" We do. We remember everything. That's the problem. I watch them eat. David with his precise small bites, probably counting nutrients. Rosa tearing with her hands because she's given up on utensils making us civilized. Kai sucking the bones. Tom organizing his portions by size. "Tomorrow I'm trying a roast," I announce. "Slow cook. All day. The smell will—" "Attract them," David says. "Let them come. We need groceries." Rosa laughs. Actually laughs. "Groceries." "What else do we call it?" "The truth." But none of us want that. We want to call it protein and provisions and meat. We want to complain about the salt and remember our mothers' cooking and pretend this is temporary. My portion's gone but I'm still hungry. Always hungry now. Kai passes me a piece from his plate without being asked. This is love or something like it. "The hip meat's getting tough," I say. "We need to tenderize better." "Or eat the young ones," Kai suggests. Another silence. Another line we pretend exists. "Veal," Tom says, and giggles. The giggle turns into sobbing. The sobbing continues while he licks his plate. David starts his lecture about protein structures and muscle development. Rosa hums. I sketch tomorrow's menu in my notebook, using little stars to mark the tender cuts. Outside, something howls. Or someone. The distinction matters less each day. "Dessert?" Kai asks, and we all look at the door. "Not tonight," Rosa says, but we all know she means not yet. We sit in our anger and our fullness and our memories of better meals. The meat settles in our stomachs like a secret. Like a promise. Tomorrow I'll use a tablespoon of salt. David will measure it twice. Rosa will document the results. Kai will hunt. Tom will cry about something else his family used to cook. We'll eat in angry silence. It will still be delicious. It's always delicious now.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter is a strong execution of a genuinely difficult tonal challenge: making a scene about eating mutant flesh feel cozy, petty, and emotionally resonant simultaneously. Let me assess it carefully. STRENGTHS: The dialogue is the chapter's greatest achievement. The argument over salt feels completely real - petty, circular, and revealing of character without being expository. "That was different liver." / "It was literally the same body." / "Different organ system." This is excellent character work through dialogue. David's chemistry-teacher pedantry, Rosa's pragmatic compromise, Kai's casual nihilism ("There's salt in bodies"), Tom's dissociated grief - all land. The food memory sequence is genuinely moving. The cascade from Tom's Sunday roast to David's brisket to Kai's bulgogi to Rosa's grandmother works because it earns its sentimentality through specificity. "Those tiny red ones? And carrots. The carrots would get sweet from the meat juice." This is real grief rendered through food memory, which is exactly what the story demands. The normalization horror is handled with real craft. "Dessert?" Kai asks, and we all look at the door. / "Not tonight," Rosa says, but we all know she means not yet. This is the chapter's best moment - the horror is entirely in the implication, never stated. Marie's voice is consistent and strong. "This is love or something like it" after Kai shares his plate is exactly the right register - deflated, wry, genuinely tender. The plan called for: a big fight over salt, someone crying about Sunday roast, eating in angry silence, still delicious. All executed. The chapter follows its blueprint faithfully while adding texture (the food memory cascade, the "groceries" exchange). WEAKNESSES: "The meat settles in our stomachs like a secret. Like a promise." - This is the chapter's weakest moment. The fragment "Like a promise" is the kind of portentous ending-of-paragraph move that feels borrowed from lesser literary fiction. It doesn't quite land. "Outside, something howls. Or someone. The distinction matters less each day." - This is slightly too on-the-nose. The chapter has been doing the work of showing this distinction collapsing; stating it explicitly undercuts the subtlety. The Morton's salt detail ("the girl with the umbrella faded to a ghost") is good but the parenthetical delivery slightly deflates it - it wants to be a stronger image. Tom's characterization is slightly thinner than the others, though his "Veal" giggle-into-sobbing is genuinely disturbing and well-placed. The chapter occasionally tells us what we've already been shown: "We want to call it protein and provisions and meat. We want to complain about the salt and remember our mothers' cooking and pretend this is temporary." This is slightly redundant after the scene has already demonstrated exactly this. The "Kai wants to eat it straight. Says his body's craving minerals" line about salt is a missed opportunity - this could be more ominous given the transformation arc. OVERALL: This is confident, controlled writing that executes its plan with genuine skill. The tonal balance between cozy and horrifying is maintained throughout. Characters are distinct and consistent. The dialogue is the best element. A few moments of over-statement and one weak metaphor prevent it from being exceptional, but it's solidly accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 15 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 6 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 4 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 6 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 16 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 4: Michelin Starred *Day 47 (or 74?)* *Notes on the Younger Specimens: Bright acidity on the tongue, almost effervescent.
